background_cases: A man deliberates carefully and chooses to accept a job offer; we say he acts freely.
A woman weighs the arguments and votes for the candidate she judges best; this too seems free.
A student considers his options and decides to study medicine; again, a free choice.
definition: An action is free if and only if (i) the agent deliberates before acting, (ii) the agent acts on his own desires, and (iii) no external force compels the action.
conditions: agent deliberates
acts on own desires
no external compulsion
counterexample: A man with an irresistible compulsion to wash his hands deliberates about whether to wash them, acts on his own desire (the compulsion is genuinely his), and no external force compels him. The three conditions are met, yet the action is not free — it is driven by a compulsion he cannot resist.
abductive_insight: The background cases involved agents whose desires were ones they reflectively endorsed. The definition failed to distinguish between desires the agent identifies with and desires that grip him against his reflective judgement.
passage:
Consider three cases in which we readily ascribe freedom. A man deliberates carefully and chooses to accept a job offer; we say he acts freely. A woman weighs the arguments and votes for the candidate she judges best; this too seems free. A student considers his options and decides to study medicine; again, a free choice. From these cases one might extract the following definition: an action is free if and only if the agent deliberates before acting, acts on his own desires, and no external force compels the action.

Yet this definition proves too permissive. Consider a man who suffers from an irresistible compulsion to wash his hands. He deliberates — he knows the compulsion is irrational and considers resisting it. He acts on his own desire, for the compulsion is genuinely his; it is not imposed by another person. And no external force compels him. The three conditions are met. Yet we should not say he acts freely, for his action is driven by a compulsion he cannot overcome by any effort of will. The background cases all involved agents whose desires were ones they could reflectively endorse — desires that were, as it were, truly their own. The definition failed to distinguish between desires the agent identifies with and desires that grip him against his better judgement.

background_cases: A man sees a red apple on the table before him and correctly identifies it as a red apple.
A woman hears a church bell ringing and correctly identifies the sound.
A child touches a hot stove and immediately perceives the heat.
definition: A person perceives an object if and only if (i) the object causally affects his sense organs, (ii) he forms a mental representation of the object, and (iii) the representation is veridical.
conditions: object causally affects sense organs
forms mental representation
representation is veridical
counterexample: A man looks at a white wall illuminated by red light. The wall causally affects his eyes, he forms a representation of a red wall, and the representation is veridical in the sense that the wall does appear red under the prevailing conditions. Yet he does not perceive the wall as it truly is — he perceives a white wall as red.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involved objects perceived under standard conditions. The definition did not specify what counts as a veridical representation when conditions are non-standard, conflating the appearance of the object under prevailing conditions with its intrinsic properties.
passage:
Consider three straightforward cases of perception. A man sees a red apple on the table before him and correctly identifies it as a red apple. A woman hears a church bell ringing and correctly identifies the sound. A child touches a hot stove and immediately perceives the heat. From these cases one might define perception: a person perceives an object if and only if the object causally affects his sense organs, he forms a mental representation of the object, and the representation is veridical — that is, it accurately represents the object.

But consider a man who looks at a white wall illuminated by a red light. The wall causally affects his eyes; he forms a mental representation — of a red wall. Is the representation veridical? In one sense, yes: the wall does appear red under the prevailing conditions, and the man's visual system is functioning correctly. In another sense, no: the wall is white, and the man perceives it as red. The background cases all involved perception under standard conditions — normal lighting, unobstructed view, functioning senses. The definition failed to specify what counts as veridical representation when conditions are non-standard, and accordingly cannot distinguish between perceiving an object as it is and perceiving it as conditions make it appear.

background_cases: A woman whose husband is plainly unfaithful insists, against all evidence, that he is devoted to her; we say she is self-deceived.
A gambler who has lost a fortune persists in the conviction that his luck is about to turn, despite overwhelming statistical evidence to the contrary; he too is self-deceived.
A man diagnosed with a serious illness insists he is perfectly well, ignoring the testimony of multiple physicians; again, self-deception.
definition: A person is self-deceived about p if and only if (i) p is false, (ii) the person holds p to be true, and (iii) the person has access to sufficient evidence that p is false.
conditions: p is false
person holds p to be true
person has access to sufficient evidence that p is false
counterexample: A man holds the true proposition that his colleagues like him, and he has access to evidence that they do not — whispered complaints, cool greetings, exclusion from gatherings. Yet his colleagues do in fact like him; the apparent evidence of hostility arises from their shyness and cultural reserve. He holds p to be true and p is true. The first condition fails. Yet one may wish to say he is self-deceived, for his grounds for holding p are not the evidence but his desperate need to be liked, and if the evidence of hostility were genuine, he would be ignoring it in exactly the same way.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involved false propositions held true against the evidence. The definition required the falsity of p, but self-deception may be characterised more by the irrational process of belief-formation than by the truth-value of the proposition believed.
passage:
Consider three cases of self-deception. A woman whose husband is plainly unfaithful insists, against all evidence, that he is devoted to her; we say she is self-deceived. A gambler who has lost a fortune persists in the conviction that his luck is about to turn, despite overwhelming statistical evidence to the contrary; he too is self-deceived. A man diagnosed with a serious illness insists he is perfectly well, ignoring the testimony of multiple physicians; again, self-deception. From these cases one might extract a definition: a person is self-deceived about p if and only if p is false, the person holds p to be true, and the person has access to sufficient evidence that p is false.

Yet the analysis encounters a subtle difficulty. Suppose a man holds the proposition that his colleagues like him. He has access to what appears to be evidence to the contrary: his colleagues whisper behind his back, greet him coolly, and exclude him from social gatherings. Yet in fact his colleagues do like him; the apparent evidence of hostility arises from their natural reserve and cultural reticence. The proposition p — that his colleagues like him — is true. The first condition of the definition fails. Yet the man's grounds for holding p are not the evidence at all; he holds p because of a desperate need to be liked, and he would hold p just as tenaciously if his colleagues truly despised him. His belief-forming process is precisely that of the self-deceived person: he ignores apparent counter-evidence and clings to a comforting proposition for motivational reasons. The background cases all involved false propositions, and the definition required falsity. But self-deception, one suspects, is better characterised by the irrational, motivationally-driven process of belief-formation than by the truth-value of the resulting proposition.

background_cases: A student concentrates on a difficult passage in a textbook, blocking out the noise of the common room; we say he is attending to the text.
A birdwatcher scans a hedgerow, singling out a rare species from among the foliage; she is attending to the bird.
A pianist practising a difficult passage focuses intently on the coordination of her fingers; she is attending to the fingering.
definition: A subject attends to X if and only if (i) he selects X from a field of competing stimuli, (ii) he sustains focus on X over some interval of time, and (iii) his processing of X is enhanced relative to unselected stimuli.
conditions: selects X from competing stimuli
sustains focus over interval
processing of X enhanced relative to unselected stimuli
counterexample: A man walking down a quiet street suddenly hears a loud explosion. His attention is involuntarily captured by the sound. He did not select it from competing stimuli — it overwhelmed all other stimuli. He may not sustain focus on it for any interval — the sound is over in an instant. Yet in that instant he is undeniably attending to the explosion. The first and second conditions fail, yet the case is a paradigmatic instance of attending.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all instances of voluntary, sustained attention directed by the subject's goals. The definition captured the selective and sustained character of these cases but failed to accommodate involuntary, transient attention — the capture of awareness by a sudden, salient stimulus.
passage:
Consider three cases of attention. A student concentrates on a difficult passage in a textbook, blocking out the noise of the common room; we say he is attending to the text. A birdwatcher scans a hedgerow, singling out a rare species from the foliage; she is attending to the bird. A pianist practising a difficult passage focuses on the coordination of her fingers; she is attending to the fingering. From these cases one might define attention: a subject attends to X if and only if he selects X from a field of competing stimuli, sustains focus on X over some interval, and his processing of X is enhanced relative to unselected stimuli.

But this definition, drawn from cases of voluntary, goal-directed attention, fails to accommodate a familiar phenomenon. Suppose a man is walking down a quiet street, lost in thought. A loud explosion occurs nearby. In an instant, his awareness is wholly captured by the sound. He did not select the explosion from a field of competing stimuli — on the contrary, the explosion overwhelmed all other stimuli and seized his awareness without any act of selection on his part. Nor did he sustain focus over any interval, for the sound was over almost as soon as it began. Yet in that brief moment he was undeniably attending to the explosion — attending to it with an intensity that surpassed anything in the background cases. The definition captured the selective, sustained, goal-directed character of the background cases but missed the phenomenon of involuntary attentional capture, in which a sufficiently salient stimulus seizes awareness without selection, without sustained focus, and without any contribution from the subject's goals.

background_cases: A man dreams that he is being chased through a dark forest and wakes in a state of terror; we call this a dream.
A woman dreams that she is flying over a city, experiencing vivid sensations of wind and height; this too is a dream.
A child dreams that he is at school without his clothes and wakes embarrassed; again, a dream.
definition: A mental episode is a dream if and only if (i) it occurs during sleep, (ii) it presents a narrative or sequence of events, and (iii) the events are experienced as if they were perceived rather than merely thought.
conditions: occurs during sleep
presents narrative or sequence of events
events experienced as perceived rather than merely thought
counterexample: A person in the early stages of sleep experiences a single, isolated image — a vivid flash of a face or a landscape — with no narrative and no sequence of events. The image is experienced as perceived rather than thought. The first and third conditions are met, but the second fails, for there is no narrative. Yet these hypnagogic images are commonly described as dreams, or at the very least as dream-like episodes, and they share the phenomenal character of dreaming.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all extended, narrative dreams of the sort most commonly reported upon waking. The definition captured the narrative structure of these canonical examples but excluded the fragmentary, non-narrative dream images that occur at the threshold of sleep, which share the phenomenological character of dreaming without its narrative form.
passage:
Consider three cases of dreaming. A man dreams that he is being chased through a dark forest and wakes in terror; we call this a dream. A woman dreams she is flying over a city, experiencing vivid sensations of wind and height; this too is a dream. A child dreams that he is at school without his clothes and wakes embarrassed; again, a dream. From these cases one might extract a definition: a mental episode is a dream if and only if it occurs during sleep, presents a narrative or sequence of events, and the events are experienced as if perceived rather than merely thought.

But this definition, drawn from canonical cases of extended, narrative dreams, proves too narrow. Consider a person in the early stages of sleep who experiences a single, isolated image: a vivid flash of a face, brilliant and unbidden, with no narrative context and no sequence of events. The image appears and vanishes. It is experienced not as a thought but as a perception — it has the vividness and the involuntary character of something seen. The first and third conditions are met: the episode occurs during sleep and is experienced as perceived. But the second condition fails, for there is no narrative and no sequence of events. Yet these hypnagogic images are commonly described as dreams, or at the very least as phenomena continuous with dreaming, and they share the essential phenomenal character of the dream: involuntary, vivid, perception-like experience during sleep. The background cases were all extended narrative dreams, and the definition captured their narrative structure. But it excluded the fragmentary, imagistic episodes at the threshold of sleep, which belong, one suspects, to the same family of phenomena.

background_cases: A man who touches a hot stove feels a sharp pain in his hand and withdraws it immediately.
A woman who steps on a thorn feels a stabbing pain in her foot and limps to avoid further pressure.
A child who falls and skins his knee cries out in pain and holds the injured area.
definition: A sensation is pain if and only if (i) it is localised in a particular part of the body, (ii) it has a characteristic unpleasant quality, and (iii) it is caused by actual or potential tissue damage at the site of the sensation.
conditions: localised in particular body part
characteristic unpleasant quality
caused by actual or potential tissue damage at site
counterexample: A man whose leg has been amputated experiences intense, localised pain in the absent limb — so-called phantom pain. The sensation has a characteristic unpleasant quality and is localised in a particular part of the body (or rather, in the space where the limb once was). The first two conditions are met. But the third condition fails, for there is no tissue at the site of the sensation and no tissue damage to cause it. Yet the pain is as real and as agonising as any pain caused by actual injury.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involved pain at the site of actual bodily damage. The definition assumed a correspondence between the location of pain and the location of its cause, but phantom pain reveals that pain can be localised where no body part exists, dissociating the sensation from its supposed peripheral cause.
passage:
Consider three cases of pain. A man who touches a hot stove feels a sharp pain in his hand and withdraws it immediately. A woman who steps on a thorn feels a stabbing pain in her foot. A child who falls and skins his knee cries out in pain and holds the injured area. From these cases one might define pain: a sensation is pain if and only if it is localised in a particular part of the body, it has a characteristic unpleasant quality, and it is caused by actual or potential tissue damage at the site of the sensation.

Yet this definition, natural as it seems, is refuted by a well-known clinical phenomenon. A man whose leg has been amputated reports intense pain in the missing limb. The pain is exquisitely localised — he can point to the precise spot in the space where his foot once was. The sensation has all the characteristic unpleasant quality of ordinary pain: it burns, it throbs, it aches. The first two conditions of the definition are satisfied. But the third fails entirely, for there is no tissue at the site of the sensation — no foot, no nerves, no flesh to be damaged. The pain is generated centrally, in the nervous system itself, without any peripheral cause. The background cases all involved pain at the site of actual tissue damage, and the definition assumed a correspondence between the location of pain and the location of injury. Phantom pain reveals that this correspondence, though typical, is not essential: pain can be localised where no body part exists, and its cause may lie far from the site at which it is felt.

background_cases: A man recalls his wedding day: the church, the guests, the vows. He re-experiences the scene in vivid detail; we say he remembers it.
A woman recalls a childhood holiday at the seaside: the smell of salt air, the sound of waves. She re-experiences these sensations; she remembers the holiday.
A soldier recalls a battle: the noise, the fear, the faces of his comrades. He re-experiences these with painful clarity; he remembers the battle.
definition: A person remembers an event if and only if (i) the event occurred in his past, (ii) he has a present mental representation of the event, and (iii) the representation takes the form of a re-experiencing of the event — that is, it has a sensory, quasi-perceptual character.
conditions: event occurred in person's past
person has present mental representation
representation has sensory re-experiencing character
counterexample: A man knows that he was born in a particular hospital on a particular date. The event occurred in his past, and he has a present mental representation of it. But his representation has no sensory, quasi-perceptual character whatever: he does not re-experience the hospital, the sounds, or the sensations. He simply holds the propositional content that he was born there. The third condition fails. Yet it would be strange to deny that he remembers where he was born.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all instances of vivid, experiential, episodic memory. The definition captured the sensory, re-experiential character of these particular memories but excluded propositional or semantic memory — the capacity to recall facts about one's past without any accompanying sensory imagery.
passage:
Consider three cases of memory. A man recalls his wedding day: the church, the guests, the vows. He re-experiences the scene in vivid detail; we say he remembers it. A woman recalls a childhood holiday at the seaside: the smell of salt air, the sound of waves. She re-experiences these sensations; she remembers the holiday. A soldier recalls a battle: the noise, the fear, the faces of his comrades. He re-experiences these with painful clarity; he remembers the battle. From these cases one might define memory: a person remembers an event if and only if the event occurred in his past, he has a present mental representation of it, and the representation takes the form of a re-experiencing — that is, it has a sensory, quasi-perceptual character.

But consider a man who knows that he was born in a particular hospital on a particular date. The event occurred in his past and he has a present mental representation of this fact. Yet his representation has no sensory character whatever: he does not re-experience the hospital, the sounds, or the sensations of his birth. He simply holds the proposition that he was born there, as one holds any other fact. The third condition fails. Yet it would be decidedly odd to say that this man does not remember where he was born. The background cases were all instances of vivid, episodic memory — the kind in which past experience is relived in sensory detail. The definition captured the re-experiential character of these canonical examples but excluded the vast domain of propositional or factual memory, in which we recall truths about our past without any accompanying imagery or sensory experience.

background_cases: A man under general anaesthesia exhibits no awareness, no qualitative experience, and no integration of sensory input — he is paradigmatically unconscious.
A woman reading a novel is aware of the narrative, experiences vivid imagery, and integrates the text with her emotional responses — she is paradigmatically conscious.

A child absorbed in play is aware of the objects before him, experiences their colours and textures, and integrates these experiences into a unified perceptual field — he is conscious of his surroundings.
definition: A mental state S of a subject P is a conscious state if and only if (i) P is aware of being in S, (ii) S possesses a qualitative character accessible to P through introspection, and (iii) S is integrated into P's unified field of experience.
conditions: P is aware of being in S
S possesses a qualitative character accessible to P through introspection
S is integrated into P's unified field of experience
counterexample: Consider a case in which a subject, engaged in a demanding visual task, fails to notice a large and conspicuous change in her visual field — a phenomenon well documented in the psychological literature. The stimulus is present, it is integrated into her visual processing (as demonstrated by subtle behavioural effects), and it possesses qualitative character in the sense that it would be experienced as having such character were attention directed towards it. Yet the subject is not aware of the change and cannot introspect upon it. The state appears to be integrated at a sub-personal level whilst failing conditions (i) and (ii) at the personal level.
abductive_insight: The best explanation of why the three conditions fail in this case is that consciousness admits of degrees and levels of processing: a state may be integrated into the subject's perceptual economy at a sub-personal level without rising to the level of personal awareness. The definition assumes a sharp boundary between conscious and unconscious states, but the case suggests that the boundary is graded, with some states occupying an intermediate position — processed and integrated but not available for introspective report.
passage:
It has seemed to many philosophers that consciousness may be satisfactorily characterised by three conditions: the subject's awareness of being in the state, the state's possession of introspectively accessible qualitative character, and its integration into the subject's unified experiential field. The paradigmatic cases lend considerable support to this view. A man under general anaesthesia satisfies none of these conditions and is plainly unconscious. A woman absorbed in a novel satisfies all three and is plainly conscious. A child at play likewise meets each requirement with ease. The definition appears to draw the boundary in the right place.

Yet one may reflect upon cases that do not sit so comfortably within this framework. It is a well-attested phenomenon that a subject engaged in a demanding visual task may fail entirely to notice a large and conspicuous alteration in her visual field — the disappearance of an object, the change of a colour, the substitution of one figure for another. The stimulus is present and is processed by her visual system, as may be demonstrated by subtle effects upon her subsequent behaviour. In that sense, the state possesses integration into her perceptual economy. Moreover, the stimulus is of a kind that would, under conditions of directed attention, present a vivid qualitative character. Yet the subject is not aware of the change and cannot, upon being asked, report anything about it. She does not satisfy conditions (i) and (ii) at the level of personal awareness, even though something answering to condition (iii) obtains at a sub-personal level. The most plausible explanation of this failure, one is inclined to think, is that consciousness does not observe the sharp boundary that the definition presupposes. Rather, there appear to be degrees or levels of processing, such that a state may be integrated into the subject's perceptual system without attaining the threshold of awareness and introspective accessibility. The definition treats consciousness as an all-or-nothing matter, but the case suggests that certain states occupy an intermediate position — processed, causally efficacious, and in some sense 'in' the subject's experience, yet not available for the reflective operations that conditions (i) and (ii) demand.

background_cases: A woman decides to bake a cake, assembles the ingredients, follows the recipe, and produces the cake — her intention is straightforwardly carried out through a sequence of deliberate steps.
A man resolves to learn French, enrols in classes, practises daily, and gradually acquires the language — his intention structures a sustained course of purposive conduct.
A committee votes to build a new library, commissions architects, secures funding, and oversees construction — the collective intention is implemented through coordinated institutional action.
definition: An agent A has the intention to φ if and only if (i) A has formed a plan to φ, (ii) A is committed to executing the plan, and (iii) A's plan is the organising principle of A's relevant conduct.
conditions: A has formed a plan to φ
A is committed to executing the plan
A's plan is the organising principle of A's relevant conduct
counterexample: Consider a case in which a man forms a plan to write a novel, is genuinely committed to the project, and organises his daily routine around it — setting aside writing hours, purchasing materials, declining social engagements. Yet each time he sits down to write, he finds himself unable to produce anything; the plan organises his conduct in every respect except the actual writing. He has a plan, he is committed to it, and it organises his behaviour — yet the intention never issues in the intended action, and one begins to wonder whether an intention that is systematically frustrated in its execution remains a genuine intention or becomes something else entirely.
abductive_insight: The best explanation is that the three conditions capture the structure of intention but not its executive dimension. An intention is not merely a plan that organises conduct; it must also possess a functional connection to the capacity for execution. When this connection is severed — when the plan organises peripheral conduct but cannot generate the core action — the state resembles intention in its formal properties but lacks the generative power that distinguishes genuine intention from mere aspiration. The definition conflates the organisational role of intention with its executive role.
passage:
The notion of intention has been analysed in terms of plan-formation, commitment, and the organising role that the plan plays in the agent's conduct. This analysis draws support from a range of paradigmatic cases. When a woman decides to bake a cake and proceeds to assemble ingredients, follow a recipe, and produce the finished article, her intention is manifested as a plan that she is committed to and that organises her actions from start to finish. When a man resolves to learn a language and enrols in classes, his intention similarly structures a sustained course of purposive conduct. The institutional case of a committee that votes to build a library and then oversees its construction offers a collective analogue of the same structure.

Yet consider a man who forms a plan to write a novel. His commitment to the project is beyond question: he has set aside daily writing hours, purchased a typewriter and reams of paper, declined social engagements that would encroach upon his schedule, and arranged his domestic affairs so as to be free from disturbance during the appointed times. His plan is genuinely the organising principle of his relevant conduct — his entire routine is structured around the writing of the novel. Yet each time he sits at his desk, he finds himself unable to write. The words do not come; the page remains blank; the hours pass in fruitless effort. This is not a temporary difficulty but a persistent condition. The plan organises everything except the writing itself. All three conditions of the definition are met: there is a plan, there is commitment, and the plan organises the agent's conduct. Yet the intention never issues in the intended action. The most satisfying explanation of this puzzling state of affairs, it seems to me, is that the definition captures the structural and organisational features of intention whilst omitting its executive dimension. A genuine intention must not only organise the agent's peripheral conduct but must also possess a functional connection to the agent's capacity to perform the intended action. When this connection is absent — when the plan is impotent to generate the core activity it prescribes — the state, however much it resembles intention in its formal properties, lacks the very feature that distinguishes intention from mere aspiration or unfulfilled resolve.

background_cases: A man who has been insulted feels anger — a hot, urgent affect directed at the offender, disposing him towards confrontation. His anger has clear intentional content, felt quality, and behavioural expression.
A woman who learns of her child's safety after a period of danger feels relief — a warm dissolution of tension directed at the welcome news, disposing her towards relaxation and tears.
A boy who receives an unexpected gift feels delight — a bright, expansive affect directed at the gift and the giver, disposing him towards laughter and expressions of gratitude.
definition: A mental state S of a subject P is an emotion if and only if (i) S has a determinate intentional content — it is about or directed at some object or state of affairs, (ii) S involves a characteristic felt quality or affective tone, and (iii) S can be assessed as appropriate or inappropriate to its intentional object.
conditions: S has a determinate intentional content — it is about or directed at some object or state of affairs
S involves a characteristic felt quality or affective tone
S can be assessed as appropriate or inappropriate to its intentional object
counterexample: Consider a case in which a man listens to a piece of music and is moved to tears. He experiences a profound affective state — a bittersweet, yearning quality that pervades his experience. The state has genuine felt quality and might be assessed as appropriate to the music in some sense. Yet it is unclear what the determinate intentional content of the emotion is. He is not sad about anything in particular; the music does not represent any state of affairs towards which his feeling is directed. The emotion is 'about' the music only in the loosest possible sense, and its intentional content, if it has any, resists specification in propositional terms.
abductive_insight: The best explanation of this difficulty is that the definition models all emotions on the pattern of the so-called 'cognitive' emotions — anger, fear, jealousy — whose intentional structure is relatively clear and propositionally articulable. But aesthetic and musical emotions, whilst possessing genuine felt quality and being subject to normative assessment, do not conform to this pattern. Their intentional structure, if they have one, is non-propositional and perhaps essentially indeterminate. The definition thus imposes upon the class of emotions a structural uniformity that the phenomena do not in fact display.
passage:
Emotions have been analysed as mental states possessing determinate intentional content, a characteristic felt quality, and susceptibility to normative assessment as appropriate or inappropriate to their objects. The paradigmatic cases support this analysis convincingly. Anger at an insult has clear intentional content (the insult), a characteristic phenomenology (hot urgency), and is assessable as appropriate (if the insult was genuine) or inappropriate (if one has misunderstood). Relief at good news, delight at a gift — these emotions likewise conform to the tripartite pattern without difficulty.

Yet consider a man who is listening to a piece of music — let us say a late quartet by Beethoven — and who is moved to tears by what he hears. He experiences a profound affective state, a bittersweet, yearning quality that pervades his experience and that he would, if pressed, describe as one of the most intense emotional episodes of his life. The felt quality is unmistakable; condition (ii) is satisfied beyond doubt. One might also maintain that the emotion is assessable as appropriate to the music; condition (iii) is at least arguably met. But what of condition (i)? What is the determinate intentional content of his emotion? He is not sad about any particular state of affairs. The music does not represent a loss, a danger, or a slight — or if it does, the man cannot say what loss, danger, or slight it represents. His emotion is directed at the music, certainly, but 'at the music' does not specify a determinate intentional content in the way that 'at the insult' or 'at the good news' does. The emotion resists articulation in propositional terms; its aboutness, if it has any, is diffuse and essentially indeterminate. The most satisfactory explanation of this recalcitrant case, it seems to me, is that the definition has been modelled upon the so-called cognitive emotions — anger, fear, jealousy, gratitude — whose intentional structure is relatively clear and propositionally expressible. Aesthetic emotions, and musical emotions in particular, do not conform to this pattern. They possess genuine felt quality and are subject to normative assessment, but their intentional structure, if indeed they possess one, is of a fundamentally different kind — non-propositional, perhaps essentially resistant to determinate specification. The definition imposes upon the entire class of emotions a structural uniformity that the phenomena simply do not display.

background_cases: A woman raises her arm to hail a cab — her bodily movement is caused by her intention, she is aware of what she is doing, and the movement is under her voluntary control throughout.
A man signs his name on a document — his intention causes the movement of his hand, he monitors the process, and he controls the pen's trajectory.
A child reaches for a sweet — her desire causes the reaching movement, she is aware of her goal, and she adjusts her reach as needed.
definition: A bodily movement M of agent A constitutes an intentional action if and only if (i) M is caused by an intention of A directed at bringing about M, (ii) A is aware of M as the execution of A's intention, and (iii) M matches the content of A's intention — that is, the movement that occurs is the movement that A intended.
conditions: M is caused by an intention of A directed at bringing about M
A is aware of M as the execution of A's intention
M matches the content of A's intention
counterexample: Consider a case in which a man intends to flip a light switch by moving his finger upward. His intention causes a finger movement, he is aware of making the movement, and the movement matches the content of his intention — his finger moves upward and flips the switch. However, unbeknown to him, the wiring has been altered so that the switch he flips does not control the light he intends to illuminate but instead activates a different light in another room. His bodily movement is exactly as intended, yet the action described as 'turning on the kitchen light' — which is what he intended to do — has not been performed, even though the bodily movement is a perfect match.
abductive_insight: The best explanation is that intentional action cannot be fully characterised in terms of the match between intention and bodily movement alone, because intentions typically have a richer content than the mere bodily movement — they encompass the expected consequences of the movement in the world. The definition restricts the matching condition to the movement itself, but most intentions are directed not merely at bodily movements but at the worldly outcomes those movements are expected to produce. When the world fails to cooperate — when the normal connection between movement and outcome is disrupted — the intention is frustrated even though the movement perfectly matches its motoric content.
passage:
Intentional action has been analysed as requiring that a bodily movement be caused by an appropriate intention, that the agent be aware of the movement as the execution of that intention, and that the movement match the intention's content. The standard cases — hailing a cab, signing a document, reaching for a desired object — conform to this pattern without difficulty. In each case, the agent's intention causes a movement, the agent is aware of executing the intention, and the movement that occurs is the movement that was intended.

Yet consider a man who intends to turn on the light in his kitchen by flipping the switch beside the door. His intention causes a movement of his finger — an upward flick — that is precisely the movement he intended to make. He is aware of making the movement and recognises it as the execution of his intention. The movement matches the content of his intention in every physical particular: his finger moves upward, the switch is flipped, and the mechanical action is completed. All three conditions are satisfied. However, unbeknown to the man, the wiring in his house has been altered by a workman earlier that day, and the switch now controls not the kitchen light but a lamp in an upstairs room that the man did not intend to illuminate. The kitchen remains dark. Now, the man's bodily movement was exactly as he intended, and he was aware of executing the intention. But the action he intended to perform — turning on the kitchen light — has not been performed. He has turned on a different light entirely. The most satisfactory explanation of this discrepancy, it seems to me, is that the definition restricts the matching condition to the bodily movement alone, whereas the content of most intentions extends well beyond the movement to encompass its expected worldly consequences. The man intended not merely to move his finger but to illuminate the kitchen. When the world fails to cooperate — when the customary connection between the movement and its consequence is disrupted — the intention is frustrated even though the movement matches its motoric content perfectly. An adequate analysis of intentional action must therefore accommodate the fact that intentions are characteristically directed at outcomes in the world, not merely at the bodily movements that are expected to produce those outcomes.

background_cases: A woman recalls her wedding day — she has a vivid mental image of the ceremony, the image was produced by her experience of the event, and she recognises it as a memory of that occasion.

A man remembers a conversation with his father — the words return to him with some accuracy, the recollection originates in his having participated in the conversation, and he identifies it as a memory.
A child recalls a holiday at the seaside — she can describe the beach, the weather, and certain incidents, and these recollections derive from her experience and are recognised by her as memories.
definition: A subject P has an episodic memory of event E if and only if (i) P experienced E, (ii) P has a present mental representation that preserves the content of the original experience of E, and (iii) P's present representation is phenomenologically marked as a memory — that is, it carries a felt sense of pastness and of having been personally experienced.
conditions: P experienced E
P has a present mental representation that preserves the content of the original experience of E
P's present representation is phenomenologically marked as a memory — it carries a felt sense of pastness and of having been personally experienced
counterexample: Consider a case in which a woman experienced an event in childhood — say, a family gathering at which a particular incident occurred. She has entirely forgotten the event. Years later, she is told the story of the incident by a relative, and from this telling she forms a vivid mental representation of the scene. Because the story is told in the first person and with great vividness, her representation acquires a felt sense of pastness and of personal experience — she 'seems to remember' the event. She satisfies all three conditions: she experienced the event, she has a representation preserving its content (derived, via the relative's testimony, from the original event), and the representation carries the phenomenological mark of memory. Yet the representation was not produced by her own retentive faculty but by her imaginative reconstruction from testimony.
abductive_insight: The best explanation is that the phenomenological mark of memory — the felt sense of pastness and personal experience — is not in fact a reliable indicator of genuine mnemonic origin. It can be produced by vivid narrative testimony combined with the knowledge that one was present at the event. The definition treats this phenomenological feature as a constitutive condition, but the case reveals that it is merely a typical accompaniment of genuine memory, capable of being elicited by non-mnemonic means. What is truly required is a causal condition specifying that the representation must have been sustained by the subject's own retentive processes, rather than reconstructed from external sources. The phenomenological mark is a symptom, not the substance, of genuine episodic memory.
passage:
Episodic memory has been characterised as requiring that the subject experienced the remembered event, that the subject presently entertains a representation preserving the content of that experience, and that the representation carry a distinctive phenomenological mark — a felt sense of pastness and of personal involvement. These conditions are satisfied in the paradigmatic cases. A woman who recalls her wedding day has a representation derived from her experience, preserving its content, and carrying the unmistakable phenomenological quality of memory. Similar accounts may be given for the man who remembers a conversation and the child who recalls a holiday.

Yet consider a woman who, in early childhood, attended a family gathering at which a memorable incident took place — let us say that a much-loved uncle performed an amusing conjuring trick. The woman has entirely forgotten this event; no trace of it remains in her memory, and she would deny, if asked, that any such thing occurred. Some years later, a relative describes the incident to her in vivid detail, narrating it from the woman's own perspective: 'You were sitting on the carpet, and Uncle James produced a coin from behind your ear, and you laughed so hard that you fell over backwards.' From this narration, the woman forms a rich and detailed mental representation of the scene. Because she knows she was present, and because the narrative is told with such immediacy, the representation acquires a felt quality of pastness and personal experience — it 'seems to her' that she remembers the incident. She can, as it were, 'see' Uncle James producing the coin. Now, all three conditions of the definition are met. She experienced the event. She has a present representation preserving its content — the representation is accurate, for her relative's account was faithful. And the representation carries the phenomenological mark of memory. Yet what she has is not a genuine episodic memory but an imaginative reconstruction from testimony. The most illuminating explanation of this failure, one is inclined to think, is that the phenomenological mark of memory — the felt sense of pastness — is not constitutive of genuine remembering but merely its typical accompaniment. It can be produced by vivid testimony combined with background knowledge of one's own presence at the event. An adequate analysis must supplement or replace the phenomenological condition with a causal requirement: the representation must have been sustained by the subject's own retentive faculty from the time of the experience to the present, rather than having been recreated from an external source.

background_cases: A man who refrains from stealing despite strong temptation exercises self-control — he acts against his strongest desire on the basis of his moral judgement.
A woman who persists with a demanding exercise regimen despite discomfort exercises self-control — she overrides her desire to stop on the basis of her long-term goals.
A student who resists the temptation to cheat on an examination exercises self-control — he subordinates his desire for a good mark to his commitment to honesty.
definition: An agent A exercises self-control in performing φ rather than ψ if and only if (i) A has a desire to ψ that is stronger than A's desire to φ, (ii) A nevertheless performs φ on the basis of A's evaluative judgement that φ is the better course of action, and (iii) A's performance of φ is effortful — that is, it requires the expenditure of motivational resources to overcome the desire to ψ.
conditions: A has a desire to ψ that is stronger than A's desire to φ
A nevertheless performs φ on the basis of A's evaluative judgement that φ is the better course of action
A's performance of φ is effortful — it requires the expenditure of motivational resources to overcome the desire to ψ
counterexample: Consider a case in which a man has a strong desire to eat a rich dessert and a weaker desire to decline it. He judges that declining is the better course and does so. Yet his declining is not effortful at all — the judgement immediately and automatically overrides the desire without any felt struggle, expenditure of motivational resources, or sense of exertion. He simply declines, smoothly and without inner conflict. Conditions (i) and (ii) are met, but condition (iii) is not, and yet we should wish to say that he has exercised self-control, for he has acted against his stronger desire on the basis of his evaluative judgement.
abductive_insight: The best explanation is that the definition conflates self-control with effortful self-control, treating the latter as though it exhausted the category. But self-control may be exercised with varying degrees of effort, including none at all, when the agent's evaluative capacities are sufficiently well-developed to override recalcitrant desires automatically. The virtuous person, on many traditional accounts, acts rightly without inner struggle, and it would be paradoxical to deny that such a person exercises self-control. The effortfulness condition mistakes a common feature of self-control for an essential one, and in doing so excludes the most accomplished exercises of the capacity it purports to define.
passage:
Self-control has been analysed as the effortful overriding of a stronger desire by an evaluative judgement. The paradigmatic cases support this account: the man who resists stealing, the woman who persists with exercise, the student who refuses to cheat — all act against powerful desires through the expenditure of motivational effort, and all are rightly said to exercise self-control. The felt struggle, the sense of exertion, the awareness of overcoming an internal resistance — these features seem central to the phenomenon.

Yet consider a man who is offered a rich and tempting dessert at the conclusion of a dinner. He has a genuine and strong desire to eat it — the dessert is appealing and he is fond of such things. He also has a weaker desire to decline, grounded in his concern for his health. He judges, upon brief reflection, that declining is the better course of action. And he declines. But — and here is the crucial point — his declining involves no effort whatever. There is no felt struggle, no sense of exertion, no awareness of overcoming internal resistance. His evaluative judgement simply and smoothly overrides the desire, as a matter of well-established psychological habit. He declines as easily and automatically as he would return a greeting. Conditions (i) and (ii) are plainly satisfied: the desire to eat was stronger, and the action of declining proceeded from his evaluative judgement. But condition (iii) is not satisfied, for there was no effortfulness, no expenditure of motivational resources. Yet it seems clearly correct to say that the man exercised self-control. He acted against his stronger desire on the basis of his better judgement — this is precisely what self-control consists in. The most satisfying explanation, I venture to suggest, is that the definition has confused self-control with one particular and conspicuous manifestation of it — the effortful variety. Self-control may be exercised at varying degrees of effort, and the most accomplished exercises of it are those in which the evaluative judgement prevails without struggle. The virtuous man, on many traditional philosophical accounts, does the right thing smoothly and without inner turmoil. To deny that such a person exercises self-control is to exclude the highest form of the very capacity one means to define.

background_cases: A man tastes a ripe strawberry and is immediately aware of the distinctive sweetness, the slight acidity, the fragrance — a unified, vivid sensory experience that he can subsequently describe and compare with other gustatory experiences.

A woman listens to a string quartet performing a late Beethoven composition and is aware of the harmonies, the interplay of the instruments, the emotional colouring of the music — a rich, structured auditory experience.
A child touches snow for the first time and is aware of the cold, the wetness, the peculiar granular texture — a novel tactile experience that impresses itself vividly upon his consciousness.
definition: A mental state M is a conscious experience if and only if (i) M has a distinctive qualitative character — a 'what it is like' — that is immediately apprehended by the subject, (ii) M is a unified, temporally bounded episode with a discernible beginning and end, and (iii) M is novel or salient enough to stand out from the background of the subject's ongoing mental life.
conditions: M has a distinctive qualitative character — a 'what it is like' — that is immediately apprehended by the subject
M is a unified, temporally bounded episode with a discernible beginning and end
M is novel or salient enough to stand out from the background of the subject's ongoing mental life
counterexample: Consider the case of a man who has sat reading in the same chair for three hours, dimly aware of the pressure of the chair against his back, the warmth of the fire, the faint ticking of the clock. These are conscious experiences — he is aware of them, and if asked he would confirm their presence — yet they have no distinctive qualitative character that stands out; they are utterly familiar and unremarkable. They are not unified, temporally bounded episodes with discernible beginnings and ends; they persist as a continuous, unstructured background that blends into the general texture of the afternoon. And they are not novel or salient; they are precisely the opposite — habitual, inconspicuous, and easily overlooked. Yet they are conscious experiences.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all vivid, salient, temporally bounded experiences — tasting a strawberry, hearing a quartet, touching snow — that stood out sharply from the background of ordinary mental life. The definition abstracted qualitative distinctiveness, temporal boundedness, and novelty as essential to conscious experience. But the cases were drawn from a narrow class of vivid, attention-grabbing episodes, and the definition mistook their salience for a necessary condition of consciousness. The dim background awareness of the man reading in his chair reveals that consciousness encompasses a vast penumbra of faint, familiar, temporally unbounded, and wholly unremarkable experience that does not stand out from anything, yet is genuinely conscious. The definition confused the foreground of consciousness with consciousness itself.
passage:
Consider three cases of conscious experience. A man bites into a ripe strawberry and is immediately aware of the distinctive sweetness, the delicate acidity, the fragrance that floods his palate — a vivid, unified sensory episode that he can subsequently describe in detail. A woman listens to a string quartet performing a late Beethoven work and is aware of the interweaving voices, the harmonic tensions and resolutions, the emotional depth of the music — a rich, structured auditory experience with a clear beginning and end. A child touches snow for the first time and is struck by the cold, the wetness, the strange granular texture — a novel experience that impresses itself vividly upon his awareness. From these cases one might extract a definition: a mental state is a conscious experience if and only if it has a distinctive qualitative character immediately apprehended by the subject, is a unified temporally bounded episode, and is novel or salient enough to stand out from the background of ongoing mental life.

But one must consider the man reading in his chair by the fire. He has sat in the same chair for three hours, absorbed in his book. Throughout that time he has been dimly aware of the pressure of the upholstery against his back, the warmth of the fire upon his right side, the faint ticking of the clock upon the mantelpiece. These are not vivid experiences; they do not stand out. They have no distinctive qualitative character that arrests attention — the warmth is unremarkable, the pressure of the chair is familiar, the ticking is a sound he has heard ten thousand times. They are not unified, temporally bounded episodes; they have no discernible beginning or end but persist as a formless, continuous background that merges indistinguishably into the general texture of the afternoon. And they are not novel or salient; they are the very opposite — habitual, inconspicuous, the sort of thing one scarcely notices. Yet they are conscious experiences. The man is aware of them; if interrupted and asked whether he feels the warmth of the fire, he would confirm it at once. The definition, drawn from vivid, salient, temporally bounded episodes, mistook the features of the foreground of consciousness for the features of consciousness itself, and thereby excluded the vast, dim, continuous penumbra of background awareness that accompanies nearly every moment of waking life.

background_cases: A man intends to build a stone wall around his garden: he purchases stones, mixes mortar, and lays each stone in a deliberate sequence over the course of several weeks until the wall is complete.
A woman intends to learn Italian: she enrols in a course, studies vocabulary each evening, practises conversation with a tutor, and persists for two years until she can read Dante in the original.
A boy intends to save enough money to buy a bicycle: he sets aside a portion of his pocket-money each week, keeps a careful tally, and after nine months makes his purchase.
definition: A person P has an intention I if and only if (i) I is directed towards a temporally distant goal that requires sustained effort over an extended period, (ii) P takes concrete, sequential steps towards the achievement of I, and (iii) P is able to articulate I as a definite plan with identifiable intermediate stages.
conditions: I is directed towards a temporally distant goal that requires sustained effort over an extended period
P takes concrete, sequential steps towards the achievement of I
P is able to articulate I as a definite plan with identifiable intermediate stages
counterexample: A man walking along a pavement steps aside to avoid a puddle. He intends to avoid the puddle — his movement is purposive rather than accidental — yet his intention is not directed towards a temporally distant goal; it is directed at something that is to be accomplished in the next half-second. He takes no sequential steps; his sidestep is a single, fluid movement. And he cannot articulate a plan with intermediate stages — there are no stages; the whole affair is over almost before it has begun. Yet his stepping aside is an intentional action.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all long-term projects — building a wall, learning a language, saving for a bicycle — in which intention was manifested through sustained effort, sequential steps, and an articulable plan. The definition abstracted temporal distance, sequential action, and articulable planning as essential to intention. But the cases were drawn from a narrow class of extended, project-like intentions, and the definition mistook their features for the features of intention in general. The man stepping aside to avoid a puddle reveals that intention may be instantaneous, may consist in a single fluid movement, and may operate without any plan that the agent could articulate. The definition confused the features of long-term project-intentions with the necessary conditions of intention as such, overlooking the vast domain of momentary, unreflective, yet perfectly intentional actions that constitute the greater part of everyday conduct.
passage:
Consider three cases of intention. A man intends to build a stone wall around his garden: he purchases materials, prepares the ground, and lays each course of stone over the course of several weeks, following a plan he has sketched beforehand. A woman intends to learn Italian: she enrols in a course, studies vocabulary each evening, practises with a tutor each Saturday, and persists in this regimen for two years. A boy intends to save for a bicycle: he sets aside a portion of his pocket-money each week, keeps a written tally of his savings, and after nine months makes his purchase. From these cases one might extract a definition: a person has an intention if and only if it is directed towards a temporally distant goal requiring sustained effort, the person takes concrete sequential steps towards its achievement, and the person can articulate a definite plan with identifiable intermediate stages.

But one must consider the man and the puddle. A man is walking briskly along a pavement after a rain-shower. Ahead of him, occupying the greater part of the pavement, lies a large puddle. Without breaking stride, he steps to the right, describes a brief arc around the puddle, and continues on his way. His action is intentional — he stepped aside in order to avoid the puddle, and had the puddle not been there he would have continued in a straight line. But the intention is not directed towards a temporally distant goal; it is directed at an outcome — the avoidance of wet shoes — that is accomplished in less than a second. He takes no sequential steps; his movement is a single, fluid, almost instantaneous adjustment of his path. And he cannot articulate a plan with identifiable stages; there is nothing to articulate. The whole affair is over before any process of deliberation or planning could begin. Yet his action is intentional — as intentional as any action can be. The definition, drawn from long-term projects requiring sustained effort and articulate planning, mistook the features of that particular class of intentions for the features of intention itself, and thereby excluded the momentary, unreflective, but thoroughly purposive actions that make up the bulk of everyday intentional conduct.

background_cases: A man at fifty recalls in vivid detail the day of his wedding at twenty-five: the church, the music, the faces of the guests, the trembling of his hands as he spoke the vows.
A woman at seventy remembers her first day at university: the great hall, the unfamiliar faces, the excitement and anxiety of arriving in a strange city.
A soldier, decades after the war, remembers the night his company held a bridge under fire: the noise, the darkness, the faces of the men beside him, the cold of the river below.
definition: A person P at time t1 is identical with a person Q at time t2 if and only if (i) Q possesses vivid, first-person memories of events experienced by P, (ii) Q's memories of P's experiences are substantially accurate as to their content, and (iii) Q can, upon reflection, trace an unbroken chain of memories linking every year of the interval from t1 to t2.
conditions: Q possesses vivid, first-person memories of events experienced by P
Q's memories of P's experiences are substantially accurate as to their content
Q can, upon reflection, trace an unbroken chain of memories linking every year of the interval from t1 to t2
counterexample: Consider the case of a man who, at the age of sixty, can remember nothing whatsoever of the years between his fifteenth and twentieth birthdays. Those five years are a blank — not suppressed or reluctantly recalled, but simply gone, as though they had never been. He cannot say where he lived during that period, what he did, or whom he knew. Yet he is, by every reasonable reckoning, the same person as the boy of fifteen and the young man of twenty. His wife, who knew him at eighteen, recognises him; his old schoolmaster, who taught him at fourteen, would swear to his identity; and the law holds him responsible for obligations incurred during the forgotten years.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all instances of vivid, accurate, long-distance memory — the man recalling his wedding, the woman recalling university, the soldier recalling combat — in which identity was sustained by memorable, emotionally charged experiences that could be recalled across decades. The definition abstracted vivid memory, accuracy, and an unbroken chain of recollection as essential to personal identity. But the cases were drawn from a narrow class of highly memorable life-events, and the definition mistook the presence of vivid memories for a necessary condition of identity. The man with five years of total amnesia reveals that personal identity may persist through gaps in memory that are absolute and irrecoverable. The definition confused a sufficient evidential ground for identity — the ability to remember — with a necessary condition of identity itself, and thereby generated the absurd result that a person who forgets a period of his life ceases to have been the person who lived through it.
passage:
Consider three cases in which personal identity across time seems grounded in memory. A man at fifty recalls his wedding day with perfect vividness: the church, the music, the faces of the guests, the trembling of his hands. A woman at seventy remembers her first day at university: the vast hall, the anxious faces, the thrill of independence. A soldier, decades after the war, remembers the night his company held a bridge: the noise of the guns, the darkness, the cold river below. In each case the person at the later time possesses vivid, accurate, first-person memories of events experienced at the earlier time, and can trace an unbroken chain of recollection linking the two. From these cases one might extract a definition: a person at one time is identical with a person at a later time if and only if the later person possesses vivid first-person memories of events experienced by the earlier, those memories are substantially accurate, and the later person can trace an unbroken memorial chain across the intervening years.

But one must consider the man with the gap. A man of sixty, in good health and sound mind, discovers that he can remember nothing at all of the years between his fifteenth and twentieth birthdays. The period is a total blank. He can recall his fourteenth year — the school, the masters, the games — and he can recall his twenty-first — the beginning of his career, his first lodgings — but between these there is nothing. No amount of effort, no prompting by friends or family, no examination of old photographs or letters, restores so much as a single memory. Those five years are as thoroughly effaced from his mind as if they had been lived by another person. He cannot say where he was, what he did, whom he loved. The memorial chain is broken utterly. Yet he is, by every reasonable standard, the same person. His wife, who first knew him at eighteen, recognises him without hesitation. His former schoolmaster would testify to his identity. The law holds him to contracts signed and debts incurred during the forgotten years. The definition, drawn from cases of vivid, unbroken memory, mistook the presence of memories for a necessary condition of identity, when in truth identity persists through even the most complete and irrecoverable failure of recollection.

background_cases: A man feels a surge of anger when another man insults him to his face — his fists clench, his jaw tightens, his pulse quickens, and the anger is directed squarely at the person who insulted him.
A woman feels a flash of fear when a large dog lunges at her — her heart races, her muscles tense for flight, and the fear is directed at the dog before her.
A child feels delight upon receiving a gift — he laughs, claps his hands, his eyes widen, and the delight is directed at the bright, shiny object in his hands.
definition: A mental state M is an emotion if and only if (i) M is an acute, short-lived episode triggered by a specific event in the subject's immediate environment, (ii) M is accompanied by pronounced and characteristic bodily changes — changes in heart-rate, muscle tension, respiration, or facial expression, and (iii) M is directed at a specific, present object or person that the subject can identify.
conditions: M is an acute, short-lived episode triggered by a specific event in the subject's immediate environment
M is accompanied by pronounced and characteristic bodily changes — changes in heart-rate, muscle tension, respiration, or facial expression
M is directed at a specific, present object or person that the subject can identify
counterexample: Consider the melancholy that settles upon a man on a grey November afternoon. He is sitting alone in his room; nothing has happened — no event has triggered the feeling. The melancholy is not acute or short-lived; it persists for hours, perhaps days, as a diffuse, low-lying mood that colours everything without attaching to anything in particular. It is not accompanied by pronounced bodily changes; his heart-rate is normal, his muscles are relaxed, his breathing is even. And it is not directed at any specific, present object or person; if asked what he is melancholy about, he would be unable to say. Yet the melancholy is an emotion — one of the most familiar and deeply human emotions there is.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all acute, triggered, bodily, object-directed emotional episodes — anger at an insult, fear of a lunging dog, delight at a gift. The definition abstracted acuteness, bodily change, and object-directedness as essential to emotion. But the cases were drawn from a narrow class of reactive, stimulus-driven emotional episodes, and the definition mistook their features for the features of emotion in general. The November melancholy reveals that an emotion may be untriggered, chronic rather than acute, accompanied by no discernible bodily change, and directed at no identifiable object. The definition confused the features of reactive emotional episodes with the necessary conditions of emotion as such, and thereby excluded the vast domain of diffuse, objectless, long-lasting moods that are among the most pervasive and humanly significant of all emotional phenomena.
passage:
Consider three cases of emotion. A man is insulted to his face and feels a surge of anger — his fists clench, his jaw tightens, his pulse races, and the anger is directed unmistakably at the person who has insulted him. A woman walking in a lane is startled by a large dog that lunges at her from behind a gate — her heart pounds, her muscles tense, and the fear is directed at the dog before her. A child receives a gift and feels delight — he laughs, claps his hands, his eyes shine, and the delight is directed at the bright object he holds. From these cases one might extract a definition: a mental state is an emotion if and only if it is an acute, short-lived episode triggered by a specific event, is accompanied by pronounced bodily changes, and is directed at a specific, present object or person.

But one must consider the melancholy of a November afternoon. A man sits alone in his room on a grey, still day in late autumn. Nothing has happened. No one has insulted him, no animal has startled him, no gift has been given or withheld. Yet a melancholy settles upon him — gradually, imperceptibly, like the fading of the light outside his window. The feeling is not acute; it is diffuse and pervasive, colouring his thoughts, his perceptions, the very quality of the afternoon. It is not short-lived; it persists for hours, and he suspects it will be with him tomorrow as well. It was not triggered by any specific event; if asked when it began, he could not say. It is accompanied by no pronounced bodily changes; his heart beats at its normal rate, his muscles are relaxed, his breathing is even. He does not clench his fists or tense his jaw or widen his eyes. And it is not directed at any specific, present object or person. If asked what he is melancholy about, he would hesitate, and then perhaps say that he does not know — that the feeling seems to be about nothing in particular, or about everything. Yet the melancholy is an emotion. It is as real, as characteristically human, and as deeply felt as any flash of anger or stab of fear. The definition, drawn from acute, reactive, object-directed emotional episodes, mistook the features of that particular class for the necessary conditions of emotion, and thereby excluded the diffuse, objectless, untriggered moods that pervade so much of human emotional life.

background_cases: A man sits in his study and reflects upon his own thoughts; he is aware that he is thinking, aware of the content of his thoughts, and can report his mental states to another.
A woman listens to a piece of music and is moved by its beauty; she is conscious of the music, conscious of her emotional response, and can describe the character of her experience.
A child touches a hot stove and immediately withdraws his hand; he is conscious of the pain, conscious of its location, and cries out in response.
definition: A being B is conscious if and only if (i) B has subjective experiences — that is, there is something it is like to be B, (ii) B can reflect upon its own mental states, and (iii) B can communicate its experiences to others by means of language or symbolic expression.
conditions: B has subjective experiences — there is something it is like to be B
B can reflect upon its own mental states
B can communicate its experiences to others
counterexample: Consider a dog that has been struck by a passing vehicle and lies injured upon the roadside. The dog whimpers, trembles, and attempts to lick its wounds. Its behaviour is not that of an automaton; it exhibits every sign of suffering. One would be hard pressed to deny that there is something it is like to be this dog in this moment — that the dog has a subjective experience of pain. Yet the dog cannot reflect upon its own mental states in the manner that a human philosopher reflects upon his. It does not form second-order thoughts about its pain; it does not consider the nature of its suffering or compare the present pain with past pains. Nor can it communicate its experiences by means of language or symbolic expression. It cannot say 'I am in pain' or describe the quality of its suffering. The second and third conditions fail, yet the dog is, by any reasonable assessment, a conscious being.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all instances of mature human consciousness — a man reflecting upon his thoughts, a woman describing her emotional response to music, a child who, however young, is acquiring the capacity for linguistic expression. The definition, abstracted from these cases, treated reflective self-awareness and linguistic communication as necessary conditions of consciousness. But the background cases were drawn from a single species at a particular stage of cognitive development, and the definition mistook the sophisticated features of adult human consciousness for the essential features of consciousness as such. The injured dog reveals the limitation: a being may have subjective experience — may suffer, may feel — without possessing the capacity for self-reflection or linguistic expression. The definition confused the features of human consciousness with the conditions of consciousness in general, and thereby excluded from the domain of the conscious the vast multitude of sentient creatures that experience the world without reflecting upon or reporting their experience.
passage:
Consider three cases of consciousness. A man sits in his study, reflects upon his own thoughts, and is aware both of what he thinks and of the fact that he is thinking; he can report his mental states to a companion. A woman listens to a symphony and is moved by its beauty; she is conscious of the music, of her emotional response, and can describe the character of her experience in words. A child touches a hot stove, feels pain, and cries out; he is conscious of the pain and communicates his distress. In each case, the being has subjective experience, can reflect upon its mental states, and can communicate its experiences. From these cases one might extract a definition: a being is conscious if and only if it has subjective experiences, can reflect upon its own mental states, and can communicate its experiences to others.

But one must consider the case of a dog that has been struck by a passing vehicle and lies injured upon the roadside. The animal whimpers and trembles; it attempts to drag itself to the verge; it licks its wounds with a persistence that bespeaks distress. Its eyes are wide and its body is tense with what, in a human being, one would unhesitatingly call suffering. One would be hard pressed, observing this animal, to deny that there is something it is like to be this creature in this moment — that it has a subjective experience of pain, that the pain is felt, that the suffering is real. Yet the dog cannot reflect upon its own mental states. It does not form the thought 'I am in pain' or consider the nature of its experience. It does not compare the present pain with past pains or wonder whether the pain will subside. Its consciousness, if consciousness it is, lacks the reflective, self-monitoring character that the second condition demands. Nor can the dog communicate its experience by means of language or symbolic expression. It whimpers, it cries, it exhibits behavioural signs of distress, but it cannot describe the quality of its suffering or articulate the nature of what it feels. The second and third conditions of the definition fail. The background cases were all drawn from the experience of human beings possessed of the capacity for self-reflection and linguistic report. The definition, shaped by those paradigms, treated self-reflection and communication as essential to consciousness, when in truth these are distinctive achievements of the human mind that are not shared by the countless other sentient beings — dogs, horses, birds, and many more — whose capacity for subjective experience is attested by every observation of their behaviour and every analogy with our own.

background_cases: A man goes to sleep at night and wakes in the morning; he remembers falling asleep, recognises his surroundings, and continues the projects he began the day before; he is the same person.
A woman travels abroad for a year and returns home; she remembers her journey, is recognised by her family, and resumes her former life; she is the same person.

A child grows into an adult over the course of twenty years; despite great changes in appearance, knowledge, and character, the adult remembers his childhood and is continuous with the child; he is the same person.
definition: A person P at time t1 is the same person as a person Q at time t2 if and only if (i) Q remembers the experiences of P, (ii) Q's memories are caused, through the normal processes of memory retention, by P's original experiences, and (iii) there is no branching — that is, there is no other person R at t2 who also satisfies conditions (i) and (ii) with respect to P.
conditions: Q remembers the experiences of P
Q's memories are caused through normal memory processes by P's original experiences
there is no branching — no other person also satisfies the first two conditions
counterexample: Consider an elderly man in the advanced stages of a progressive disease of the brain that has destroyed his capacity for memory. He does not remember yesterday, let alone the experiences of his youth. He does not recognise his wife, does not recall his children's names, and cannot say where he was born or what trade he followed. His memories have been erased — not merely rendered inaccessible, but destroyed by the degeneration of the neural structures that once sustained them. The first condition of the definition fails: he does not remember the experiences of the man he was. Yet he is the same person. His wife knows him; the law regards him as the same individual; his property and his obligations remain his own. The definition, which makes personal identity depend upon the preservation of memory, cannot account for the persistence of identity through the total loss of memory.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all ones in which memory was intact: the man who sleeps and wakes, the woman who travels and returns, the child who grows into an adult. In every case, the later person remembered the experiences of the earlier person, and the continuity of memory provided both the evidence of identity and, seemingly, its basis. The definition, abstracted from these cases, treated memory as the essential condition of personal identity. But the background cases were drawn from the normal course of human life, in which memory is preserved, and the definition therefore did not need to confront the possibility of its total destruction.
