The elderly man whose memory has been annihilated by disease reveals the limitation: identity persists even when memory is wholly extinguished. The definition, shaped by cases of intact memory, mistook a reliable accompaniment of personal identity for its necessary condition, and could not accommodate the case in which the accompaniment is absent but the identity remains.
passage:
Consider three cases in which personal identity through time is unproblematic. A man sleeps and wakes; he remembers falling asleep, recognises his bedroom, and continues the tasks he began the evening before. A woman travels abroad for a year and returns; she remembers her departure, her travels, and her homecoming, and resumes her former life. A child grows into an adult over two decades; the adult remembers his childhood and is continuous with the child he was. In each case, the later person remembers the experiences of the earlier person, the memories are caused through the normal processes of retention, and there is no branching — no other person shares those memories. From these cases one might extract a definition: a person at one time is the same person at a later time if and only if the later person remembers the earlier person's experiences, the memories are normally caused, and there is no branching.

But one must consider the case of an elderly man in the advanced stages of a progressive disease that destroys the structures of the brain. Over the course of several years, the disease has erased his memories — not merely obscured them or rendered them difficult to retrieve, but destroyed the neural tissue in which they were encoded. He does not remember yesterday. He does not remember last year. He does not remember his wedding, his children's births, the house in which he lived for forty years, or the trade he followed for a lifetime. He does not recognise his wife when she visits him; he does not know his own name. The first condition of the definition fails entirely: the man at the present time does not remember the experiences of the man he was. The causal chain of memory has been severed not by some exotic philosophical thought-experiment but by the ordinary, if devastating, progress of disease. Yet he is the same person. His wife, sitting beside him, knows that this is the man she married. The law regards him as the same individual — his property is his, his debts are his, his legal identity is continuous with the identity of the man who, years ago, possessed a full and vivid memory of his life. The definition, drawn from cases in which memory was intact and identity was secured by the continuity of recollection, could not accommodate the case in which memory is wholly extinguished and yet identity persists. It mistook a characteristic accompaniment of personal identity — the preservation of memory — for the condition upon which identity depends, and thereby excluded from the domain of self-identical persons those whose memories have been destroyed by the contingencies of disease.

background_cases: A man decides to post a letter and walks to the pillar-box; his intention is to post the letter, and he carries out the intention by performing the relevant actions in sequence.
A woman decides to bake a cake, gathers the ingredients, follows a recipe, and produces the cake; her intention is carried out step by step.
A student decides to learn Latin, enrols in a course, attends lectures, and completes the examinations; his intention is realised over the course of a year.
definition: A person P intends to bring about a state of affairs S if and only if (i) P has a mental representation of S as a goal to be achieved, (ii) P has formulated a plan — a sequence of actions — that P believes will bring about S, and (iii) P is disposed to execute the plan and to adjust it if obstacles arise.
conditions: P has a mental representation of S as a goal
P has formulated a plan — a sequence of actions — believed to bring about S
P is disposed to execute the plan and adjust it if obstacles arise
counterexample: Consider a man who intends to live a good life. He does not have a plan — a formulated sequence of actions — that he believes will bring about a good life. The intention is not directed at a discrete, achievable goal in the manner of posting a letter or baking a cake; it is an orientation of the whole person towards an ideal that is never fully attained and that does not admit of a determinate sequence of steps. He cannot enumerate the actions that will constitute living well; the content of a good life unfolds in response to circumstances that cannot be anticipated. Yet his intention is genuine. It shapes his choices, governs his conduct, and gives direction to his existence. The second condition of the definition fails, yet the intention is as real as any.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all instances of intention directed at discrete, achievable goals — posting a letter, baking a cake, learning Latin — for which a determinate plan could be formulated and executed. The definition, abstracted from these cases, treated the formulation of a plan as a necessary condition of intention. But the background cases were drawn from the domain of specific, bounded projects, and the definition mistook the features of project-directed intention for the features of intention as such. The intention to live a good life reveals the limitation: one may intend something for which no determinate plan can be formulated, something that is not a discrete goal but an ongoing orientation towards an ideal. The definition, shaped by the paradigm of plan-governed action directed at specific outcomes, could not accommodate the broader and more fundamental species of intention that consists not in planning a sequence of actions but in committing oneself to a way of living.
passage:
Consider three cases of intention. A man decides to post a letter, walks to the pillar-box, and posts it; his intention was to post the letter, and he formulated and executed a simple plan to that end. A woman decides to bake a cake, gathers the ingredients, follows a recipe, and produces the cake; her intention was directed at a specific goal, and she carried it out by a determinate sequence of actions. A student decides to learn Latin, enrols in a course, attends lectures, and sits the examinations; his intention was realised over the course of a year through a planned sequence of steps. In each case, the person has a mental representation of a goal, has formulated a plan believed to achieve it, and is disposed to execute and adjust the plan. From these cases one might extract a definition: a person intends to bring about a state of affairs if and only if he has a mental representation of it as a goal, has formulated a plan to achieve it, and is disposed to execute the plan.

But one must consider the man who intends to live a good life. His intention is genuine and deeply held. It shapes the decisions he makes — the career he pursues, the friendships he cultivates, the manner in which he treats those who depend upon him. It governs his conduct in moments of temptation and difficulty, and it gives direction and coherence to his existence as a whole. Yet he has not formulated a plan — a determinate sequence of actions — that he believes will bring about a good life. He cannot enumerate the steps, for the content of a good life is not fixed in advance; it unfolds in response to circumstances, opportunities, and misfortunes that cannot be foreseen. There is no recipe for living well as there is a recipe for baking a cake. The good life is not a discrete goal that can be achieved and then set aside; it is an ongoing endeavour, an orientation of the whole person towards an ideal that is never fully realised and that admits of no determinate terminus. The second condition of the definition fails. The background cases — posting a letter, baking a cake, learning Latin — were all instances of intention directed at bounded, achievable objectives for which a plan could be articulated and followed. The definition, drawn from these cases, treated the formulation of a plan as essential to intention, and thereby excluded the deeper and more pervasive form of intention that consists not in planning a specific sequence of actions but in committing oneself to a manner of living whose particular content cannot be specified in advance.

background_cases: A man deliberates between two career paths, weighs the advantages of each, and chooses the one that best accords with his long-term aspirations; his choice is free.
A woman considers whether to accept a proposal of marriage, reflects upon her feelings and her prospects, and decides to accept; her decision is free.
A student chooses to study philosophy rather than law, having considered both options and concluded that philosophy better suits his temperament; his choice is free.
definition: A person P makes a free choice if and only if (i) P is presented with genuine alternatives, (ii) P deliberates rationally about the alternatives — weighing reasons for and against each, and (iii) P selects the alternative that P judges to be supported by the strongest reasons.
conditions: P is presented with genuine alternatives
P deliberates rationally about the alternatives
P selects the alternative judged to be supported by the strongest reasons
counterexample: Consider a person who is presented with two courses of action that he judges to be exactly equally supported by reasons. Neither alternative is favoured by his deliberation; the reasons on each side are, so far as he can determine, precisely balanced. After prolonged reflection, he simply chooses one — not because he judges it to be supported by stronger reasons, for he does not, but because he must act and cannot remain indefinitely in a state of indecision. His choice is free; it is not coerced, not compelled, and not the product of external manipulation. Yet the third condition fails: he does not select the alternative he judges to be supported by the strongest reasons, for he judges neither to be so supported. He simply chooses.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all instances in which the deliberating agent found that one alternative was supported by stronger reasons than the other — one career path was more promising, the proposal of marriage was worth accepting, philosophy was better suited than law. The definition, abstracted from these cases, treated the selection of the best-supported alternative as a necessary condition of free choice. But the background cases were ones in which deliberation yielded a determinate verdict, and the definition did not need to address the possibility that deliberation might fail to discriminate. The case of the agent confronted with equally balanced reasons reveals the gap: free choice may occur precisely where deliberation runs out, where the reasons are exhausted and the agent must exercise a capacity for selection that goes beyond the weighing of reasons. The definition, drawn from cases in which reasons determined the outcome, could not accommodate the possibility that freedom is most clearly manifested when one must choose without the guidance of a preponderant reason.
passage:
Consider three cases of free choice. A man deliberates between two career paths, weighs the advantages and disadvantages of each, and chooses the one that best accords with his aspirations; his choice is free because it issues from rational deliberation and is determined by the strongest reasons. A woman considers a proposal of marriage, reflects upon her feelings and her prospects, and accepts because, on balance, the reasons for acceptance outweigh those against; her decision is free. A student chooses philosophy over law, having concluded that the reasons favouring philosophy — his temperament, his interests, his aptitudes — are stronger. In each case, the agent deliberates among alternatives and selects the one judged to be best supported by reasons. From these cases one might extract a definition: a person makes a free choice if and only if he is presented with genuine alternatives, deliberates rationally, and selects the alternative supported by the strongest reasons.

But one must consider the case of a person confronted with two alternatives that he judges to be exactly equally supported by reasons. He must choose between two positions of employment that are, so far as he can determine, indistinguishable in their merits. The salary is the same; the duties are equally congenial; the prospects for advancement are comparable; the locations are equally convenient. He deliberates at length. He draws up lists of advantages and disadvantages. He consults friends and weighs their counsel. And at the end of his deliberation, he finds that the reasons on each side are precisely balanced. Neither alternative is favoured; neither is disfavoured; the scales are level. Yet he must act. He cannot remain in a state of perpetual indecision; both positions require an answer by a stated date. He chooses one. His choice is not the product of coercion, not the result of a coin-toss, not an arbitrary whim. It is a genuine exercise of agency — a decision made by a rational person in full possession of the relevant facts. Yet he does not select the alternative he judges to be supported by the strongest reasons, for he judges neither to be so supported. The third condition of the definition fails. The background cases were all ones in which deliberation yielded a clear verdict, and the definition, shaped by those cases, assumed that free choice always consists in following the strongest reasons. But freedom of choice may be most clearly manifested precisely when the reasons are exhausted — when one must choose not because the balance of reasons compels a particular selection but because one is an agent who can act even in the absence of a determining reason.

background_cases: A man describes in vivid detail the redness of the sunset, the warmth of the sand beneath his feet, and the melancholy the scene evokes in him; we say that he is conscious of the sunset.

A woman tastes a wine and reports that it is dry, with a hint of blackcurrant and a long finish; she discriminates among flavours with precision and communicates her experience; we say that she is conscious of the wine's qualities.
A child cries out that the water is too hot and pulls his hand away, then tells his mother what happened; we say that he is conscious of the heat.

A philosopher, in the course of introspection, reports a sequence of mental images — a face, a melody, a feeling of unease — that pass through his mind; we say that he is conscious of these inner states.
definition: A creature C is conscious if and only if (i) C has internal states that represent features of C's environment or of C's own body, (ii) C is able to report upon or verbally describe those internal states, and (iii) C can discriminate among those states — that is, C can distinguish one state from another and respond differentially to each.
conditions: C has internal states that represent features of C's environment or of C's own body
C is able to report upon or verbally describe those internal states
C can discriminate among those states — distinguishing one from another
counterexample: Consider a dog that has been struck by a motor car and lies whimpering on the road, its leg broken. The dog has internal states that represent a feature of its body — the injury — and it discriminates among states, for its behaviour in response to pain is markedly different from its behaviour when it is well. Yet the dog cannot report upon or verbally describe its internal states; it has no language, no system of verbal communication. The second condition fails. Yet one would have to be in the grip of a very peculiar theory to deny that the dog is conscious of its pain.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all drawn from human beings who possess language and use it to communicate their experiences. The definition abstracted the capacity for verbal report as essential to consciousness, when in truth it is merely a feature of the particular creatures — adult, linguistically competent human beings — from which the background cases were drawn. Consciousness may be present wherever there is subjective experience, whether or not the creature possessing it can put that experience into words.
passage:
Consider four cases of what we should ordinarily call consciousness. A man stands on a beach at sunset and describes the scene in vivid detail — the redness of the sky, the warmth of the sand, the melancholy that the fading light evokes. A woman tastes a wine and reports its qualities with discrimination — the dryness, the hint of blackcurrant, the length of the finish. A child cries out that the water is too hot and withdraws his hand, then recounts the experience to his mother. A philosopher introspects and reports a sequence of mental images — a face, a melody, a feeling of unease — passing through his awareness. In each case, the subject has internal states that represent features of the environment or of the body, is able to report upon those states verbally, and can discriminate among them. One might extract from these cases a definition: a creature is conscious if and only if it has representational internal states, is able to report upon them, and can discriminate among them.

But one must consider the case of a dog that has been struck by a motor car. The animal lies on the road, whimpering, its hind leg broken. It flinches when the injured limb is touched; it licks the wound; it responds to the approach of a human being with a mixture of hope and apprehension. The dog has internal states that represent a feature of its body — the injury, the pain. It discriminates among its states with evident precision, for its behaviour when it is in pain is strikingly different from its behaviour when it is well — the whimpering, the flinching, the altered posture are responses specifically to the injury and not to any other condition. But the dog cannot report upon its internal states. It cannot say that it is in pain, or describe the character of the pain, or tell us where it hurts, for it has no language and no system of verbal communication. The second condition of the definition fails. Yet it would require a remarkable insensitivity — or a remarkable attachment to a philosophical theory — to deny that the dog is conscious of its pain. The background cases from which the definition was drawn were all cases of linguistically competent adult human beings, and the definition abstracted the capacity for verbal report as a necessary condition of consciousness. But this capacity is a feature of the particular creatures from which the cases were drawn, not a feature of consciousness as such. The dog reveals that consciousness may be present wherever there is subjective experience, whether or not the creature possessing it has the linguistic resources to communicate that experience to another.

background_cases: A man deliberates at length about which university to attend, weighs the merits of each option, and finally decides upon Oxford; we say that he intends to go to Oxford.
A woman considers whether to accept a position in London or remain in Edinburgh, discusses the matter with her family, and resolves to move; we say that she intends to move to London.
A boy thinks carefully about whether to spend his pocket money on a book or a toy, and after some reflection chooses the book; we say that he intends to buy the book.
definition: An agent A intends to do X if and only if (i) A has considered at least two alternative courses of action, (ii) A has weighed the reasons for and against each alternative, and (iii) A has selected X as the preferred alternative on the basis of that weighing.
conditions: A has considered at least two alternative courses of action
A has weighed the reasons for and against each alternative
A has selected X as the preferred alternative on the basis of that weighing
counterexample: Consider a man who wakes in the morning and, without any deliberation or comparison of alternatives, gets out of bed. He does not weigh the reasons for and against remaining in bed; the thought of remaining in bed does not so much as cross his mind. He simply gets up, as he has done every morning for forty years. He has not considered alternatives, he has not weighed reasons, and he has not selected getting up as the preferred option on the basis of deliberation. All three conditions fail. Yet he intends to get up — his action is intentional, not a reflex or a spasm — and to deny that he has an intention would be to render the concept of intention inapplicable to the greater part of human conduct.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all cases of considered choice among explicit alternatives — the kind of decision that occupies a prominent place in our conscious lives and that we recall and describe with ease. The definition abstracted the structure of deliberate choice as essential to intention. But most of our intentions are not the products of deliberation at all; they are habitual, automatic, and formed without any consideration of alternatives, and they are intentions nonetheless.
passage:
Consider three cases of what we should ordinarily call intention. A man deliberates at length about which university to attend, weighing the merits of Oxford against those of Cambridge and of several other institutions, and finally decides upon Oxford. A woman considers whether to accept a position in London or remain in Edinburgh, discusses the matter with her family over the course of several weeks, and resolves to move. A boy thinks carefully about whether to spend his pocket money on a book or a toy, and after some reflection chooses the book. In each case, the agent has considered at least two alternatives, has weighed the reasons for and against each, and has selected one as the preferred option. One might extract a definition: an agent intends to do something if and only if the agent has considered alternative courses of action, has weighed the reasons for and against each, and has selected the intended action on the basis of that weighing.

But consider a man who wakes in the morning and gets out of bed. He does not lie there deliberating about whether to rise or to remain under the covers. The thought of remaining in bed does not cross his mind. He does not weigh the reasons for getting up against the reasons for staying — the warmth of the blankets, the chill of the air, the obligations of the day. He simply gets up, as he has done every morning for forty years, with the same unreflective regularity with which he breathes. He has not considered alternatives; he has not weighed reasons; he has not selected getting up as the preferred option on the basis of any process of deliberation. All three conditions of the definition fail. Yet his getting up is intentional. It is not a reflex, not a spasm, not a movement caused by an external force. If one were to ask him, he would say that he got up because it was morning and there were things to be done — but this retrospective rationalisation should not be mistaken for the deliberation that the definition requires. The background cases from which the definition was drawn were all cases of significant, considered choices — the kind of choices we remember, discuss, and take pride in. The definition abstracted the structure of deliberate decision as a necessary condition of intention. But the great majority of our intentions arise without any deliberation at all; they are the products of habit, training, and the unreflective responsiveness of a competent agent to the demands of daily life.

background_cases: A man looks at photographs of himself as a child and remembers the occasions on which they were taken — the birthday party, the seaside holiday, the first day at school; we say he is the same person as the child in the photographs.

A woman reads her old diaries and recognises the handwriting, the preoccupations, and the emotional tone as her own, even though she has changed greatly in the intervening years; we say she is the same person as the diarist.

A soldier, now elderly, recalls his experiences in the war — the fear, the camaraderie, the particular smell of the trenches — and tells his grandchildren about them; we say he is the same person who served in the war.
definition: A person P at time t2 is the same person as P at time t1 if and only if (i) P at t2 can recall experiences had by P at t1, (ii) P at t2 recognises the thoughts, feelings, and character of P at t1 as belonging to the same self, and (iii) there is a felt continuity — P at t2 has the sense of being the same person who existed at t1.
conditions: P at t2 can recall experiences had by P at t1
P at t2 recognises the thoughts, feelings, and character of P at t1 as belonging to the same self
There is a felt continuity — P at t2 has the sense of being the same person
counterexample: Consider a man who suffers from severe retrograde amnesia following an accident. He cannot recall any experience from before the accident — not his childhood, not his marriage, not his career. He does not recognise the thoughts and feelings recorded in his old letters as his own; they seem to him to belong to a stranger. He has no felt sense of continuity with the person who existed before the accident. All three conditions fail. Yet his wife, his children, and the law continue to regard him as the same person, and there seem to be compelling reasons — the continuity of his body, the persistence of deep character traits that survived the amnesia — for thinking that they are right to do so.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involved persons who possessed rich autobiographical memories and a vivid sense of continuity with their past selves. The definition abstracted these psychological features as constitutive of personal identity. But the background cases were drawn from persons with normally functioning memories, and the definition mistook the deliverances of memory — which is a means by which we become aware of our identity — for the grounds of identity itself.
passage:
Consider three cases in which we judge that a person at one time is the same person as at an earlier time. A man looks at photographs of himself as a child and remembers the occasions — the birthday party, the seaside holiday, the first day at school. A woman reads her old diaries and recognises the handwriting and the preoccupations as her own. An elderly soldier recalls his experiences in the war and recounts them to his grandchildren. In each case, the later person can recall experiences had by the earlier person, recognises the earlier person's thoughts and feelings as belonging to the same self, and has a felt sense of continuity — the sense of being the very same person who underwent those experiences. One might extract a definition: a person at a later time is the same person as at an earlier time if and only if the later person can recall the earlier person's experiences, recognises the earlier person's inner life as his own, and has a felt continuity of selfhood.

But consider a man who suffers a devastating injury to his brain — a fall, a stroke, an infection — and is left with severe retrograde amnesia. He cannot recall a single experience from before the injury. His childhood is a blank; his marriage, which lasted twenty years, is unknown to him; his career, his friendships, his travels — all are lost. When his wife shows him letters he wrote during their courtship, the handwriting is unfamiliar to him and the sentiments expressed seem to belong to another person entirely. He has no felt sense of continuity with the man who wrote those letters; when he looks in the mirror, the face that looks back is that of a stranger who has somehow become familiar. All three conditions of the definition fail. He cannot recall the earlier person's experiences, he does not recognise the earlier person's inner life as his own, and there is no felt continuity. Yet his wife insists that he is the same man she married, and she seems to be right. His body is the same body; certain deep traits of character — a particular stubbornness, a dry wit, a tendency to kindness — have survived the amnesia intact, manifesting themselves in new situations in ways that those who knew him recognise immediately. The law treats him as the same person, and for practical purposes it is difficult to see how it could do otherwise. The background cases were all drawn from persons with normally functioning memories, and the definition mistook memory — which is one means by which we become aware of our identity over time — for the thing that constitutes that identity.

background_cases: A philosopher, offered a choice between tea and coffee at breakfast, considers the merits of each and selects coffee; we say he chose freely.
A voter in a general election weighs the policies of the competing parties, reflects upon her own values, and casts her ballot for the Liberal candidate; we say she voted freely.
A young man, having been offered positions at two firms, considers the salary, the prospects, and the character of each, and accepts the offer from the smaller firm; we say he chose freely.
definition: An agent A chooses freely in choosing X if and only if (i) A is presented with two or more options, (ii) A reflects upon the merits of each option in relation to A's own values and preferences, and (iii) A selects X on the basis of that reflection, without any option being forced upon A by external pressure.
conditions: A is presented with two or more options
A reflects upon the merits of each option in relation to A's values and preferences
A selects X on the basis of that reflection without external pressure
counterexample: Consider a mother who sees her child fall into a river. Without a moment's reflection, without considering alternatives, without weighing the option of diving in against the option of calling for help, she plunges into the water and pulls the child to safety. She was not presented with options in any psychologically real sense — the action erupted from her before any process of reflection could begin. She did not weigh merits or consult her values; she simply acted. No external pressure forced her hand. Yet we should say that she chose freely — indeed, that her action expressed her deepest values more authentically than any reflective choice could have done.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all cases of calm, reflective choice among explicitly presented alternatives — the kind of choosing that occurs in comfortable circumstances where time permits deliberation. The definition abstracted reflection and the comparison of options as necessary conditions. But the mother's case reveals that free choice may be most fully realised in the absence of reflection, when a person's action springs directly from her character and values without the mediation of deliberation.
passage:
Consider three cases of what we should ordinarily call free choice. A philosopher at breakfast considers whether to take tea or coffee, reflects upon his preference, and selects coffee. A voter weighs the policies of competing parties, consults her own values, and casts her ballot for the Liberals. A young man compares two positions of employment, considers the salary and prospects of each, and accepts the offer from the smaller firm. In each case, the agent is presented with options, reflects upon their merits, and selects one on the basis of that reflection without external pressure. One might extract a definition: an agent chooses freely if and only if the agent is presented with two or more options, reflects upon the merits of each in relation to his own values, and selects one on the basis of that reflection.

But consider a mother who sees her child fall into a river. The child is swept away by the current, and within an instant — before any thought can form, before any alternative can present itself — the mother plunges into the water. She does not pause to consider options. The thought of standing on the bank and calling for help does not cross her mind. She does not weigh the risk to her own life against the prospect of saving the child's. She does not consult her values or reflect upon what she ought to do. She acts — immediately, wholly, and without the slightest hesitation. No external force compels her; no one pushes her into the river or threatens her with harm if she fails to jump. Yet none of the definition's conditions are satisfied. She was not presented with options in any psychologically real sense; she did not reflect upon merits; she did not select her action on the basis of deliberation. All three conditions fail. And yet her action is free — more free, one might think, than the philosopher's choice of coffee, which is a trivial exercise of preference, or the voter's choice of party, which may be influenced by any number of pressures she does not fully recognise. The mother's action springs from the deepest stratum of her character, from a love so fundamental that it does not require the permission of reflection to express itself. The background cases were drawn from situations of comfortable, low-stakes deliberation, and the definition abstracted the features of such situations as essential to free choice. The mother reveals that free choice may reach its fullest expression precisely when reflection falls away and action arises directly from what the agent most fundamentally is.

background_cases: A person who is awake and alert reports a rich stream of sensory experience — colours, sounds, tastes — and can describe these experiences in detail. We take this to be a paradigmatic case of consciousness.
A person in dreamless sleep exhibits no awareness of her surroundings and cannot report any experiences upon waking. We take this to be a paradigmatic case of unconsciousness.
A person under light sedation responds sluggishly to stimuli and, upon recovery, reports fragmentary and confused impressions. We regard this as a borderline or diminished case of consciousness.
definition: A being X is conscious at time t if and only if (i) X has ongoing sensory or cognitive states at t, (ii) X is able at t to report or otherwise express the content of those states, and (iii) X exhibits integrated responsiveness to environmental stimuli at t.
conditions: X has ongoing sensory or cognitive states at t
X is able at t to report or express the content of those states
X exhibits integrated responsiveness to environmental stimuli at t
counterexample: Consider a patient suffering from a severe motor impairment who, as subsequent brain imaging reveals, is fully conscious — experiencing a rich inner life of thought and sensation — yet is entirely unable to report, express, or outwardly manifest any of these states. She has ongoing sensory and cognitive states and is internally responsive to stimuli, yet she satisfies neither condition (ii) nor condition (iii) as judged from external observation. The definition excludes her from the class of conscious beings, which is plainly wrong.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involve beings whose consciousness is accompanied by the capacity for outward expression, tempting us to treat expressibility as a necessary condition. Yet the locked-in patient reveals that consciousness and the capacity for expression are dissociable, and that the definition has mistaken a frequent accompaniment for an essential feature.
passage:
If we examine the paradigmatic cases of consciousness and unconsciousness, a pattern emerges that might initially seem to warrant a tripartite definition. The fully conscious person has ongoing mental states, can report them, and responds in an integrated fashion to her environment. The unconscious sleeper lacks all three. The sedated patient occupies a middle ground, with diminished states, impaired reportability, and fragmented responsiveness. One might therefore propose that a being X is conscious at time t if and only if X has ongoing sensory or cognitive states at t, X is able at t to report or otherwise express the content of those states, and X exhibits integrated responsiveness to environmental stimuli at t.

This definition draws its plausibility from the background cases, in which the capacity for expression and the capacity for integrated response are reliably correlated with the presence of inner mental life. Yet the correlation is not a necessary connection, and a case which reveals this is the locked-in patient — a person who, owing to severe motor impairment, retains full conscious awareness while being wholly unable to communicate or to manifest outward responsiveness. Brain imaging may reveal vigorous neural activity corresponding to rich sensory and cognitive processing. The patient sees, hears, thinks, and feels, yet she can express none of this. The definition, having been induced from cases in which consciousness and expressibility travel together, fails precisely when they come apart. What the background cases have tempted us to do is to build the observable indicators of consciousness into the definition of consciousness itself, conflating the thing with the evidence for it.

background_cases: A man decides over breakfast to buy flowers on his way home, keeps the plan in mind throughout the day, and executes it upon passing the florist. We regard this as a clear case of an intention carried through to completion.
A woman resolves to learn Italian but abandons the project after a week, having decided it is not worth the effort. We regard this as an intention that was formed and then explicitly rescinded.
A child announces that he will build a sandcastle, begins immediately, and persists until it is finished. We regard this as a straightforward case of intending and acting.
A student plans to submit an essay by Friday, keeps the deadline in mind, and submits on Thursday evening. We take this as another paradigmatic instance of intention.
definition: An agent S intends to perform action A if and only if (i) S has consciously formulated a plan to perform A, (ii) S maintains that plan without revocation until the appropriate time for action, and (iii) when the appropriate time arrives, S initiates A.
conditions: S has consciously formulated a plan to perform A
S maintains that plan without revocation until the appropriate time
When the appropriate time arrives, S initiates A
counterexample: Consider a woman who consciously formulates a plan to attend a lecture, maintains the plan without revocation throughout the day, yet when the time arrives she initiates the journey to the lecture hall only to discover that the hall has burnt down. She has satisfied all three conditions — she planned, she persisted, she initiated — and yet the action is not completed. The definition counts her as having intended and, by condition (iii), as having acted upon her intention. But suppose we alter the case: she initiates the journey not from her maintained plan but because a friend, unaware of her plan, happens to invite her. She would have gone anyway, but the proximate cause of her departure is the friend's invitation, not her own plan. All three conditions appear to be met, yet the connection between her plan and her action is severed.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involve instances in which the plan is not only maintained but is the operative cause of the resulting action. The definition, drawn from these cases, fails to include a causal condition linking the plan to the initiation of action, because in every background case the causal link was present but unnoticed.
passage:
The background cases present a consistent picture: an agent formulates a plan, holds it in mind, and acts upon it when the moment comes. From these cases one might extract the definition that an agent S intends to perform action A if and only if S has consciously formulated a plan to perform A, S maintains that plan without revocation until the appropriate time for action, and when the appropriate time arrives, S initiates A. The definition appears to capture the structure common to all the paradigmatic instances.

Yet the background cases share a feature that the definition does not make explicit: in each case, the plan is not merely present in the agent's mind at the time of action but is the operative cause of the action's initiation. The man buys flowers because of his plan; the child builds the sandcastle because of his announcement; the student submits because of her intention. This causal link is so uniformly present in the background cases that it is easy to overlook. A case which exposes its absence from the definition may be constructed as follows. A woman plans to attend a lecture and maintains her plan throughout the day. When the time arrives, she sets out for the lecture hall — but not because of her plan. A friend, knowing nothing of her intention, telephones and invites her at just that moment, and it is the invitation rather than the plan that prompts her departure. She would have gone regardless, but the actual causal pathway runs from the friend's invitation, not from her own maintained plan. All three conditions are satisfied, yet the sense in which she acts on her intention is compromised. The definition has been induced from cases in which cause and plan coincided, and it has failed to register the causal connection as a separate necessary condition.

background_cases: A man at sixty recalls his childhood, recognises his own face in photographs from each decade, and traces a continuous bodily and psychological history from birth to the present. We unhesitatingly identify him as the same person throughout.

A woman emigrates, changes her name, learns a new language, and adopts new customs, yet her friends from childhood, upon meeting her again, recognise her personality, her manner of thought, and her characteristic turns of phrase. We regard her as the same person despite the surface changes.

A patient recovers from a prolonged illness during which his personality was temporarily altered by fever and medication; upon recovery, his former character reasserts itself. We regard the pre-illness and post-recovery person as the same individual.
definition: A person P at time t2 is identical to a person Q at time t1 if and only if (i) there is a continuous physical organism linking P to Q, (ii) P shares significant psychological characteristics with Q, and (iii) P can, at least in principle, recall experiences had by Q.
conditions: There is a continuous physical organism linking P to Q
P shares significant psychological characteristics with Q
P can at least in principle recall experiences had by Q
counterexample: Consider a case of severe and irreversible amnesia in which a man loses all capacity to recall any experience prior to the onset of his condition. His body is continuous, and his temperament, values, and intellectual dispositions remain strikingly unchanged. He satisfies conditions (i) and (ii) but fails condition (iii) entirely, for he cannot recall, even in principle, any experience from his prior life. Yet everyone who knows him — his wife, his colleagues, his physician — regards him without question as the same person. The legal system, too, holds him responsible for his prior acts and honours his prior contracts.
abductive_insight: The background cases tempt us to include memory as a necessary condition because in each case memory is conspicuously present alongside the other continuities. But these cases happen never to include total amnesia, and the definition consequently mistakes a feature common to the observed cases for a feature essential to identity itself.
passage:
The background cases present a reassuring convergence of criteria: bodily continuity, psychological similarity, and mnemonic connection all point in the same direction. The man at sixty is bodily continuous with his younger self, psychologically recognisable, and able to recall his past. The emigrant woman, despite her outward transformations, retains her psychological character and her memories. The recovering patient returns to his former self, memories intact. From these cases it seems natural to extract the definition that a person P at a later time is identical to a person Q at an earlier time if and only if there is a continuous physical organism linking them, P shares significant psychological characteristics with Q, and P can at least in principle recall experiences had by Q.

The difficulty is that the background cases, while representative of ordinary experience, do not exhaust the possibilities. In each case, the three criteria converge, and so we are not forced to ask which, if any, is dispensable. A case which reveals the insufficiency of the definition arises when the criteria come apart. Consider a man who suffers irreversible and total amnesia. His body is continuous with its former state. His temperament, his values, his intellectual habits, and his emotional dispositions are unchanged — those who know him find him recognisably the same person in every respect save one. He cannot recall a single experience from his prior life, not even in principle, for the neural substrates of his episodic memory have been destroyed. Condition (iii) is violated decisively. Yet it would be perverse to deny that he is the same person. His wife does not consider herself widowed. The law does not release him from his obligations. The definition, induced from cases in which memory was always present, has elevated a common accompaniment to a necessary condition.

background_cases: A woman deliberates at length over whether to accept a job offer, weighs the considerations on both sides, and finally decides to accept. We regard her decision as free because it issued from her own reflective process and she could have chosen otherwise.
A man chooses between two dishes at a restaurant after considering his preferences. We take this to be a free choice, trivial though it is, because nothing constrained him beyond his own tastes.
A citizen votes after careful reflection on the candidates' merits. We regard the vote as a free act because it was reached through deliberation and could have gone the other way.
definition: An agent S acts freely with respect to action A if and only if (i) S deliberates about whether to perform A, (ii) S's deliberation is the proximate cause of A, and (iii) S could, in the same circumstances, have deliberated to a different conclusion and performed a different action.
conditions: S deliberates about whether to perform A
S's deliberation is the proximate cause of A
S could have deliberated to a different conclusion in the same circumstances
counterexample: Consider a man with a severe compulsive disorder who deliberates extensively about whether to check that his front door is locked for the twentieth time. His deliberation is the proximate cause of his checking — he reasons, however irrationally, that it would be unwise not to check. And one might argue that he could, in the same circumstances, have deliberated differently and refrained. All three conditions appear to be met. Yet it seems wrong to describe his checking as a free action; the compulsion drives the deliberation, shaping it from within so that the appearance of rational choice is illusory.
abductive_insight: The background cases all feature agents whose deliberative faculties are functioning normally, and the definition drawn from them equates freedom with the presence of deliberation. What the background cases conceal is the possibility that deliberation itself can be pathologically hijacked, so that its presence is no guarantee of freedom.
passage:
The background cases share a common structure: an agent reflects, weighs considerations, and arrives at a decision that issues in action. The woman deliberates over the job offer; the man weighs his culinary preferences; the citizen reflects on the candidates. In every case, the presence of genuine deliberation is accompanied by our confident judgement that the agent acts freely. One might therefore propose that an agent S acts freely with respect to action A if and only if S deliberates about whether to perform A, S's deliberation is the proximate cause of A, and S could, in the same circumstances, have deliberated to a different conclusion and performed a different action.

This definition captures what is common to the background cases. But the background cases all involve agents whose deliberative faculties are in good order — agents who reason clearly about genuine options and whose mental processes are not distorted by pathological compulsion. The definition, drawn from these healthy cases, identifies freedom with the bare presence of deliberation without inquiring into the quality or autonomy of the deliberative process itself. Consider a man suffering from a severe compulsive disorder. He deliberates at considerable length about whether to check his front door for the twentieth time. His deliberation is the proximate cause of his subsequent checking. And one might maintain that he could, in some abstract sense, have deliberated differently. All three conditions appear to be satisfied. Yet we should hesitate to call his action free. The compulsion does not bypass his deliberation — it corrupts it from within, ensuring that the deliberative process, however elaborate, will arrive at a predetermined conclusion. The definition, having been abstracted from cases of healthy deliberation, lacks the resources to distinguish genuine from pathologically compromised reasoning.

background_cases: When a person withdraws her hand from a hot stove, we ascribe consciousness to her because her behaviour is accompanied by a felt sensation of pain — an inner qualitative state that she can report and reflect upon.

When a dog yelps and recoils from a sharp object, we ascribe some form of consciousness to it because its behaviour resembles our own pain behaviour and because we suppose it to be accompanied by a felt quality, however rudimentary.

When an infant cries upon being pricked by a needle, we ascribe consciousness despite the infant's inability to articulate its experience, because the behavioural indicators — crying, withdrawal, facial contortion — suggest the presence of an inner qualitative state.
definition: A system S is conscious if and only if (i) S receives sensory input from its environment, (ii) S processes that input in a manner that produces differential behavioural responses to different stimuli, and (iii) S is capable of learning — of modifying its future responses on the basis of past input.
conditions: S receives sensory input from its environment
S processes input to produce differential behavioural responses
S is capable of learning from past input
counterexample: Consider a sophisticated thermostat connected to a network of temperature sensors throughout a building. It receives sensory input — temperature readings from dozens of locations. It processes this input differentially, producing different responses (adjusting heating, cooling, ventilation) for different patterns of temperature. And it is capable of learning: a modern adaptive thermostat modifies its future responses on the basis of past patterns, anticipating the building's thermal behaviour. All three conditions are met. Yet one would hesitate to ascribe consciousness to a thermostat, however adaptive. It lacks the inner qualitative dimension — the felt character of experience — that the background cases all share.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involve systems whose behavioural responses are accompanied by an inner qualitative dimension — a felt experience that the subject can, at least in principle, report or reflect upon. The definition captures the functional profile common to the background cases — sensory reception, differential processing, learning — but omits the qualitative dimension that distinguishes conscious experience from mere information processing.
passage:
When we examine the cases in which we are most confident in ascribing consciousness, a pattern emerges. The person who withdraws from the hot stove, the dog that recoils from a sharp object, the infant that cries upon being pricked — in each case, the system receives sensory input, processes it differentially, and is capable of modifying its behaviour in the light of experience. One might be tempted, on the strength of these cases, to define consciousness as the conjunction of sensory reception, differential processing, and learning capacity. This definition has the considerable advantage of being empirically applicable: one can, in principle, determine whether any given system satisfies the three conditions.

Yet the definition, though it captures the functional profile shared by the background cases, omits what is arguably the most important feature they have in common. The person at the stove does not merely process thermal information and withdraw her hand; she feels the pain. The dog does not merely detect the sharp object and recoil; it undergoes something — a qualitative state, however rudimentary. The infant's cry is not merely a behavioural output but the expression of a felt distress. In each case, the behaviour is accompanied by an inner qualitative dimension — a 'what it is like' to undergo the experience. Now consider a sophisticated adaptive thermostat that monitors temperature throughout a building, produces differential responses, and learns to anticipate the building's thermal patterns. All three conditions of the definition are satisfied. But one would hesitate to say that the thermostat is conscious. It processes information and modifies its behaviour, but there is, we suppose, nothing it is like to be a thermostat. The definition has identified the functional features that are typically associated with consciousness but has missed the qualitative character that distinguishes genuine experience from mere computation.

background_cases: When a chess player deliberates for several minutes and then moves her queen to a particular square, we regard her action as intentional because she considered alternatives, selected a move in light of her strategic goals, and executed the move she chose.

When a carpenter measures a plank, marks it, and saws along the mark, we regard the cutting as intentional because each step was guided by the carpenter's plan and executed under his continuous monitoring.

When a speaker pauses to find the right word, selects it, and incorporates it into her sentence, we regard the word choice as intentional because it was the product of deliberate selection from among alternatives.
definition: An agent A performs an action X intentionally if and only if (i) A has a goal that X is meant to achieve, (ii) A believes that performing X will contribute to achieving that goal, and (iii) A performs X as a result of this goal and this belief.
conditions: A has a goal that X is meant to achieve
A believes X will contribute to achieving the goal
A performs X as a result of the goal and belief
counterexample: Consider a tennis player who, in the midst of a rally, executes a brilliantly placed backhand return. She had the goal of winning the point; she believed, in some sense, that returning the ball would contribute to that goal; and she performed the return as a result of her competitive aim. All three conditions are met. Yet the player, when asked afterwards how she placed the shot so precisely, cannot explain her action. She did not deliberate among alternatives, did not form a plan for that particular stroke, did not monitor its execution step by step. The shot was, as athletes say, instinctive — produced by trained reflexes operating below the level of conscious deliberation. The background cases all involved actions guided by explicit deliberation and step-by-step monitoring. The tennis return satisfies the definition's conditions but lacks the deliberative character that made the background cases paradigmatic instances of intentional action.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involve actions produced through explicit deliberation — the chess player considers alternatives, the carpenter follows a plan step by step, the speaker pauses to select. The definition extracts the goal-belief-action structure common to these cases but drops the requirement that the action be guided by conscious deliberation, thereby admitting skilled, reflexive actions that meet the structural conditions without exhibiting the deliberative process that characterised the original cases.
passage:
The cases in which we most readily ascribe intentionality share a common structure. The chess player who deliberates before moving her queen, the carpenter who measures and marks before cutting, the speaker who pauses to find the right word — in each case, the agent has a goal, believes that a particular action will advance that goal, and performs the action as a result of this goal-belief pair. One might therefore propose that an action is intentional if and only if it is performed in pursuit of a goal, the agent believes the action will serve that goal, and the action results from this combination of goal and belief.

Yet one might observe that this definition, though it captures the structural features of the background cases, neglects a feature they share that is not reflected in the conditions. In each background case, the agent engages in explicit deliberation: the chess player considers alternatives, the carpenter monitors each step, the speaker weighs candidate words. Now consider a tennis player who, in the heat of a rally, executes a brilliantly placed backhand return. She had the goal of winning the point, she believed that returning the ball would serve that goal, and she acted as a result. All three conditions are met. But the shot was not the product of deliberation. It was executed by trained reflexes, faster than conscious thought could guide. The player herself cannot explain how she placed the ball so precisely. The definition admits this case as intentional action, yet it differs from the background cases in a respect that seems important: the absence of the deliberative process by which alternatives are consciously weighed and a course of action is explicitly chosen. The definition, it appears, has captured the skeleton of intentional action — the goal-belief-action structure — while discarding the deliberative flesh that gave the background cases their distinctive character.

background_cases: When a mother sees her child in danger, she experiences a surge of fear that includes a racing heart, a tightening of the chest, and an overwhelming urge to intervene — the fear is directed at the specific danger and motivates protective action.

When a man discovers that a trusted friend has betrayed him, he experiences anger that includes a flush of heat, a clenching of the jaw, and a desire to confront the betrayer — the anger is directed at the betrayal and motivates confrontation.

When a student receives news of an unexpected academic success, she experiences joy that includes a lightness in the chest, a smile she cannot suppress, and an urge to share the news — the joy is directed at the achievement and motivates celebration.
definition: A mental state M is an emotion if and only if (i) M has a specific intentional object — it is directed at some person, event, or state of affairs, (ii) M involves a characteristic pattern of bodily arousal, and (iii) M motivates the subject toward a characteristic type of action.
conditions: M has a specific intentional object
M involves characteristic bodily arousal
M motivates toward a characteristic type of action
counterexample: Consider the pervasive sense of melancholy that sometimes descends upon a person for no identifiable reason — the diffuse sadness that colours an entire afternoon without being directed at any particular object, event, or state of affairs. The subject feels a heaviness in her limbs, a constriction in her throat, and a disinclination toward activity. The bodily arousal is present; the motivational effect — withdrawal, passivity — is present. But the state lacks a specific intentional object. The subject cannot say what she is sad about; the melancholy is undirected, free-floating. Condition (i) is not met. Yet the state is recognisably an emotion — it is sadness, felt as such by the subject and recognisable as such by others.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involve emotions that are directed at specific objects — the child's danger, the friend's betrayal, the academic success. The definition generalises from these cases to require a specific intentional object as a necessary condition. But moods and diffuse emotional states, which are recognised as genuine emotions by those who experience them, often lack any identifiable intentional object, revealing that the background cases were not representative of the full range of emotional phenomena.
passage:
The paradigmatic cases of emotion share a striking structural similarity. The mother's fear for her child, the man's anger at a betrayal, the student's joy at a success — each involves a state directed at a specific object, accompanied by characteristic bodily arousal, and motivating a characteristic type of action. Fear is directed at danger and motivates flight or protection; anger is directed at an offence and motivates confrontation; joy is directed at a good and motivates celebration. From these cases, one might extract the following definition: a mental state is an emotion if and only if it has a specific intentional object, involves characteristic bodily arousal, and motivates toward a characteristic type of action.

Yet this definition, though faithful to the background cases, encounters a difficulty with a class of emotional states that are familiar to everyone. Consider the diffuse melancholy that sometimes settles upon a person without apparent cause — the sadness that colours an entire afternoon, making the world seem grey and burdensome, without being directed at any particular event or circumstance. The subject feels a heaviness in her body, a constriction in her throat, a disinclination to engage with the world. The bodily arousal is present; the motivational effect — withdrawal, lethargy — is present. But the state lacks any identifiable intentional object. The subject, if asked what she is sad about, can offer no answer; the melancholy is undirected, pervasive, objectless. The definition, by requiring a specific intentional object, excludes this state from the category of emotion. But the state is manifestly an emotion — it is experienced as sadness, recognised as such by others, and has all the phenomenal and motivational characteristics of sadness save the intentional directedness. The background cases, it appears, were not representative of the full range of emotional experience; they were drawn exclusively from emotions with clear objects, and the definition inherits this limitation.

background_cases: When a person looks at a red apple in good light and judges it to be red, her perceptual experience is veridical: the apple is red, her visual system accurately represents its colour, and the experience is caused by the apple through the normal operation of her eyes.

When a birdwatcher hears the distinctive call of a nightingale and identifies the bird by its song, his auditory experience accurately represents the sound, the sound is caused by the bird, and the identification is correct.
When a cook tastes a dish and judges that it contains too much salt, her gustatory experience accurately represents the flavour, the experience is caused by the dish, and the judgement is correct.
definition: A subject S has a veridical perception of a property F belonging to an object O if and only if (i) O has the property F, (ii) S has a sensory experience that represents O as having F, and (iii) S's experience is caused by O's having F.
conditions: O has the property F
S has a sensory experience representing O as having F
The experience is caused by O's having F
counterexample: Consider a man who looks at a white wall that is illuminated by a red light. He has a sensory experience of the wall as red. Suppose that, unbeknownst to him, someone has painted the wall red moments before, so that the wall is, in fact, red. The wall has the property of being red; his experience represents the wall as red; and his experience is caused by the wall — specifically, by the light reflected from it. All three conditions appear to be met. But his experience of the wall as red is not caused by the wall's being red; it is caused by the red illumination falling upon what he takes to be a white wall. The wall's redness and his experience of redness coincide by accident. His perception is veridical in content but not in the manner required for genuine perceptual contact with the property in question.
abductive_insight: In the background cases, the causal chain runs from the property itself — the apple's redness, the nightingale's call, the dish's saltiness — through the appropriate sensory channel to the subject's experience. The definition requires that the experience be caused by the object's having the property, but it does not require that the causal chain run through the right property. The red-wall case shows that the experience can be caused by the object while the match between experienced property and actual property is coincidental, sustained by an irrelevant intermediary.
passage:
The cases in which we are most confident that perception is veridical share a common structure. The person who sees the red apple, the birdwatcher who hears the nightingale, the cook who tastes the salt — in each case, the object has the property attributed to it, the subject's experience represents the object as having that property, and the experience is caused by the object's possession of the property. One might therefore define veridical perception as requiring that the object have the relevant property, that the experience represent it as having the property, and that the experience be caused by the object's having the property.

Yet one might observe that this definition, though it fits the background cases, is vulnerable to a class of cases in which the three conditions are satisfied but the perception is not genuinely veridical in the way the background cases exemplify. Consider a man who looks at a wall illuminated by a red light. He sees the wall as red. Suppose that the wall has, in fact, just been painted red — though the paint is invisible under the red illumination, which makes the wall look exactly as it would if it were white and lit by red light. The wall has the property of being red; his experience represents it as red; and his experience is caused by the wall, via reflected light. All three conditions are met. But his experience of redness is caused by the red illumination, not by the red paint. The fact that the wall is actually red is entirely irrelevant to his visual experience; had the wall been white, his experience would have been identical. The causal chain runs through the wrong property. In the background cases, the causal chain ran from the very property perceived — the apple's redness, the bird's call, the dish's saltiness — to the experience. The definition captures the requirement that the experience be caused by the object, but it does not ensure that the causal chain passes through the specific property that the experience represents.

background_cases: A man who is awake and engaged in conversation exhibits consciousness: he reports his experiences, adjusts his behaviour in response to subtle changes in his interlocutor's tone, and can reflect upon what he is doing while he is doing it. His consciousness is manifest in the integration of perception, reflection, and report.

A patient under general anaesthesia exhibits the absence of consciousness: she does not respond to speech, does not adjust her behaviour, and upon waking reports no experience of the intervening period. The anaesthesia has extinguished the integrative capacity that characterises the waking state.

A sleeping person who is dreaming occupies an intermediate state: she has experiences — vivid, structured, emotionally charged — but cannot report them in real time, does not adjust her behaviour to her actual environment, and cannot reflect upon her dreaming while it occurs. Integration is partial.

A person in a persistent vegetative state shows sleep-wake cycles and reflexive responses but no evidence of awareness, integration, or reportable experience, despite the continued functioning of brainstem mechanisms.
definition: A creature C is conscious if and only if (i) C processes information from its environment, (ii) C generates behavioural responses to that information, and (iii) C is capable of verbal or gestural report of its internal states.
conditions: C processes information from its environment
C generates behavioural responses to that information
C is capable of verbal or gestural report of its internal states
counterexample: Consider a patient suffering from what neurologists call blindsight. Following damage to the primary visual cortex, the patient sincerely reports that she cannot see anything in a portion of her visual field. Yet when forced to guess the location or orientation of objects presented in that region, she performs far above chance. She processes visual information and generates accurate behavioural responses, but she cannot report seeing anything — she denies any visual experience whatsoever. The first two conditions are met, but the third is not, at least with respect to the visual modality in question.
abductive_insight: The background cases suggest that consciousness involves not merely the processing of information and the generation of responses, but a distinctive integration of these processes with the capacity for reflective awareness. Blindsight reveals that information processing and accurate responding can occur in the complete absence of reportable awareness, indicating that the definition's three conditions are jointly insufficient — one may process, respond, and yet lack the phenomenal dimension that the background cases associate with consciousness.
passage:
When one examines the cases in which consciousness is most confidently attributed, a pattern emerges. The waking conversationalist processes information from his environment, responds to it with exquisite sensitivity, and can report what he experiences while he experiences it. His consciousness is manifest in the integration of these capacities. The patient under anaesthesia lacks all three: she processes nothing, responds to nothing, reports nothing. The dreamer possesses experience but lacks the capacity for concurrent report and environmental responsiveness. The vegetative patient retains reflexive processing but no reportable awareness. These cases suggest that consciousness requires not merely information processing and behavioural response, but an integrative awareness that binds the two together.

One might attempt to codify these observations by defining consciousness as the conjunction of environmental information processing, behavioural responsiveness, and the capacity for report. This definition appears to distinguish the conscious from the unconscious with tolerable accuracy. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency is provided by the neurological phenomenon of blindsight. A patient with damage to the primary visual cortex reports, with evident sincerity, that she sees nothing in a portion of her visual field. She is not being evasive or inattentive; she genuinely experiences no visual awareness in that region. Yet when experimenters present stimuli in the affected field and ask her to guess their properties — their location, their orientation, their shape — she performs with remarkable accuracy, far exceeding what chance would predict. She processes visual information from her environment. She generates accurate behavioural responses. But she cannot report seeing anything; from her own perspective, there is nothing to report. The first two conditions of the definition are satisfied with respect to the visual stimuli, but the third is not. The definition would compel us to deny that anything interesting is occurring — the creature processes and responds but does not report, and so is not conscious with respect to the stimuli. Yet what the background cases reveal is something the definition fails to capture: that the mere conjunction of processing and response does not constitute consciousness. What is required is a phenomenal dimension — an experiential awareness — that blindsight demonstrates can be absent even when processing and response are intact.

background_cases: A woman decides to learn Italian and enrols in a course. Her intention is formed through deliberation, persists over time, guides her actions — purchasing textbooks, attending classes — and can be cited as her reason for those actions. The intention plays a governing role in organising a sequence of subsidiary actions.

A man resolves to give up smoking. His intention is the product of reflection on his health, persists through temptation, and governs his behaviour — he avoids situations in which he might be tempted, declines offered cigarettes, and purchases substitutes. The intention exerts a controlling influence over his conduct.

A chess player forms the intention to execute a particular combination of moves. The intention is specific, governs a temporally extended sequence of actions, and can be revised if the opponent's response renders it impractical. The intention is flexible yet directive.
definition: An agent A intends to do X if and only if (i) A has formed a plan to bring about X, (ii) A's plan is directed toward X as its goal, and (iii) A has begun to execute the plan or is committed to executing it at an appropriate time.
conditions: A has formed a plan to bring about X
A's plan is directed toward X as its goal
A has begun or is committed to executing the plan
counterexample: Consider a man who is walking across a room to fetch a book. In the course of walking, he steps over a rug. He did not form a plan to step over the rug; stepping over the rug was not the goal of any plan; he was not committed to stepping over the rug at an appropriate time. Yet his stepping over the rug was not a mere reflex — he adjusted his gait, raised his foot appropriately, and would have acted differently had the rug not been there. It appears that he intended to step over the rug, yet none of the three conditions is satisfied.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involve intentions that are formed in advance, directed toward an explicit goal, and that govern a temporally extended sequence of actions. The definition captures this paradigm of prior, plan-based intention but fails to accommodate the intentions that are formed and executed in the very course of action — what one might call intentions-in-action — where no prior plan exists and the goal is not explicitly represented before the action begins.
passage:
The background cases present a picture of intention as a prior mental state that governs conduct. The woman who resolves to learn Italian forms a plan, pursues a goal, and organises a series of actions around that plan. The man who decides to stop smoking likewise forms a resolution that persists through time and controls his behaviour. The chess player forms a specific intention that guides a sequence of moves. In each case, the intention is formed before the actions it governs, is explicitly directed toward a goal, and exerts a controlling influence over what the agent does.

One might codify these observations by defining intention as the formation of a plan directed toward a goal, accompanied by commitment to execution. This definition captures the structure of the paradigm cases admirably. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises in the course of ordinary, unreflective action. Consider a man walking across a room to fetch a book from a shelf. In the course of walking, he encounters a rug and steps over it. His stepping over the rug is not a reflex — he adjusts his gait, raises his foot to the appropriate height, and navigates the obstacle with practised ease. Had the rug not been there, he would not have raised his foot. It appears that he intended to step over the rug. Yet he formed no prior plan to do so; stepping over the rug was not the goal of any plan; and he was not committed, in advance, to stepping over it at an appropriate time. The intention, if that is what it is, was formed and executed in the very course of action, without prior deliberation or explicit goal-setting. The definition, built from cases of prior, plan-based intention, cannot accommodate this species of intention-in-action. What the background cases reveal, and what the definition fails to preserve, is the distinction between two kinds of intention: the prior intention that governs a planned sequence of actions and the intention that arises spontaneously in the flow of unreflective conduct.

background_cases: A man's anger upon discovering he has been betrayed by a trusted friend is directed at the friend, involves a characteristic feeling of heated agitation, and includes an appraisal of the friend's action as a wrong done to him. The anger makes sense in light of the appraisal — it is intelligible as a response to perceived betrayal.

A woman's grief upon the death of her mother is directed at the loss, involves a characteristic feeling of heaviness and desolation, and includes an appraisal of the death as a profound personal loss. The grief is proportionate to the significance of what has been lost.

A child's delight upon receiving a longed-for gift is directed at the gift, involves characteristic feelings of elation and excitement, and includes an appraisal of the situation as one in which something good has happened to him. The delight is intelligible as a response to the fulfilment of desire.
definition: A mental state S of an agent A is an emotion if and only if (i) S involves an appraisal of a situation as having significance for A's concerns, (ii) S is accompanied by a characteristic bodily feeling, and (iii) S is directed at an intentional object — a person, event, or state of affairs.
conditions: S involves an appraisal of a situation as significant for A's concerns
S is accompanied by a characteristic bodily feeling
S is directed at an intentional object
counterexample: Consider a man who wakes one morning pervaded by a deep and inexplicable sadness. He has suffered no loss, received no bad news, and can identify nothing in his circumstances to account for the feeling. The sadness is not directed at anything — there is no object of his grief, no event he mourns, no person he misses. He simply feels sad. The state involves a characteristic bodily feeling — a heaviness in the chest, a lassitude in the limbs — and one might stretch to say it involves a vague appraisal of the world as bleak. But it lacks an intentional object. The third condition is not met, yet the man would without hesitation describe his state as one of sadness, and it would be strange to deny that he is experiencing an emotion.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involve emotions with clearly identifiable objects — the betraying friend, the dead mother, the received gift. The definition captures this object-directed character as a necessary condition.
