Yet the case of objectless sadness reveals that emotions may occur without being directed at any identifiable intentional object, suggesting that the background cases represent a privileged but not exhaustive subclass of emotional experience.
passage:
The emotions that are most readily analysed share a common structure. Anger at betrayal is directed at the betrayer and involves an appraisal of his conduct as a wrong. Grief over a mother's death is directed at the loss and involves an appraisal of its gravity. A child's delight at a gift is directed at the gift and involves an appraisal of the situation as fortunate. In each case, the emotion has a clear intentional object — something at which it is directed — and the emotion is intelligible in light of the appraisal that accompanies it.

One might therefore define an emotion as a mental state involving an appraisal of significance, a characteristic bodily feeling, and directedness at an intentional object. This definition captures the structure of the paradigm cases with precision. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency is provided by the experience of objectless mood. Consider a man who wakes one morning pervaded by a deep and unaccountable sadness. He has received no bad news; he has suffered no loss; he can point to nothing in his life that warrants the feeling. The sadness is not directed at any identifiable object — he is not sad about anything in particular. He simply feels sad. The state is accompanied by a characteristic bodily feeling: a heaviness in the chest, a lethargy in the limbs, a disinclination to engage with the world. One might attempt to locate an appraisal — a vague sense that things are bleak — but there is no intentional object to anchor the appraisal. The third condition of the definition is not satisfied. Yet the man would describe his state, without the slightest hesitation, as sadness, and anyone observing him would concur. The background cases, in which emotions are always directed at identifiable objects, represent a central but not exhaustive class of emotional experience. The definition, built from these cases, mistakes a frequent feature of emotions for a necessary one.

background_cases: A woman sees a red apple on the table before her. Her visual experience is caused by the apple, corresponds to its properties — its colour, shape, and location — and she is in a position to act upon it: she can reach out and grasp it. The perception connects her to the world and enables purposive interaction with it.

A man hears a church bell tolling in the distance. His auditory experience is caused by the bell, corresponds to its properties — its pitch, timbre, and direction — and puts him in a position to orient toward the sound or move away from it. Perception again connects the perceiver to the source.

A child feels the warmth of a radiator against her hand. Her tactile experience is caused by the radiator, corresponds to the radiator's thermal property, and enables her to withdraw if the heat becomes excessive. Perception serves as a channel of information connecting agent to environment.
definition: An agent A perceives an object O if and only if (i) O causally produces a sensory experience in A, (ii) A's experience corresponds in relevant respects to O's actual properties, and (iii) the causal process linking O to A's experience is of the appropriate type — it proceeds through the normal sensory channels.
conditions: O causally produces a sensory experience in A
A's experience corresponds to O's actual properties
The causal process proceeds through normal sensory channels
counterexample: Consider a neurosurgeon who, during an operation on a patient's visual cortex, stimulates a particular region of the brain. The patient reports seeing a bright flash of light. No light is present; the experience is caused entirely by the electrical stimulation. Yet suppose, by extraordinary coincidence, a lightning bolt strikes outside the hospital window at the very moment the surgeon applies the stimulus. The patient's experience of a bright flash corresponds, in its qualitative character, to the actual flash of lightning. The lightning causally contributes to the environment, though it does not cause the patient's experience. The first condition is met — the lightning exists and the patient has an experience — but the causal process does not proceed through the normal sensory channels; it proceeds through the surgeon's electrode. The third condition is not satisfied. Yet the patient's experience corresponds to what is actually occurring outside the window.
abductive_insight: The background cases all exhibit a tight coupling between the object, the experience, and the causal pathway: the apple causes the visual experience through the eyes, the bell causes the auditory experience through the ears. The definition captures this coupling but is vulnerable to cases of deviant causal chains, where the correspondence between experience and reality is coincidental rather than the product of the normal perceptual process. The background cases succeed as perception precisely because the correspondence is non-accidental, and the definition provides no means of ruling out accidental correspondences.
passage:
When one surveys the paradigmatic cases of perception, a regular structure presents itself. The woman who sees the apple, the man who hears the bell, the child who feels the radiator — in each case, an object in the environment causally produces an experience that corresponds to the object's properties, and the causal process runs through the normal sensory channels: light through the eyes, sound through the ears, heat through the skin. The correspondence between experience and object is not accidental; it is sustained by a reliable causal mechanism.

One might codify this by defining perception as the causal production of a sensory experience that corresponds to the object's properties through the normal sensory channels. This definition appears to capture the essential features of the paradigm cases. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises when the correspondence between experience and reality is genuine but causally deviant. Suppose a neurosurgeon, operating upon a patient's visual cortex, applies an electrical stimulus that produces in the patient the experience of a bright flash of light. At that very moment, by a coincidence too extraordinary to be anticipated, a bolt of lightning strikes outside the hospital window. The patient's experience corresponds in its qualitative character to the actual state of affairs — there is indeed a bright flash of light outside. But the experience was not caused by the lightning through the normal visual pathway; it was caused by the surgeon's electrode. The definition's conditions are partially met: the lightning exists, the experience corresponds to it, but the causal process is of the wrong type. What the background cases possessed, and what the definition fails adequately to secure, is a requirement that the correspondence between experience and reality be non-accidental — that it be produced by the very causal pathway that connects the perceived object to the perceiver's sensory apparatus. The definition leaves open the possibility that a coincidental correspondence, produced by a deviant cause, might be mistaken for genuine perception.

background_cases: When a person sees a vivid red sunset, the conscious state is characterised not only by the presence of a visual image but by an evaluative dimension — the subject finds the scene beautiful, or calming, or striking — and this evaluative colouring is inseparable from the experience itself; the perception is not neutral but imbued with significance.

When a person tastes an unfamiliar spice for the first time, the conscious experience includes not merely a gustatory sensation but an affective response — pleasure, curiosity, or mild aversion — that is woven into the experience and shapes the subject's subsequent behaviour towards the spice.

When a person hears a piece of music that she associates with a period of her youth, the conscious state includes not merely an auditory perception but a complex affective resonance — nostalgia, warmth, a sense of time passing — that transforms the mere hearing into a richly layered experience.

When a person feels a sudden sharp pain, the conscious state includes not only the sensory quality of the pain but an immediate aversive evaluation — the pain is bad, to be avoided — and this evaluative component is not a separate judgement added to the sensation but is intrinsic to the painful experience itself.
definition: A mental state M is conscious if and only if (i) M has a qualitative character — there is something it is like for the subject to be in M, (ii) M is accessible to the subject's introspective awareness, and (iii) M has representational content — it represents some feature of the world or of the subject's own body.
conditions: M has qualitative character
M is accessible to introspective awareness
M has representational content
counterexample: Consider a state of diffuse anxiety that arises without any identifiable object. The subject feels uneasy, agitated, and restless. The state has a definite qualitative character — there is something it is like to feel anxious — and it is accessible to introspection. But condition (iii) is problematic: the anxiety does not represent any specific feature of the world or of the subject's body. The subject cannot say what she is anxious about; the state is objectless. Yet the state is plainly conscious — it dominates the subject's experience and influences her behaviour profoundly.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involve conscious states that possess an evaluative or affective dimension — the sunset is experienced as beautiful, the spice as pleasant or unpleasant, the music as nostalgic, the pain as aversive. The definition captures the qualitative, introspective, and representational features of these states but omits the evaluative dimension that is present in every background case and that may constitute a more fundamental feature of consciousness than representational content.
passage:
When one examines the conscious states that serve as the most familiar examples in philosophical psychology, a striking feature emerges that is not captured by the standard analyses. Seeing a vivid sunset is not merely having a visual image with qualitative character; it is finding the scene beautiful or striking, and this evaluative colouring is intrinsic to the experience. Tasting an unfamiliar spice is not merely registering a gustatory quality; it is experiencing pleasure or curiosity or mild aversion, and this affective response is woven into the sensation itself. Hearing music associated with one's youth is not merely perceiving sounds; it is being suffused with nostalgia, warmth, and a sense of temporal distance. Feeling a sudden pain is not merely experiencing a sensory quality; it is experiencing something bad, something to be avoided. In each case, the conscious state possesses an evaluative or affective dimension that is inseparable from the experience.

One might attempt to codify the essential features of consciousness by defining a conscious state as one that has qualitative character, is accessible to introspective awareness, and possesses representational content. This definition captures three features that are indeed present in the paradigm cases. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency is the state of diffuse, objectless anxiety. A subject may feel profoundly uneasy — agitated, restless, apprehensive — without being able to identify any object of her anxiety. The state has a vivid qualitative character and is fully accessible to introspection. But it lacks representational content in the ordinary sense: it does not represent any feature of the world or of the subject's body. It is anxiety about nothing in particular. Yet the state is undeniably conscious — it pervades the subject's experience and governs her behaviour. What the background cases all possess, and what the definition omits, is the evaluative dimension — the fact that conscious states are characteristically experienced as good or bad, pleasant or unpleasant, attractive or aversive. This evaluative colouring may be a more fundamental feature of consciousness than representational content, present even in states that lack a determinate object.

background_cases: A chess player who deliberates over her next move, weighing the merits of a kingside attack against a queenside defence, and finally settles on a plan after sustained reflection, is regarded as acting freely because her action flows from her own reasoning — she considered alternatives, evaluated them, and chose on the basis of considerations she endorses.

A voter who reads the party manifestos, attends public debates, and ultimately casts her ballot for the candidate whose policies she judges most sound is regarded as acting freely because her choice is the product of her own informed deliberation rather than coercion, habit, or unreflective impulse.

A scientist who reviews the experimental evidence, considers rival hypotheses, and adopts the theory best supported by the data is regarded as acting freely in her theoretical commitment because the commitment flows from her own assessment of the evidence rather than from dogma, authority, or professional pressure.
definition: A person P acts freely in performing an action A if and only if (i) P could have done otherwise — that is, it was within P's power to refrain from A or to perform a different action, (ii) P's performing A was not compelled by any external force, and (iii) P chose to perform A on the basis of P's own reasons.
conditions: P could have done otherwise
P's action was not externally compelled
P chose A on the basis of P's own reasons
counterexample: Consider a recovering alcoholic who, after months of sobriety, encounters a bottle of whisky at a friend's house. He experiences an overwhelming craving. He deliberates: he considers the consequences of drinking, he recalls the suffering his addiction has caused, and he decides, on the basis of his own reasons, not to drink. He is not externally compelled — no one forces whisky upon him, and no one physically restrains him. He acts on his own reasons. But one might ask whether he could genuinely have done otherwise. The craving is so powerful that, had his resolve wavered even slightly, he would have drunk. His capacity to refrain is real but fragile — it depends on the full deployment of every resource of self-control he possesses. Condition (i) is satisfied only in the most attenuated sense, yet his refraining is precisely the kind of action we most want to call free — indeed, it is a paradigm of the exercise of freedom against powerful internal opposition.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involve agents whose freedom consists not merely in the abstract capacity to do otherwise but in the quality of the deliberative process by which they arrive at their decision — the chess player's sustained reflection, the voter's informed consideration, the scientist's assessment of evidence. What makes these actions free is the character of the reasoning, not the bare metaphysical possibility of alternative action. The definition captures the metaphysical condition but omits the deliberative quality that the background cases reveal to be central.
passage:
The actions most securely regarded as free share a feature that deserves closer attention than the standard analyses typically afford it. A chess player who deliberates over her move, weighing alternatives and choosing on the basis of strategic considerations she endorses, is regarded as acting freely not merely because she could have moved differently but because her choice flows from sustained, high-quality reasoning. A voter who studies the candidates and casts an informed ballot is free not merely because she could have voted otherwise but because her vote reflects her own considered judgement. A scientist who adopts a theory on the strength of the evidence is free in her commitment not merely because she could have adopted a rival theory but because her adoption flows from a careful assessment of the data.

One might codify these cases by defining free action as action that the agent could have refrained from, that was not externally compelled, and that was chosen on the basis of the agent's own reasons. This definition captures the elements of alternative possibility, absence of compulsion, and rational self-governance that the paradigm cases exhibit. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency is that of the recovering alcoholic who resists a powerful craving. He encounters a bottle of whisky and experiences an overwhelming desire to drink. He deliberates carefully: he recalls the devastation his addiction has wrought, he considers the consequences of relapse, and he decides, on the basis of these reasons, to refrain. No external force compels him either way. He acts on his own reasons, and his refraining is a triumph of reflective self-governance. But could he genuinely have done otherwise? The craving is so intense that his capacity to refrain depends on the full and precarious deployment of every resource of self-control. Had his attention lapsed for a moment, he would have drunk. The alternative possibility is real but gossamer-thin. Yet his refraining is, if anything, a more impressive exercise of freedom than the chess player's move or the voter's ballot, precisely because it is won against such powerful internal opposition. The background cases suggest that what constitutes free action is the quality of the deliberative process — the thoughtfulness, the weighing of reasons, the reflective endorsement — rather than the bare metaphysical capacity to do otherwise.

background_cases: A mother's fear upon seeing her child run towards a busy road is characterised not merely by a felt bodily disturbance — the racing heart, the surge of adrenaline — but by a structured appraisal of the situation: she perceives the child as being in danger, evaluates the danger as serious, and her emotional response is proportioned to the gravity of the threat as she appraises it.

A student's shame upon being caught cheating in an examination is characterised not merely by a bodily sensation — the flush, the desire to hide — but by a complex evaluative judgement: he appraises his own action as a violation of a standard he endorses, and his shame is proportioned to the seriousness with which he takes that standard.

An art collector's joy upon discovering an unknown masterpiece is characterised not by any particular bodily sensation but by an evaluative apprehension of the painting's qualities — its composition, its colour, its originality — and her joy is proportioned to her assessment of the work's merit.

A mountaineer's awe upon reaching a summit and surveying the landscape below involves a complex appraisal of the scene — its vastness, its beauty, the achievement of having reached it — and the emotion is shaped by and proportioned to this appraisal rather than to any particular bodily sensation.
definition: A mental state M is an emotion if and only if (i) M involves a felt bodily disturbance, (ii) M is directed towards an intentional object — a person, situation, or state of affairs, and (iii) M motivates the subject towards characteristic patterns of action.
conditions: M involves a felt bodily disturbance
M is directed towards an intentional object
M motivates characteristic action
counterexample: Consider a person who learns, through careful study of a complex financial document, that a corporation has been systematically defrauding its employees of their pensions. She feels moral indignation. Her indignation is directed at the corporation and its officers — it has a clear intentional object. It motivates her to write to her member of parliament and to the newspapers. But the indignation, as she experiences it, involves little in the way of felt bodily disturbance. She is not trembling, her heart is not racing, she feels no heat or tension. Her state is calm, focused, and intellectual — a settled moral assessment rather than a visceral upheaval. Condition (i) is not satisfied, yet her indignation is plainly an emotion, and a morally important one.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involve emotions whose character is shaped by an evaluative appraisal — the mother appraises the situation as dangerous, the student appraises his action as a violation of a standard, the collector appraises the painting as meritorious, the mountaineer appraises the landscape as sublime. The definition captures the bodily, intentional, and motivational features of emotion but omits the evaluative appraisal that structures the emotion and determines its specific character. It is the appraisal, not the bodily disturbance, that distinguishes fear from anger, shame from embarrassment, joy from relief.
passage:
When one attends to the emotions most readily analysed by philosophical psychology, a structural feature emerges that the standard accounts have not always made explicit. A mother's fear for her child running towards traffic is not a raw bodily disturbance accompanied by a perception; it is structured by an appraisal — she perceives the child as endangered, evaluates the danger as serious, and her fear is proportioned to the gravity of the threat. A student's shame at being caught cheating is structured by an evaluative judgement — he appraises his action as a violation of a standard he endorses, and his shame is proportioned to the seriousness of the violation. An art collector's joy at discovering a masterpiece is structured by an aesthetic evaluation — she appraises the painting's composition, colour, and originality, and her joy is proportioned to the work's merit. A mountaineer's awe at a summit vista is shaped by an appraisal of vastness, beauty, and personal achievement. In each case, the emotion is not merely a feeling accompanied by a perception; it is an evaluative response whose specific character — fear rather than anger, shame rather than guilt, joy rather than relief — is determined by the appraisal.

One might define an emotion as a state involving a felt bodily disturbance, directed at an intentional object, and motivating characteristic action. This definition captures three genuine features of the paradigm cases. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency is that of calm moral indignation. A person studies a financial document and discovers that a corporation has been defrauding its employees of their pensions. She feels indignation — directed at the corporation, motivating her to seek redress. But her indignation is not accompanied by any salient bodily disturbance. She is calm, focused, and deliberate. Her state is an evaluative response to a moral violation, not a visceral upheaval. Condition (i) fails. Yet her indignation is plainly an emotion — indeed, it is precisely the kind of dispassionate moral emotion that one might regard as most admirable. The background cases reveal that what gives an emotion its specific identity is the evaluative appraisal, not the bodily accompaniment, and the definition, by requiring a bodily disturbance, mistakes a frequent accompaniment for a necessary condition.

background_cases: A gambler who has lost repeatedly at the races yet persists in believing that he has a reliable system for picking winners is self-deceived. He possesses abundant evidence — his mounting losses — that his system does not work, yet he maintains his belief by attending selectively to the occasions on which he won and dismissing his losses as the result of bad luck or exceptional circumstances.

A parent who refuses to acknowledge that her child is struggling at school, despite clear reports from teachers and declining marks, is self-deceived. She possesses the evidence but reinterprets it — the teachers are biased, the examinations are unfair — so as to preserve her belief in the child's ability.

A politician who genuinely believes in his own integrity despite having accepted favours from lobbyists is self-deceived. He possesses the evidence of the favours but construes them as normal courtesies, thereby preserving his self-image as an honest public servant.
definition: A person P is self-deceived about a proposition q if and only if (i) P believes that q, (ii) q is false, and (iii) P possesses evidence that is sufficient to show that q is false but fails to recognise the force of this evidence.
conditions: P believes q
q is false
P possesses sufficient counter-evidence but fails to recognise its force
counterexample: Consider a talented musician who believes, despite the enthusiastic reception of her performances and the praise of respected critics, that she is a mediocre performer. She possesses abundant evidence that she is talented — the applause, the critical praise, the invitations to perform — yet she discounts this evidence, attributing the applause to politeness, the praise to the critics' ignorance, and the invitations to personal connections. She is widely considered to be self-deceived about her own abilities. But q — the proposition that she is mediocre — is false, so condition (ii) is satisfied, and she possesses counter-evidence whose force she does not recognise. All three conditions are met, and the case appears to fit the definition. Yet note: the definition requires that q be false, and thus cannot accommodate the possibility that a person might be self-deceived about a proposition whose truth-value is indeterminate or a matter of degree. More importantly, the subject's self-deception seems to involve an active motivated distortion — she does not merely fail to recognise the evidence but actively reinterprets it so as to preserve a belief that serves a psychological function (protection from the anxiety of success). The definition captures the static features of self-deception but not its dynamic, motivated character.
abductive_insight: The background cases all exhibit a motivated distortion of evidence: the gambler attends selectively to wins, the parent reinterprets unfavourable reports, the politician reconstrues favours as courtesies. In each case, self-deception is not a mere failure to recognise evidence but an active, psychologically motivated process of reinterpreting or selectively attending to evidence in order to preserve a belief that serves the subject's needs. The definition captures the product of self-deception — a false belief maintained in the face of counter-evidence — but not the process, which is essentially motivated and dynamic.
passage:
The cases most readily identified as self-deception share a dynamic feature that is obscured by the standard static analyses. A gambler who persists in believing that he has a reliable system, despite mounting losses, does not merely fail to notice the counter-evidence. He actively manages his attention: he dwells on his wins, dismisses his losses as exceptional, and construes ambiguous outcomes in a favourable light. His self-deception is a motivated process, sustained by the psychological need to believe in his own competence. A parent who denies her child's difficulties does not merely overlook the evidence but actively reinterprets it: the teachers are biased, the tests are unfair, the child was tired. A politician who believes in his own integrity reframes the favours he has accepted: they are courtesies, not bribes; gestures of friendship, not instruments of influence. In each case, self-deception is not a passive failure of recognition but an active, motivated distortion of evidence.

One might define self-deception as the state of believing a false proposition while possessing evidence sufficient to show its falsity but failing to recognise the evidence's force. This captures the static structure of the paradigm cases. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is the talented musician who believes herself to be mediocre. She receives enthusiastic applause, critical praise, and prestigious invitations, yet she discounts all of it — the applause is mere politeness, the critics are ill-informed, the invitations reflect personal connections rather than talent. The definition's conditions are met: she believes a falsehood and possesses counter-evidence whose force she does not recognise. But the definition treats her self-deception as a cognitive failure — a failure to recognise evidence — when what is actually occurring is a motivated reinterpretation of evidence that serves a psychological function. Her belief in her own mediocrity protects her from the anxiety of high expectations and the vulnerability of public success. The background cases all exhibit this motivated, dynamic character, and the definition, by describing self-deception as a mere failure to recognise evidence, omits precisely the feature — the motivated distortion — that makes self-deception a distinctive psychological phenomenon rather than a simple error of reasoning.

background_cases: When a person gazes at a red rose, she has a visual experience with a distinctive qualitative character — a redness that pervades her visual field — and this qualitative character is something of which she is aware; her consciousness of the rose involves not merely a functional response but a felt quality.

When a person listens to a cello sonata, she does not merely discriminate the frequencies of sound; she experiences a rich auditory quality — the warmth and resonance of the cello — that is something over and above any functional description of her auditory processing.

When a person feels a sharp pain in her knee, the pain has a distinctive qualitative character — an intrinsic awfulness — that is not exhausted by the functional role the pain plays in causing her to limp or to seek medical attention; the qualitative character is an irreducible feature of the experience.
definition: A system S is conscious if and only if (i) S processes information from its environment, (ii) S produces discriminative responses to different environmental stimuli, and (iii) S modifies its behaviour in light of prior interactions with its environment.
conditions: S processes information from its environment
S produces discriminative responses to different environmental stimuli
S modifies its behaviour in light of prior interactions with its environment
counterexample: Consider a sophisticated sorting machine in a factory that processes information about the size, shape, and colour of objects on a conveyor belt, produces discriminative responses — directing each object to the correct bin — and modifies its behaviour in light of prior errors through an adaptive algorithm. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet we should not wish to say that the sorting machine is conscious. It lacks any inner qualitative experience: there is nothing it is like for the machine to sort objects.
abductive_insight: The background cases each involve a feature that the sorting machine lacks: a qualitative, felt character of experience — the redness of the rose, the warmth of the cello, the awfulness of the pain. The definition captures the functional and behavioural dimensions of consciousness but omits the requirement that the system's states possess subjective, qualitative character, which is what distinguishes conscious processing from mere information-handling.
passage:
When one examines the cases most clearly recognised as instances of consciousness, a feature emerges that goes beyond functional information-processing. A person gazing at a red rose has a visual experience with a distinctive qualitative character — a felt redness — that is something of which she is aware. A person listening to a cello sonata experiences a warmth and resonance that is more than a functional response to sound frequencies. A person feeling a sharp pain in her knee undergoes an experience with an intrinsic qualitative awfulness that is not captured by describing the pain's causal role in producing limping or medical-seeking behaviour. In each case, the conscious state has a felt, qualitative character — there is something it is like for the subject to undergo it.

One might attempt to capture the nature of consciousness by the following definition: a system is conscious if and only if it processes information from its environment, produces discriminative responses to different stimuli, and modifies its behaviour in light of prior interactions. This definition captures the functional dimensions of the paradigmatic cases. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency is a sophisticated sorting machine in a factory. The machine processes information about the size, shape, and colour of objects; it produces discriminative responses, directing each to the correct bin; and it modifies its behaviour through an adaptive algorithm that corrects prior errors. All three conditions are met. Yet one might observe that the machine is not conscious. It lacks the qualitative, felt character that pervades the experiences of seeing, hearing, and feeling pain. What the background cases possess, and what the machine lacks, is subjective experience — the inner qualitative dimension that makes consciousness more than mere information-processing.

background_cases: When a man feels anger at an insult, his anger is not merely a bodily disturbance — a rush of blood, a clenching of fists — but involves a judgement that he has been wronged; the anger represents the insult as an offence, and it is this evaluative component that gives the emotion its characteristic intentional direction.

When a woman feels grief at the death of a friend, her grief is not merely a heaviness of mood but involves the judgement that something of great value has been lost; the grief represents the friend's death as a profound loss, and this evaluative representation is what distinguishes grief from mere sadness or depression.

When a person feels fear upon encountering a bear on a forest path, the fear involves more than a set of bodily responses — the racing heart, the quickened breath — it involves the appraisal that the bear is dangerous; the fear represents the situation as threatening, and this appraisal shapes the character of the emotional experience.

When a person feels indignation at the sight of cruelty to an animal, the indignation involves a judgement that a moral wrong is being committed; the emotion represents the cruelty as unjust, and it is this evaluative dimension that distinguishes moral indignation from mere discomfort or displeasure.
definition: A psychological state E is an emotion if and only if (i) E involves characteristic bodily sensations — changes in heart rate, muscular tension, visceral disturbances, and the like, (ii) E has a positive or negative hedonic valence — it is experienced as pleasant or unpleasant, and (iii) E is triggered by a stimulus event in the subject's environment.
conditions: E involves characteristic bodily sensations — changes in heart rate, muscular tension, visceral disturbances, and the like
E has a positive or negative hedonic valence — it is experienced as pleasant or unpleasant
E is triggered by a stimulus event in the subject's environment
counterexample: Consider a patient who, following damage to the autonomic nervous system, reports feeling profound grief at the death of her mother. She judges that something of immeasurable value has been lost; her grief is directed at the loss; and she is disposed to withdraw, to mourn, to reflect on what has passed. Yet she experiences no characteristic bodily sensations — no heaviness in the chest, no tightness in the throat, no visceral disturbance. Her autonomic responses are absent. The first condition fails. Yet we should not deny that her state is one of grief, for it possesses the evaluative and intentional structure characteristic of emotion.
abductive_insight: The background cases all share an evaluative or appraisal component — the judgement that one has been wronged, that a loss has occurred, that danger is present, that a wrong is being committed — and it is this component that gives each emotion its distinctive intentional character. The definition captures the somatic and hedonic dimensions but omits the requirement that the state involve an evaluative appraisal or judgement, which is what makes an emotion more than a mere bodily disturbance with a pleasant or unpleasant tone.
passage:
When one examines the emotions most clearly recognised as such — anger at an insult, grief at a death, fear of a predator, indignation at cruelty — a common feature emerges that goes beyond bodily sensation. The angry man does not merely experience a rush of blood; he judges that he has been wronged. The grieving woman does not merely feel a heaviness; she judges that something of great value has been lost. The frightened person does not merely feel her heart race; she appraises the situation as dangerous. The indignant bystander does not merely feel uncomfortable; he judges that a moral wrong is being committed. In each case, the emotion involves an evaluative appraisal — a representation of its object under some evaluative aspect — and it is this appraisal that gives the emotion its distinctive intentional direction.

One might attempt to define emotion as a psychological state involving characteristic bodily sensations, possessing a positive or negative hedonic valence, and triggered by a stimulus event. This definition captures the somatic and hedonic features of emotion well. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency concerns a patient who, following damage to the autonomic nervous system, reports profound grief at the death of her mother. She judges that something of immeasurable value has been lost; her grief is directed at the loss; she is disposed to mourn. Yet she experiences no bodily sensations — no heaviness, no tightness, no visceral disturbance. Her autonomic responses are absent. The first condition fails, but we should not deny that her state is grief. What the background cases possess, and what the definition requires, is bodily sensation. But what makes those states emotions is something the definition has omitted: the evaluative appraisal that represents the object under an evaluative aspect.

background_cases: When a person sees a cat sitting on a mat, her visual experience presents the cat as occupying a particular spatial location, as having a certain shape and colour, and as being a distinct object in her environment; the experience has a presentational character — it puts the subject in direct contact with the object.

When a person hears the tolling of a church bell, her auditory experience presents the sound as coming from a particular direction and as possessing a particular pitch and timbre; the experience has the character of a direct presentation of the sound as it is in the world.

When a person touches a piece of velvet, her tactile experience presents the fabric as possessing a certain smoothness and softness; the experience is one of direct contact with the texture of the object, not merely an inference about it.
definition: A subject S perceives an object O if and only if (i) O is present in S's environment, (ii) S has a sensory experience whose content matches the properties of O, and (iii) O causes S's sensory experience through the operation of S's sense organs.
conditions: O is present in S's environment
S has a sensory experience whose content matches the properties of O
O causes S's sensory experience through the operation of S's sense organs
counterexample: Consider a neuroscientist who implants an electrode in a subject's visual cortex. The electrode, when activated, produces a visual experience indistinguishable from the experience of seeing a red sphere. As it happens, a red sphere is present in the subject's environment and would, under normal circumstances, produce just such an experience. But the subject's experience is caused not by the sphere through the operation of the eyes but by the electrode. All three conditions are satisfied — the sphere is present, the experience matches, and the sphere is indeed in the causal history of the experiment (the neuroscientist positioned the electrode to mimic the sphere's effect). Yet we should hesitate to say the subject perceives the sphere.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involve a specific mode of causal connection: the object acts upon the subject's sense organs through a natural sensory pathway — light reflected from the cat stimulates the retina, sound waves from the bell stimulate the cochlea, the texture of the velvet stimulates tactile receptors. The definition requires only that the object cause the experience through the sense organs but does not specify the mode of causation, thereby failing to exclude cases in which the causal chain is deviant — passing through artificial intermediaries rather than along the natural sensory pathway.
passage:
When one considers the most uncontroversial instances of perception — seeing a cat, hearing a bell, touching velvet — a characteristic pattern of causal connection is evident. The cat reflects light that stimulates the retina; the bell produces sound waves that stimulate the cochlea; the velvet acts upon tactile receptors in the skin. In each case, the object acts upon the sense organs through a natural sensory pathway, and the experience has the character of direct presentation — it puts the subject in contact with the object as it is in the world.

One might codify this by the following definition: a subject perceives an object if and only if the object is present in the subject's environment, the subject has a sensory experience whose content matches the object's properties, and the object causes the experience through the operation of the sense organs. This definition captures the causal and representational structure of the background cases. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency may be constructed. A neuroscientist implants an electrode in a subject's visual cortex and activates it, producing a visual experience indistinguishable from seeing a red sphere. A red sphere happens to be present in the environment. The sphere is in the causal history of the situation — the neuroscientist calibrated the electrode to replicate the sphere's visual effect. Yet the experience is produced by the electrode, not by light from the sphere acting through the eyes. One might observe that the subject does not perceive the sphere, even though all three conditions are formally met. What the background cases possess, and this case lacks, is the natural sensory pathway — the specific mode of causal connection characteristic of genuine perception.

background_cases: When a man recalls his wedding day, his memory preserves the experiential quality of the original event — the nervousness, the joy, the particular light in the church — and it is the continuity between the original experience and the present recollection that makes the recollection a genuine memory rather than a mere representation of a past event.

When a woman recalls the taste of a madeleine dipped in tea, her memory is not merely a proposition about a past gustatory experience but a re-presentation of the qualitative character of the original taste; the continuity between the original tasting and the present recollection is what gives the memory its distinctive vivacity.

When a soldier recalls the sound of shellfire, his memory preserves something of the original auditory experience — the concussive force, the ringing aftermath — and it is this preservation of experiential content, traceable back through a continuous causal chain to the original experience, that distinguishes his memory from a mere imagining of shellfire.
definition: A mental state M constitutes a memory of an event E if and only if (i) M represents E as having occurred in the subject's past, (ii) E did in fact occur, and (iii) M is accompanied by a feeling of familiarity — a sense that the represented event is something the subject has encountered before.
conditions: M represents E as having occurred in the subject's past
E did in fact occur
M is accompanied by a feeling of familiarity — a sense that the represented event is something the subject has encountered before
counterexample: Consider a man who reads a vivid historical account of the Battle of Waterloo. The account is so detailed and so powerfully written that the man develops a mental representation of the battle as a past event, accompanied by a strong feeling of familiarity — it seems to him as though the battle is something he has encountered before. All three conditions are satisfied: the event is represented as past, the battle did occur, and the feeling of familiarity is present. Yet the man does not remember the Battle of Waterloo; he was not there. His mental state is a product of imaginative reconstruction from reading, not a preservation of original experience.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involve a continuous causal chain running from the original experience to the present recollection — the wedding day is recalled because the original experience left a trace that has been preserved; the taste of the madeleine is re-presented because the original gustatory experience was encoded and retained. The definition captures the representational and phenomenological features of memory but omits the requirement of a causal chain connecting the present state to the subject's original experience, which is what distinguishes genuine memory from imaginative reconstruction of actual events.
passage:
When one examines the most uncontroversial cases of memory — recalling one's wedding day, re-tasting a madeleine, hearing again the sound of shellfire — a distinctive feature emerges. In each case, there is a continuous causal chain running from the original experience to the present recollection. The wedding day was experienced, the experience left a trace, and the present memory is the retrieval of that trace. The taste of the madeleine was undergone, encoded, and preserved, so that the present recollection re-presents the qualitative character of the original experience. The soldier's memory of shellfire is connected by a causal pathway to the original auditory experience. It is this causal continuity that gives each recollection its status as a genuine memory.

One might attempt to define memory as a mental state that represents an event as having occurred in the subject's past, that concerns an event which did in fact occur, and that is accompanied by a feeling of familiarity. This definition captures the representational and phenomenological dimensions of memory. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency concerns a man who reads a vivid account of the Battle of Waterloo. The account is so detailed that he develops a mental representation of the battle as a past event, accompanied by a feeling of familiarity — it seems to him as though the battle is something he has encountered before. All three conditions are met. But the man does not remember Waterloo; he was not there. His state is an imaginative reconstruction, not a memory. What the background cases possess, and what this case lacks, is the causal chain connecting the present mental state to the subject's own original experience of the event.

background_cases: When a person winces upon touching a hot surface, there is something it is like for her to feel the burn — a qualitative character to the experience that is not captured by the mere functional description of stimulus and withdrawal; the experience possesses a felt quality that is prior to and independent of any behavioural response.

When a person gazes at a sunset and is moved by the vivid orange of the sky, the phenomenal character of the visual experience — its specific qualitative feel — is not exhausted by the functional role it plays in guiding her behaviour; two observers might respond identically yet differ in the qualitative character of their experiences.

When a person listens to a violin concerto and is struck by the peculiar warmth of the instrument's tone, the auditory experience has a phenomenal quality that resists reduction to a description of sound waves and cochlear responses; the warmth is a feature of the experience itself, not of the physical stimulus.
definition: A system S is conscious if and only if (i) S processes information from its environment, (ii) S produces behavioural responses that are adaptive and context-sensitive, and (iii) S can report on its internal states when queried.
conditions: S processes information from its environment
S produces behavioural responses that are adaptive and context-sensitive
S can report on its internal states when queried
counterexample: Consider an advanced robotic system that processes visual, auditory, and tactile information from its environment, produces highly adaptive and context-sensitive responses, and when queried can generate detailed reports on its sensor readings, decision weights, and recent processing history. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet there is no reason to suppose that there is anything it is like to be this robot — that its information-processing is accompanied by any phenomenal experience whatever.
abductive_insight: The background cases share a feature that the robotic system conspicuously lacks: in each, the subject's experience possesses a qualitative, phenomenal character — there is something it is like to feel the burn, to see the orange, to hear the warmth — that is not captured by, and cannot be inferred from, the functional description of information-processing and behavioural output. The definition, by attending only to functional capacities, cannot distinguish systems that genuinely experience from systems that merely process.
passage:
When one examines the cases in which we are most confident that consciousness is present, a pattern emerges that goes beyond the functional capacities of information-processing and adaptive behaviour. The person who winces upon touching a hot surface does not merely process thermal information and withdraw her hand; there is something it is like for her to feel the burn — a qualitative character to the experience that persists even when the behavioural response is suppressed. The person moved by a sunset does not merely register particular wavelengths of light; she experiences a vivid phenomenal orange that is not exhausted by any functional description. The listener struck by the warmth of a violin does not merely transduce sound waves; the warmth is a felt quality of the auditory experience itself.

One might attempt to codify this pattern with the following definition: a system is conscious if it processes environmental information, produces adaptive and context-sensitive behavioural responses, and can report on its internal states when queried. This definition is attractive in its empirical tractability. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises when we consider an advanced robotic system equipped with sophisticated sensors, adaptive algorithms, and a reporting module that can describe its own internal processing in considerable detail. Such a system satisfies all three conditions. Yet there appears to be no reason to suppose that there is anything it is like to be this robot. Its information-processing may be accompanied by no phenomenal experience whatever. What the background cases possess and the robot lacks is precisely the qualitative, felt character of experience — the phenomenal dimension that is prior to and independent of any functional role. It appears that the definition, by restricting its attention to functional capacities, cannot capture the feature that most centrally constitutes consciousness.

background_cases: When an architect draws up plans for a building, her intention is not merely a desire that the building exist but a commitment to a particular course of action — a settled resolve to bring about a specific structure through a definite sequence of steps, which she treats as reason-giving and which constrains her subsequent deliberation.

When a chess player forms the intention to execute a particular opening, the intention functions not merely as a prediction of what she will do but as a practical commitment that guides her move-by-move decisions and screens out alternatives that would be incompatible with the chosen line.

When a surgeon decides to perform a particular procedure, the intention structures the entire course of the operation: it determines the sequence of incisions, the instruments to be employed, and the responses to foreseeable complications, and it resists revision except in the face of compelling new information.
definition: An agent A intends to bring about a state of affairs S if and only if (i) A has a desire for S to obtain, (ii) A believes that some action available to A would bring about S, and (iii) A has formed a plan to carry out that action.
conditions: A has a desire for S to obtain
A believes that some action available to A would bring about S
A has formed a plan to carry out that action
counterexample: Consider a person who desires to become fluent in Mandarin, believes that enrolling in a language course would contribute to this end, and has formed a detailed plan — she has identified a course, budgeted the fees, and cleared her schedule. All three conditions are met. Yet she formed the plan last January and has not thought about it since; the plan sits in a drawer, and she has made no move to enrol. She does not regard the plan as binding on her current deliberation, and if asked, she would say she has not yet decided whether to go through with it.
abductive_insight: The background cases reveal that intention involves more than the conjunction of desire, belief, and plan: in each, the agent treats the plan as a settled practical commitment that actively guides ongoing deliberation and action. The architect, the chess player, and the surgeon do not merely have plans — they are governed by them. The Mandarin learner has a plan but is not governed by it; the plan exerts no constraining force on her present conduct. What the definition lacks is the requirement that the plan function as a present commitment that structures the agent's practical reasoning.
passage:
When one examines the cases in which intention is most clearly present, one finds that the agent's plan functions not merely as an inert mental representation but as an active commitment that structures ongoing deliberation and action. The architect who intends to build does not merely possess a plan; she treats it as reason-giving and allows it to constrain her subsequent decisions. The chess player who intends a particular opening allows that intention to screen out incompatible moves. The surgeon who intends a particular procedure is guided by it at every stage of the operation.

One might attempt to analyse intention as the conjunction of a desire for a state of affairs, a belief that some available action would bring it about, and a formed plan to carry out that action. This proposal captures the motivational, cognitive, and volitional elements that are commonly associated with intention. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises when one considers a person who desires to become fluent in Mandarin, believes that enrolling in a language course would achieve this end, and has formed a detailed plan — identifying a course, budgeting the fees, and clearing her schedule. All three conditions are met. Yet the plan was formed last January, and she has not thought about it since. It sits in a drawer, exerting no influence on her current conduct. She does not regard it as binding, and she has made no move to enrol. One might observe that what she has is not an intention but an abandoned or dormant plan. What the background cases possess and this case lacks is the active, present-tense character of intention — the way in which a genuine intention functions as a practical commitment that guides and constrains the agent's ongoing deliberation. The definition, by requiring only that a plan have been formed, cannot distinguish live intentions from dead ones.

background_cases: When a pianist plays a sonata from memory, the movements of her fingers are guided by her musical understanding and motor skill in a seamless, integrated fashion: the causal chain runs from her intention through her trained motor capacities to the precise finger movements, without interruption or deviation.

When a surgeon ties a suture, the movement of his hands is guided by his intention and his skilled understanding of the procedure: the causal route from intention to action passes through well-rehearsed motor routines that execute the intention faithfully.

When a bilingual speaker switches from English to French mid-sentence, the transition is mediated by her communicative intention operating through her linguistic competence: the intention causes the appropriate words to be selected and articulated through the normal exercise of her language faculty.
definition: A bodily movement M constitutes an action if and only if (i) M is caused by the agent's intention to bring about some end, (ii) the agent has the ability to perform M, and (iii) M is the sort of movement that typically serves the end the agent intends.
conditions: M is caused by the agent's intention to bring about some end
The agent has the ability to perform M
M is the sort of movement that typically serves the end the agent intends
counterexample: Consider a nervous public speaker who intends to gesture emphatically at a key point in her speech. She has the ability to gesture, and emphatic gesturing typically serves the purpose of emphasising a point. Yet her nervousness causes her arm to jerk upward at precisely the moment she intended to gesture. The jerk is caused — via an unusual causal pathway through anxiety and involuntary muscular contraction — by her very intention to gesture. All three conditions are met. Yet the movement is not an action but an involuntary spasm that happens to coincide with the intended gesture.
abductive_insight: The background cases share a structural feature absent from the speaker's case: in each, the causal pathway from intention to movement runs through the agent's skilled motor capacities in an integrated, non-deviant fashion. The pianist's fingers move because her motor skill executes her musical intention; the surgeon's hands move because his procedural expertise translates his surgical intention into precise action. In the speaker's case, the causal route passes through anxiety and involuntary muscular contraction — a deviant chain that bypasses the agent's rational motor control. The definition captures the existence of a causal connection between intention and movement but fails to require that the connection be of the right kind.
passage:
When one examines the cases that most clearly exemplify action, one finds that the causal pathway from intention to bodily movement passes through the agent's skilled motor capacities in an integrated and non-deviant fashion. The pianist's fingers move as they do because her musical intention operates through her trained motor skill. The surgeon's hands execute the suture because his procedural intention is mediated by his well-rehearsed surgical expertise. The bilingual speaker's words emerge because her communicative intention operates through her linguistic competence. In each case, the intention causes the movement via the normal exercise of a rational capacity.

One might attempt to characterise action by requiring that a bodily movement be caused by the agent's intention, that the agent have the ability to perform the movement, and that the movement be of a type that typically serves the intended end. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation arises with a nervous public speaker who intends to gesture emphatically at the climax of her address. She has the ability to gesture, and emphatic gesturing is precisely the sort of movement that typically serves to underscore a rhetorical point. Yet at the critical moment, her nervousness causes her arm to jerk upward in an involuntary spasm. The jerk is caused by her intention to gesture — the intention produced the anxiety that produced the spasm — but the causal route passes through involuntary muscular contraction rather than through the normal exercise of motor control. All three conditions are satisfied, yet the movement is not an action. What the background cases possess and the speaker's case lacks is a non-deviant causal pathway: a route from intention to movement that passes through the agent's rational motor capacities rather than through anxiety and involuntary reflex. It appears that the definition, by failing to specify the manner of causation, cannot distinguish genuine actions from movements that are caused by intentions in the wrong way.

background_cases: When a person feels anger at an insult, the anger is directed at the person who delivered the insult and represents that person's behaviour as offensive; the emotion is constituted in part by this evaluative appraisal of its object.

When a person feels fear upon encountering a bear on a hiking trail, the fear is directed at the bear and represents the bear as dangerous; the emotion embodies an assessment of the object as posing a threat.

When a person feels grief upon learning of a friend's death, the grief is directed at the loss and represents it as a profound deprivation; the emotion incorporates an evaluative judgement that something of great value has been taken away.
definition: A mental state E is an emotion if and only if (i) E involves a distinctive bodily sensation or pattern of physiological arousal, (ii) E is directed at an intentional object, and (iii) E has a characteristic action tendency — a disposition to behave in a particular way.
conditions: E involves a distinctive bodily sensation or pattern of physiological arousal
E is directed at an intentional object
E has a characteristic action tendency
counterexample: Consider a person who has been administered a drug that induces precisely the physiological arousal pattern associated with fear — elevated heart rate, perspiration, muscular tension — while she sits in a perfectly safe room looking at a photograph of a spider. She is directed at the photograph and has the characteristic action tendency of avoidance. All three conditions are met. Yet she knows the spider is a photograph and poses no danger, and she does not in any way appraise the situation as threatening. She reports feeling the bodily sensations of fear but insists she is not afraid.
abductive_insight: The background cases share a feature absent from the drug-induced case: in each, the emotion involves an evaluative appraisal of its object — the insult is represented as offensive, the bear as dangerous, the loss as a deprivation of value. This cognitive-evaluative component is not an accidental accompaniment but is constitutive of the emotion. The drug-induced subject has the bodily sensations and the action tendency but lacks the evaluative appraisal that would make the state genuinely an emotion rather than a mere physiological episode.
passage:
When one examines the emotions that are most clearly paradigmatic, a pattern emerges that goes beyond bodily sensation and behavioural disposition. The person who is angry at an insult does not merely experience elevated blood pressure and a tendency to retaliate; her anger represents the insult as offensive — it embodies an evaluative appraisal of its object. The person who fears a bear does not merely experience a racing heart and an urge to flee; her fear represents the bear as dangerous. The person who grieves a friend's death does not merely experience heaviness and withdrawal; her grief represents the loss as a profound deprivation of value.

One might attempt to characterise emotion by appeal to distinctive bodily sensations, intentional directedness, and characteristic action tendencies. This tripartite analysis captures the somatic, cognitive, and motivational dimensions of emotional life. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency may be constructed as follows. Suppose a subject is administered a drug that induces precisely the physiological arousal pattern associated with fear — the elevated heart rate, the perspiration, the muscular tension. She sits in a perfectly safe room, looking at a photograph of a spider. Her attention is directed at the photograph, and she experiences the characteristic action tendency of avoidance. All three conditions of the definition are met. Yet she knows the spider is a photograph, she appraises the situation as entirely unthreatening, and she reports feeling the bodily sensations of fear without being afraid. What the background cases possess and this case lacks is the evaluative appraisal that is constitutive of genuine emotion: the representation of the object as offensive, dangerous, or valuable. It appears that the definition, by attending only to sensation, intentionality, and action tendency, cannot distinguish genuine emotions from pharmacologically induced states that mimic their functional profile without embodying the evaluative assessment that gives emotions their distinctive character.

background_cases: When a person plunges her hand into ice-cold water, there is something it is like for her to experience the biting cold — a qualitative character that is not exhausted by the description of her withdrawal reflex or her verbal report; the experience possesses a phenomenal quality that is prior to any functional characterisation.

When a patient under partial anaesthesia reports that she can feel the surgeon's incision but that it does not hurt, she draws a distinction between the sensory and the affective dimensions of her experience; the phenomenal character of the sensation persists even when the characteristic pain response is pharmacologically suppressed.

When a person gazes at a brightly coloured mosaic, the visual experience has a phenomenal richness — a saturation and depth of colour — that is not captured by the specification of wavelengths reaching the retina; the qualitative character of the experience is a feature of the experience itself rather than of the physical stimulus.
definition: A system S is conscious if and only if (i) S receives and processes sensory information from its environment, (ii) S integrates this information to produce coherent behavioural outputs, and (iii) S is capable of modifying its behaviour in light of past experience.
conditions: S receives and processes sensory information from its environment
S integrates this information to produce coherent behavioural outputs
S is capable of modifying its behaviour in light of past experience
counterexample: Consider a modern industrial robot equipped with an array of sensors — cameras, microphones, pressure transducers — that receives and processes information from its factory environment, integrates this information to produce coherent assembly-line movements, and modifies its behaviour on the basis of accumulated performance data. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet there appears to be nothing it is like to be this robot. Its information-processing is accompanied by no phenomenal experience whatever.
abductive_insight: The background cases share a feature conspicuously absent from the industrial robot: in each, the subject's experience possesses a phenomenal or qualitative character — the biting quality of cold, the felt distinction between sensation and suffering, the richness of colour — that is not reducible to the functional profile of information-processing and behavioural output. The definition captures the functional architecture that conscious systems typically exhibit but omits the qualitative dimension that, in the background cases, constitutes the very essence of what it is to be conscious. The robot satisfies every functional condition yet possesses no phenomenal life.
passage:
The cases in which we are most confident that consciousness is present share a feature that transcends functional description. The person who plunges her hand into ice-cold water does not merely process thermal information and withdraw; there is something it is like for her to feel the biting cold — a qualitative character that persists even when the withdrawal response is suppressed. The patient under partial anaesthesia reveals that the phenomenal character of sensation can be dissociated from its functional consequences: she feels the incision but experiences no suffering. The observer of a brightly coloured mosaic does not merely register wavelengths; she experiences a phenomenal richness that no specification of physical stimuli can capture. In each case, the experience possesses a qualitative dimension that is prior to and independent of any behavioural output.

One might attempt to characterise consciousness as the capacity to receive and process sensory information, integrate it into coherent behavioural outputs, and modify behaviour in light of past experience. This functional definition is attractive in its empirical tractability. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises when one considers a modern industrial robot equipped with cameras, microphones, and pressure sensors. The robot receives and processes environmental information, integrates it to produce precise assembly-line movements, and modifies its behaviour on the basis of accumulated performance data. All three conditions of the definition are met. Yet there appears to be no reason to suppose that the robot enjoys any phenomenal experience whatever. What the background cases possess and the robot lacks is precisely the qualitative, felt character of experience — the something-it-is-like-ness that constitutes the central puzzle of consciousness. It appears that the definition, by restricting its attention to functional capacities, cannot distinguish genuine phenomenal consciousness from the mere functional simulation of its outward manifestations.

background_cases: When a chess player deliberates over a series of moves and then executes a carefully planned sacrifice, her action is intentional not merely because it was preceded by a plan but because the plan continued to guide her action throughout its execution; at each stage she monitored the correspondence between what she was doing and what she had planned to do.

When an architect designs a building and supervises its construction, her intention is not exhausted by the initial blueprint; she continuously adjusts her directives as unforeseen difficulties arise, maintaining the guiding connection between her design intention and the actual construction process.

When a surgeon performs a delicate operation, her intention guides each incision; she monitors the correspondence between her planned procedure and the unfolding anatomy, adjusting her movements in real time to maintain the connection between plan and execution.
definition: An agent S intends to bring about an outcome O if and only if (i) S has formed a plan whose execution would, if successful, bring about O, (ii) S has not abandoned the plan, and (iii) S believes that the plan has a reasonable chance of succeeding.
conditions: S has formed a plan whose execution would bring about O
S has not abandoned the plan
S believes that the plan has a reasonable chance of succeeding
counterexample: Consider a mountaineer who formed a plan to reach the summit by a particular route, believes the plan will succeed, and has not abandoned it. Yet a sudden storm has forced her into a crevasse where she is sheltering, unable to act on her plan and unable to monitor its progress. She has not abandoned the plan — she means to resume once the storm passes. All three conditions are satisfied during the storm. Yet one might observe that during the period of enforced inaction, the plan exercises no guiding influence on her conduct. She is not executing it, monitoring it, or adjusting it. Her intention has, in a sense, gone dormant.
abductive_insight: The background cases share a feature absent from the mountaineer's enforced shelter: in each, the agent's intention exercises a continuous guiding influence on action — the chess player monitors each move against her plan, the architect adjusts her directives, the surgeon modifies her incisions. The definition captures the formation and retention of a plan but omits the dynamic, executive dimension of intention — the ongoing governance of action by plan. The mountaineer satisfies every condition yet her intention is inert, exercising no guiding influence on anything she does.
passage:
When one examines the clearest cases of intentional action, a common feature emerges that goes beyond the mere formation and retention of a plan. The chess player who executes a planned sacrifice does not merely have a plan and then act; her plan guides each move, and she monitors the correspondence between plan and execution throughout. The architect who supervises construction does not merely hand over a blueprint; she continuously adjusts her directives as the building takes shape, maintaining the guiding connection between design and construction. The surgeon does not merely plan an operation; she monitors and adjusts her incisions in real time, keeping her actions under the continuous governance of her surgical intention.

One might attempt to characterise intention as having formed a plan whose execution would bring about the intended outcome, not having abandoned the plan, and believing it will succeed. This definition captures the static dimension of intention — its formation and persistence. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises when the agent is separated from the possibility of action. Consider a mountaineer who has formed a detailed plan to reach the summit by a particular route and who believes it will succeed. A sudden storm forces her into a crevasse, where she shelters for hours. She has not abandoned her plan — she means to resume it once the storm passes. All three conditions of the definition are satisfied. Yet during the storm her plan exercises no guiding influence on her conduct. She is not executing it, not monitoring it, not adjusting it. Her intention exists only as a dormant mental state. What the background cases possess and the sheltering mountaineer lacks is the dynamic, executive dimension of intention — the ongoing governance of action by plan. It appears that the definition, by requiring only that a plan be formed, retained, and believed feasible, cannot distinguish the active guidance that characterises paradigmatic intention from the mere possession of a plan that has been temporarily set aside.

background_cases: When a person feels fear upon encountering a bear in the wilderness, her fear is intelligible in light of the danger the bear poses; the emotion is fitting because its object — the bear — genuinely warrants the response, and the fear would disappear if the danger were removed.

When a person feels grief upon learning of the death of a close friend, her grief is intelligible as a response to genuine loss; the emotion is fitting because its object — the loss — genuinely warrants sorrow, and the grief would be irrational in the absence of any loss.

When a person feels indignation upon witnessing an unprovoked assault, her indignation is intelligible as a response to injustice; the emotion is fitting because its object — the unjust act — genuinely warrants moral outrage, and the indignation would be inappropriate if no wrong had been done.
definition: A mental state E is an emotion if and only if (i) E is caused by the subject's appraisal of a situation, (ii) E involves characteristic physiological changes, and (iii) E disposes the subject towards characteristic behaviour.
conditions: E is caused by the subject's appraisal of a situation
E involves characteristic physiological changes
E disposes the subject towards characteristic behaviour
counterexample: Consider a subject who, as a result of a pharmacological experiment, is injected with a substance that produces the characteristic physiological changes associated with anger — elevated heart rate, increased blood pressure, muscular tension — and who is then placed in a mildly frustrating situation. She appraises the situation as frustrating. The physiological changes dispose her towards aggressive behaviour. All three conditions are met. Yet one might observe that her anger is disproportionate to the situation: the frustration is trivial, and the intensity of her response is entirely an artefact of the pharmacological intervention rather than a fitting response to the situation as appraised.
abductive_insight: The background cases share a feature absent from the pharmacologically induced anger: in each, the emotion is proportionate and fitting — the fear matches the danger, the grief matches the loss, the indignation matches the injustice. The emotion is not merely caused by the appraisal but is rationally appropriate to it. The pharmacological case satisfies every condition in the definition yet produces an emotion whose intensity bears no rational relation to the situation that occasions it. The definition captures the causal structure of emotion but omits the normative dimension — the requirement that the emotion be a fitting response to its object.
passage:
The clearest cases of genuine emotion share a feature that mere causal production cannot capture. Fear upon encountering a bear is not merely caused by the perception of a large animal; it is a fitting response to a genuine danger, proportionate to the threat and intelligible in its light. Grief upon the death of a friend is not merely triggered by the receipt of bad news; it is an appropriate response to genuine loss, and it would be puzzling — even irrational — in the absence of any loss. Indignation at an unprovoked assault is not merely a reaction to a surprising event; it is a proportionate moral response to an injustice. In each case, the emotion is intelligible not merely as an effect of its cause but as a fitting response to its object.

One might attempt to characterise emotion as a mental state caused by the subject's appraisal of a situation, involving characteristic physiological changes, and disposing the subject towards characteristic behaviour. This causal-functional definition captures the psychological architecture of emotion. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises in the context of pharmacological manipulation. Suppose a subject is injected with a substance that produces the physiological profile of anger — racing heart, elevated blood pressure, muscular tension. She is then placed in a mildly frustrating situation: she is asked to solve a trivially difficult puzzle. She appraises the situation as frustrating. The physiological changes dispose her towards aggression. All three conditions are met. Yet her anger is wildly disproportionate to its occasion. The frustration is trivial; the intensity of the response is an artefact of the injection. What the background cases possess and the pharmacological case lacks is the fittingness of the emotion — its proportionality to its object.
