It appears that the definition, by requiring only that the emotion be caused by an appraisal and accompanied by physiological changes, cannot distinguish genuine emotions from pharmacologically inflated states that bear no rational relation to the situations that occasion them.

background_cases: When a birdwatcher identifies a warbler by its song, her perception is informed by years of training that have attuned her auditory system to subtle differences in pitch, rhythm, and timbre; she perceives distinctions that an untrained listener cannot detect, not because the acoustic information is absent from the untrained listener's ears but because her perceptual system has been shaped by learning to extract and organise that information.

When a radiologist reads a chest X-ray and immediately detects a small nodule that a medical student overlooks, her perception has been shaped by thousands of prior cases; she sees the nodule not by inference from the image but directly — it stands out from the background in a way that it does not for the student.

When a master vintner tastes a wine and perceives distinct layers of flavour — oak, blackcurrant, mineral — her gustatory perception has been refined by decades of practice; the untrained palate may register only a general impression of pleasant or unpleasant, but the vintner perceives a structured array of discriminable qualities.
definition: A subject S perceives a feature F of the environment if and only if (i) F is present in S's environment, (ii) S has a sensory experience whose content includes F, and (iii) S's experience is caused in the standard way by F.
conditions: F is present in S's environment
S has a sensory experience whose content includes F
S's experience is caused in the standard way by F
counterexample: Consider an untrained listener and a trained birdwatcher standing in the same woodland. A wood warbler sings nearby. The acoustic signal — the feature F — is present in both listeners' environments and reaches both their ears through the standard causal pathway. Yet only the birdwatcher has a sensory experience whose content includes the identification of the song as that of a wood warbler. The untrained listener hears a bird singing but does not perceive the song as a wood warbler's song. Condition (ii) is not satisfied for the untrained listener, yet the feature is present and the causal pathway is standard. The definition cannot explain why the same feature, acting through the same causal pathway, is perceived by one subject and not by another.
abductive_insight: The background cases reveal that perception is not merely the passive reception of environmental features through standard causal pathways but is shaped by the subject's training, experience, and perceptual expertise. The birdwatcher, the radiologist, and the vintner perceive features that are present and causally active for all observers, yet only the trained perceiver has an experience whose content includes those features. The definition captures the causal and environmental conditions of perception but omits the contribution of the perceiver's acquired perceptual capacities — the trained sensitivity that determines which features of the environment are perceptually available to which subjects.
passage:
When one examines cases of expert perception, a pattern emerges that the standard causal account of perception cannot accommodate. The birdwatcher identifies a warbler by its song, perceiving distinctions in pitch, rhythm, and timbre that an untrained listener simply cannot detect — not because the acoustic information fails to reach the untrained ear but because the birdwatcher's perceptual system has been shaped by years of learning. The radiologist reads a chest X-ray and immediately detects a small nodule that a medical student overlooks, seeing it not by inference but directly, as a salient feature of the image. The master vintner tastes a wine and perceives structured layers of flavour that the untrained palate registers only as a vague impression. In each case, training has not merely added a capacity for inference but has transformed the character of perceptual experience itself.

One might attempt to characterise perception as having a sensory experience whose content includes a feature of the environment, where the feature is present and causes the experience in the standard way. This definition captures the causal and environmental conditions that perception requires. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises when one considers two listeners standing in the same woodland. A wood warbler sings nearby. The acoustic signal is present in both listeners' environments and reaches both their ears through the standard causal pathway. Yet only the trained birdwatcher perceives the song as that of a wood warbler. The untrained listener hears a bird singing but cannot discriminate the species. The feature is present, the causal pathway is standard, yet the perceptual content differs. What the background cases reveal is that perception depends not only upon the presence and causal activity of environmental features but upon the perceiver's acquired perceptual expertise. It appears that the definition, by omitting the contribution of training and perceptual learning, cannot explain why two subjects with identical sensory stimulation may differ radically in what they perceive.

background_cases: When a subject reports seeing a red apple on the table, we accept that she is conscious of the apple because her verbal report accurately describes a feature of her environment and is accompanied by appropriate behavioural responses — she reaches for the apple, avoids bumping it, and so forth; the consciousness of the apple is evidenced by the convergence of report, perception, and action.

When a sleeping subject displays rapid eye movements and, upon being woken, describes a vivid dream involving flight over a landscape, we accept that she was conscious during the dream because her report, though not verifiable against the external world, is coherent, detailed, and accompanied by the physiological markers of dreaming; consciousness is evidenced by the convergence of report and physiological correlates.

When a person under general anaesthesia shows no verbal report, no purposive behavioural response, and no electrophysiological markers of cortical integration, we accept that she is not conscious; the absence of consciousness is evidenced by the joint absence of report, behaviour, and physiological correlates.
definition: A subject S is conscious of a content C if and only if (i) S is in a neurophysiological state that correlates with the processing of C, (ii) S can produce a verbal or behavioural report that accurately reflects C, and (iii) S's processing of C is integrated with S's other ongoing mental processes in a manner that influences reasoning and action.
conditions: S is in a neurophysiological state that correlates with the processing of C
S can produce a verbal or behavioural report that accurately reflects C
S's processing of C is integrated with other ongoing mental processes
counterexample: Consider a patient with a severe lesion of the primary visual cortex who reports being entirely blind in a portion of her visual field. When a light is flashed in the blind region, she denies seeing anything. Yet when forced to guess whether the light appeared, she guesses correctly at rates far exceeding chance. Her neurophysiological state correlates with the processing of the stimulus through subcortical pathways. Her responses demonstrate that the processing influences action. Yet she cannot produce a report that reflects the content — she sincerely denies any awareness of the light.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involved subjects in whom the three indicators of consciousness — neurophysiological correlation, reportability, and integrative processing — converged or jointly failed. The definition captured this convergence but could not accommodate cases in which one indicator is present while another is absent, revealing that consciousness may admit of dissociations that a conjunctive definition cannot represent.
passage:
Three cases of consciousness exhibit a suggestive pattern. When a subject sees a red apple, her consciousness of it is attested by the convergence of verbal report, appropriate behaviour, and the neurophysiological correlates of visual processing. When a dreamer reports vivid imagery upon waking, her dream-consciousness is attested by the convergence of report and physiological markers, even in the absence of external verification. When a patient under general anaesthesia exhibits none of these indicators, we accept that consciousness is absent. From these cases one might extract a definition: a subject is conscious of a content when she is in a correlating neurophysiological state, can report the content, and processes it in an integrated manner that influences reasoning and action.

Yet this definition is embarrassed by the clinical phenomenon sometimes called blindsight. Consider a patient with a severe lesion of the primary visual cortex who reports being entirely blind in a portion of her visual field. A light is flashed in the blind region. She sincerely denies seeing it. Condition (ii) is not satisfied — she cannot produce any report reflecting the visual content. Yet when forced to guess whether the light appeared, she guesses correctly at rates far exceeding what chance alone could explain. Her subcortical visual pathways process the stimulus, and this processing influences her behaviour — her guesses are guided by information she denies possessing. Conditions (i) and (iii) are at least partially met, while condition (ii) fails. One might observe that the background cases from which the definition was drawn were all instances in which the three indicators of consciousness either converged in their presence or converged in their absence. The definition captured this convergence but could not anticipate cases in which the indicators dissociate — in which a subject processes a stimulus, is influenced by it, and yet sincerely and emphatically denies any awareness of it. It appears that consciousness admits of partial or degraded forms that a conjunctive definition, requiring the joint satisfaction of all conditions, cannot accommodate.

background_cases: When a man hears that his child has been injured, he feels a surge of fear characterised by physiological arousal, a cognitive appraisal of the situation as threatening to his child's welfare, and an immediate disposition to rush to the child's aid; the emotion is constituted by the convergence of bodily feeling, evaluative cognition, and action tendency.

When a woman receives an unexpected promotion, she feels joy characterised by physiological lightness, a cognitive appraisal of the situation as fulfilling a long-held aspiration, and a disposition to celebrate; again, the emotion is constituted by the convergence of feeling, appraisal, and action tendency.

When a student discovers that his work has been plagiarised, he feels anger characterised by physiological tension, a cognitive appraisal of the situation as a violation of his rights, and a disposition to confront the plagiarist; the same three components converge.
definition: A mental state M is an emotion if and only if (i) M involves a characteristic pattern of physiological arousal, (ii) M involves a cognitive appraisal of a situation as bearing upon the subject's concerns, and (iii) M disposes the subject towards a characteristic pattern of action.
conditions: M involves a characteristic pattern of physiological arousal
M involves a cognitive appraisal of a situation as bearing upon the subject's concerns
M disposes the subject towards a characteristic pattern of action
counterexample: Consider a man who, years after the death of a close friend, hears a piece of music that his friend loved. He is overtaken by a feeling of profound sadness — not the acute grief of fresh bereavement but a quiet, pervasive melancholy. There is no characteristic pattern of physiological arousal: his heart rate does not increase, his breathing does not change, and he experiences no bodily tension. He makes no cognitive appraisal of the situation as threatening or harmful — he understands perfectly well that no new loss has occurred. And he is not disposed towards any characteristic action; he simply sits and feels the sadness pass through him. None of the three conditions is satisfied, yet the mental state is unmistakably an emotion.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all instances of acute emotional responses to significant events, in which physiological arousal, cognitive appraisal, and action tendency reliably converged. The definition captured the structure of these paradigmatic cases but failed to accommodate the quieter, more reflective emotional states — moods, nostalgic feelings, diffuse sadness — that lack the characteristic urgency and physiological signature of acute emotion yet are plainly emotional in character.
passage:
Consider three cases that illuminate the nature of emotion. A father hears that his child has been hurt and is seized by fear: his heart races, he appraises the situation as a threat to his child, and he is disposed to rush to the scene. A woman receives a promotion and feels joy: her body lightens, she appraises the event as fulfilling a deep aspiration, and she is disposed to celebrate. A student discovers his work has been plagiarised and feels anger: his muscles tense, he appraises the situation as a violation, and he is disposed to confront the offender. In each case, the emotion is constituted by the convergence of physiological arousal, cognitive appraisal, and action tendency. One might therefore define an emotion as a mental state involving characteristic physiological arousal, a cognitive appraisal bearing upon the subject's concerns, and a disposition towards characteristic action.

Yet this definition is challenged by a case of a rather different character. Consider a man who, many years after the death of a close friend, hears a piece of music that his friend especially loved. He is overtaken by a feeling of profound sadness — not the acute grief of fresh bereavement but a quiet, pervasive melancholy that settles upon him like a change in the weather. His heart rate does not increase. His breathing remains steady. He experiences no bodily tension. He makes no cognitive appraisal of the situation as threatening or harmful — he understands perfectly well that no new loss has occurred. And he is not disposed towards any characteristic action. He does not wish to flee, to fight, or to seek comfort. He simply sits and allows the sadness to move through him. None of the three conditions of the definition is met. Yet the mental state is unmistakably emotional. It is not a thought, not a perception, not a mood without object; it is a feeling of sadness directed towards the memory of a particular person. One might observe that the background cases from which the definition was drawn were all instances of acute response to significant events. The definition captured the structure of urgency — the raised pulse, the rapid appraisal, the readiness for action — but cannot accommodate the quieter emotional life that fills the intervals between crises.

background_cases: When a man recalls his childhood experiences, recognises his ageing face in the mirror as his own, and is held responsible by others for contracts he signed decades ago, we treat him as the same person he was in youth; his identity over time is grounded in psychological continuity — the persistence of memories, character, and self-recognition.

When a woman wakes from dreamless sleep, she takes herself to be the same person who fell asleep the previous night; the brief interruption of consciousness does not threaten her identity because her memories, personality, and bodily continuity bridge the gap.

When a patient recovers from a coma lasting several weeks, her family recognises her as the same person despite the interruption; her memories of pre-coma life, though initially confused, gradually return, and her personality reasserts itself.
definition: A person P at time t2 is the same person as Q at time t1 if and only if (i) there is psychological continuity between P and Q — that is, P's memories, character traits, and self-conception are continuous developments of Q's, (ii) this continuity is not interrupted by any period in which the relevant psychological connections are entirely absent, and (iii) no other person at t2 stands in the same relation of psychological continuity to Q at t1.
conditions: There is psychological continuity between P at t2 and Q at t1
This continuity is not interrupted by any period in which psychological connections are entirely absent
No other person at t2 stands in the same relation of psychological continuity to Q at t1
counterexample: Suppose it becomes possible to duplicate the complete psychological state of a person — all memories, character traits, personality, and self-conception — and to instantiate it in a new body. The original person, Q, exists at t1. At t2, two persons exist: P1, the original, and P2, the duplicate. Both P1 and P2 stand in a relation of psychological continuity with Q. Both possess Q's memories, character, and self-conception. Condition (iii) is violated, for two persons at t2 stand in the relevant relation to Q at t1. The definition must deny that either is identical to Q, yet it would be extraordinary to deny that the original, P1, whose body and brain have been continuous throughout, is the same person as Q.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involved scenarios in which psychological continuity was uniquely instantiated — only one candidate at the later time could claim continuity with the earlier person. The definition captured this uniqueness as a necessary condition but failed to anticipate the possibility that psychological continuity might be duplicated, forcing a denial of identity even in the case of the undisturbed original.
passage:
Three cases suggest that personal identity over time is grounded in psychological continuity. A man who recalls his childhood, recognises his own ageing face, and is held to his past commitments is treated as the same person he was decades ago. A woman who wakes from dreamless sleep takes herself to be the same person who fell asleep. A patient emerging from a coma is recognised by her family as the person she was before the interruption. In each case, psychological continuity — the persistence of memories, character, and self-conception — provides the thread that binds the earlier and later selves. One might therefore define personal identity as the holding of uninterrupted psychological continuity, with the further requirement that no other person stand in the same relation to the earlier self.

Yet suppose it becomes technologically possible to scan the complete psychological state of a person and to instantiate a perfect duplicate in a new body. At time t1, Q exists as a single person with a distinctive set of memories, character traits, and self-conception. At time t2, two persons exist: P1, the original, whose body and brain have been continuous throughout, and P2, a newly created duplicate who possesses all of Q's memories, character, and self-conception. Both P1 and P2 stand in a relation of psychological continuity with Q. Both remember Q's experiences. Both share Q's character. Both sincerely avow being Q. Condition (iii) of the definition is violated, for two persons at t2 stand in the relevant relation to Q at t1. The definition therefore denies that either P1 or P2 is identical to Q. Yet this conclusion is deeply counterintuitive in the case of P1, who is the undisturbed original — the very person whose brain has been continuously active and whose body has been continuously alive throughout the process. One might observe that the background cases from which the definition was drawn were all cases in which psychological continuity was uniquely instantiated. The definition captured this uniqueness but could not anticipate its violation by duplication, forcing it to deny the identity of the original with herself.

background_cases: When a man deliberates between two dishes on a menu and chooses one after weighing his preferences, we regard his choice as free because it proceeded from his own desires, was uncoerced, and was arrived at through a process of rational reflection that he controlled.

When a woman decides, after careful thought, to change careers despite the financial risk, we regard her decision as free because it reflects her deepest values, was made without external pressure, and resulted from deliberation over which she had authority.

When a student chooses to study philosophy rather than law, against the advice of his parents but in accordance with his own intellectual passions, we regard his choice as free because it issues from his authentic preferences rather than from the preferences of others imposed upon him.
definition: An agent S acts freely in performing action A if and only if (i) A proceeds from S's own desires and values as they figure in S's deliberation, (ii) S's deliberation is not subject to coercion or compulsion by any external agent, and (iii) S could have chosen otherwise had S's desires or values been different.
conditions: A proceeds from S's own desires and values as they figure in S's deliberation
S's deliberation is not subject to coercion or compulsion by any external agent
S could have chosen otherwise had S's desires or values been different
counterexample: Consider a man with a severe neurological condition that affects the prefrontal cortex, producing an inability to inhibit impulses. He desires to refrain from interrupting conversations — it is sincerely among his values to be courteous. Yet he invariably interrupts, because the neural mechanism that would translate his reflective preference into behavioural control is damaged. His action — interrupting — does not proceed from his values. It proceeds from an impulse that his values reject. No external agent coerces him. And had his desires been different, the result would have been the same, for the deficit is in the translation from desire to action, not in the desires themselves. None of the three conditions clearly applies.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involved agents with intact capacities for translating reflective deliberation into action. The definition captured the role of desires, values, and deliberation in free action but tacitly assumed that the agent's deliberative conclusions could be carried through into behaviour — an assumption that breaks down when the neural mechanisms connecting deliberation to action are impaired.
passage:
Three cases illustrate our ordinary conception of free action. A man choosing between dishes on a menu acts freely because his selection proceeds from his own preferences, arrived at through reflection, without external compulsion. A woman changing careers acts freely because the decision reflects her deepest values, reached through deliberation she controlled. A student choosing a field of study against parental advice acts freely because the choice issues from his authentic intellectual passions. In each case, the action proceeds from the agent's own desires and values, the deliberation is uncoerced, and the agent could have chosen otherwise had his values differed. One might therefore define free action as action that issues from the agent's own desires and values through uncoerced deliberation, with the capacity for alternative choice.

Yet this definition is embarrassed by a case drawn from clinical neurology. Consider a man with a lesion of the prefrontal cortex that impairs his capacity for impulse inhibition. He is a courteous person who sincerely values restraint in conversation. He deliberates about how to behave and resolves not to interrupt others. No one coerces him. Had his values been different — had he not cared about courtesy — he would presumably have behaved differently, though in the opposite direction from what the definition predicts. Yet he invariably interrupts, because the neural mechanism that would translate his reflective resolution into behavioural control is damaged. His action does not proceed from his values; it proceeds from an impulse that his values condemn. The definition, drawn from cases in which deliberation issued smoothly into action, tacitly assumed that the connection between reflective preference and behaviour was intact. When that connection is severed by neurological damage, the definition cannot classify the resulting behaviour. The agent deliberates, holds values, and is uncoerced, yet his action contradicts his deliberation — a possibility the definition did not foresee.

background_cases: When a person tastes a ripe strawberry, the sweetness has a distinctive qualitative character — a felt quality that is immediately present to the taster and that she can describe, compare with other tastes, and reason about. The experience is conscious because it is accessible to the subject for report, comparison, and rational use.

When a person feels a sharp pain in her knee, the pain has a vivid qualitative character that is immediately available to her — she can report it, reason about its cause, and take action to alleviate it. The experience is conscious because the subject has full cognitive access to it.

When a person sees a brilliant sunset, the visual experience has a rich qualitative character — the colours, the shapes, the luminosity — that is immediately present and available for description, aesthetic appreciation, and deliberate attention. The experience is conscious because the subject can direct her attention to it, report on it, and integrate it into her reasoning.
definition: A mental state M is conscious if and only if (i) M has a determinate qualitative character, (ii) M is present to the subject who undergoes it, and (iii) the subject can report on M or use M in reasoning.
conditions: M has a determinate qualitative character
M is present to the subject who undergoes it
The subject can report on M or use M in reasoning
counterexample: Consider a blindsight patient who, following damage to the primary visual cortex, sincerely denies seeing anything in a region of her visual field. She cannot report on any visual experience in that region. Yet when forced to guess whether a stimulus is present, she guesses correctly at rates far above chance, and her behaviour — reaching accurately for objects she denies seeing — suggests that visual information is being processed and used. She has no reportable qualitative experience, yet visual content influences her behaviour. The third condition is violated, and arguably the first, yet there is a sense in which visual processing is occurring.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share is that the qualitative character of the experience is fully accessible to the subject for report and reasoning. The definition mistakes this full accessibility for a necessary condition of consciousness, overlooking the possibility that conscious processing may occur at levels below full reportability — that there may be degrees of consciousness rather than a sharp boundary between the conscious and the unconscious.
passage:
The taste of the strawberry, the pain in the knee, and the sight of the sunset each present a qualitative character that is immediately available to the subject for report, reasoning, and deliberate attention. These cases suggest that consciousness consists in the presence of qualitative character together with cognitive accessibility — the capacity to report on one's experience and to use it in rational thought.

Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation may be drawn from the neurological condition known as blindsight. Following damage to the primary visual cortex, certain patients sincerely deny seeing anything in a region of their visual field. They have no reportable visual experience; they cannot describe what they see, because — by their own account — they see nothing at all. Yet when forced to guess whether a stimulus is present in the affected region, they guess correctly at rates far exceeding chance. They can reach accurately for objects they deny seeing, and they discriminate shapes and orientations they claim to be invisible to them. Visual information is being processed and is influencing behaviour, but the subject has no reportable qualitative experience. The third condition is violated; the first is at least doubtful. It appears that the background cases, in which consciousness coincides with full reportability, have led to a definition that draws a sharp boundary where the phenomenon may admit of degrees. Consciousness, one might observe, may not be an all-or-nothing matter but a graded property that can be present in attenuated forms below the threshold of full report.

background_cases: When we say that the elderly man sitting in the garden is the same person as the young soldier who fought in the war, we base the identification upon psychological continuity — the elderly man remembers the soldier's experiences, shares his character traits, and regards himself as the same person who once served in the army.

When we say that the woman who wakes in the morning is the same person who fell asleep the night before, we rely upon the continuity of memory, personality, and self-identification that bridges the gap of unconsciousness.

When we say that the recovering patient is the same person as the person who was admitted to hospital, we depend upon the patient's memories of her pre-hospital life, her recognisable personality, and her own sense of continuous identity.
definition: A person P₁ at time t₁ is identical with a person P₂ at time t₂ if and only if (i) P₂ has continuous memories linking her to P₁, (ii) P₂ shares the same character and personality as P₁, and (iii) P₂ identifies herself as the same person as P₁.
conditions: P₂ has continuous memories linking her to P₁
P₂ shares the same character and personality as P₁
P₂ identifies herself as the same person as P₁
counterexample: Consider a person who undergoes a gradual but profound character transformation over the course of decades — through education, religious conversion, and accumulated life experience, she becomes, in character and personality, an entirely different person from the young woman she once was. Her values, her temperament, her interests, and her manner of relating to others have all changed fundamentally. The second condition is violated. Yet she is the same person — she remembers her earlier life, identifies herself as the same individual, and is recognised as such by the law, her family, and her community.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share is that psychological continuity encompasses continuity of memory, character, and self-identification simultaneously. The definition treats the conjunction of all three as necessary, overlooking the fact that personal identity can persist through radical changes in character and personality, provided that other strands of psychological continuity — particularly memory — remain intact.
passage:
The elderly veteran, the woman who wakes in the morning, and the recovering hospital patient each remain the same person across time through the continuity of memory, character, and self-identification. These cases suggest a psychological criterion of personal identity: a person at a later time is identical with a person at an earlier time when continuous memory, shared character, and self-identification link them together.

Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of the character condition may be drawn from the phenomenon of profound personal transformation. Consider a person who undergoes, over the course of several decades, a fundamental change in character and personality. Through education, religious conversion, bereavement, and accumulated experience, she becomes — in temperament, values, interests, and manner — an entirely different person from the young woman she once was. The transformation is so complete that acquaintances from her youth would not recognise her personality. The second condition is violated; character and personality are not shared. Yet she is the same person. She remembers her earlier life with clarity, identifies herself as the same individual, and is recognised as such by her family, the law, and the community. One might observe that the background cases happen to be cases in which memory, character, and self-identification are all preserved, and that the definition has mistaken this conjunction for a necessary condition. Personal identity, it appears, can survive radical changes in character, provided that other strands of psychological continuity — particularly memory and self-identification — remain intact.

background_cases: When a person deliberates over whether to accept a job offer, weighing the advantages and disadvantages, consulting her values, and eventually choosing to accept — we regard her choice as free because it was the product of her own rational deliberation, uncoerced by external forces.

When a voter considers the candidates, reflects upon their policies, and casts her ballot — we regard the vote as free because it resulted from the voter's own reflective judgement, formed without coercion or manipulation.

When a person decides to learn a musical instrument, motivated by a genuine love of music and a desire for self-improvement — we regard the decision as free because it flows from the person's own desires, values, and reflective endorsement.
definition: An action X is free if and only if (i) X is the product of the agent's own deliberation, (ii) X is not coerced by any external force, and (iii) the agent reflectively endorses the desires that motivate X.
conditions: X is the product of the agent's own deliberation
X is not coerced by any external force
The agent reflectively endorses the desires that motivate X
counterexample: Consider a person raised in a closed ideological community who deliberates carefully, is not externally coerced, and reflectively endorses the desires that motivate her actions. All three conditions are met. Yet her deliberation operates within a framework of values and beliefs that were instilled through systematic indoctrination from childhood — she has never been exposed to alternative perspectives and lacks the resources to question the premises of her reasoning. Her reflective endorsement, though sincere, is the product of a process of formation that she had no role in choosing and no opportunity to evaluate.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share is that the agents' deliberative frameworks were formed in conditions of reasonable openness — they had access to diverse perspectives and could have thought otherwise. The definition mistakes the products of deliberation for the conditions of freedom without requiring that the deliberative framework itself was formed under conditions conducive to genuine autonomy.
passage:
The person accepting a job, the voter casting her ballot, and the aspiring musician each act freely because their actions flow from their own deliberation, are uncoerced, and are motivated by desires they reflectively endorse. These cases suggest a definition: a free action is one produced by uncoerced deliberation in which the agent endorses her own motivating desires.

Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this deliberative account may be drawn from the phenomenon of ideological indoctrination. Consider a person raised from birth in a closed community governed by a rigid ideology. She has never encountered alternative perspectives; every book, every teacher, every social interaction has reinforced the same set of values and beliefs. She deliberates carefully within this framework — she is not stupid, nor is she coerced. She reflectively endorses the desires that motivate her actions — she genuinely wants what her upbringing has taught her to want. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet one might observe that her freedom is compromised, for her deliberative framework was formed through systematic indoctrination. She endorses her desires, but she endorses them because she has never been given the opportunity to consider alternatives. Her reflective endorsement, though sincere, is the product of a formative process she did not choose and cannot evaluate. It appears that the background cases involved agents whose deliberative capacities were formed under conditions of reasonable openness, and that the definition has mistaken the outputs of deliberation for the conditions of genuine autonomy.

background_cases: When a person feels anger at being insulted, the anger is directed towards the insult and the person who delivered it — the emotion has an intentional object, and it is appropriate because the insult is a genuine offence that warrants an angry response.

When a person feels grief at the death of a loved one, the grief is directed towards the loss — the emotion has an intentional object, and it is appropriate because the death of someone dear is a genuinely grievous event.

When a person feels fear upon encountering a venomous snake, the fear is directed towards the snake — the emotion has an intentional object, and it is appropriate because the snake poses a genuine threat to the person's safety.
definition: An emotion E is appropriate if and only if (i) E is directed towards an intentional object, (ii) the object warrants E — the features of the object make E a fitting response, and (iii) E is proportionate to the features of the object that warrant it.
conditions: E is directed towards an intentional object
The object warrants E — its features make E a fitting response
E is proportionate to the features of the object
counterexample: Consider a person who feels mild irritation upon learning that an asteroid will destroy the Earth within twenty-four hours. Her emotion is directed towards an intentional object — the impending destruction of the planet. The object warrants a negative emotional response — the destruction of the Earth is genuinely terrible. But her emotion is grossly disproportionate: mild irritation is not a proportionate response to the annihilation of all human life. Now reverse the case: consider a person who feels overwhelming, paralysing terror at the sight of a harmless house spider. The object does not warrant terror; the spider poses no genuine threat. Yet the emotion may serve a useful function — a generalised wariness of spiders. The definition cannot easily adjudicate these cases because proportionality requires a standard that the definition does not supply.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share is that there is an obvious and uncontroversial fit between the emotion and its object — anger at insult, grief at death, fear at danger. The definition assumes that the fittingness of emotions can always be assessed by examining the features of the object, but this assumption breaks down in cases where the appropriate emotional response is genuinely uncertain, culturally variable, or dependent upon the individual's circumstances and history.
passage:
Anger at insult, grief at death, and fear in the face of danger each present a clear fit between emotion and object: the features of the object make the emotional response appropriate, and the magnitude of the emotion is proportionate to the significance of the occasion. These cases suggest a definition of emotional appropriateness: an emotion is appropriate when it is directed towards an object whose features warrant it and when the emotion is proportionate to those features.

Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this fittingness account may be constructed by pressing upon the notion of proportionality. Consider a person who, upon learning that an asteroid will destroy the Earth within twenty-four hours, feels only mild irritation. Her emotion is directed towards a genuine object; the impending catastrophe is a real and terrible event. But her response is grotesquely disproportionate — mild irritation is not a fitting response to the annihilation of all human life. Conversely, consider a person who feels overwhelming terror at the sight of a harmless house spider. The spider does not warrant terror; yet her response, though disproportionate by the definition's lights, may reflect a deeply ingrained aversion with a complex personal and evolutionary history. The definition assumes that proportionality can be assessed by examining the features of the object, but it provides no standard for determining what counts as proportionate. It appears that the background cases involved objects whose emotional significance was uncontroversial, and that the definition has mistaken this clarity for a general principle. In many cases, the appropriate emotional response is genuinely uncertain, culturally variable, and dependent upon the individual's history and circumstances.

background_cases: When a person awakes from dreamless sleep, the transition to waking consciousness is marked not merely by the resumption of behavioural responsiveness but by the reappearance of a unified field of experience — a single perspectival vantage from which sights, sounds, and bodily sensations are simultaneously apprehended as belonging to the same subject.

When a patient recovers from general anaesthesia, the return of consciousness is characterised not simply by the patient's ability to respond to commands but by the re-emergence of an integrated experiential perspective: the patient again finds herself situated in a world of interconnected perceptions, each informing and constraining the others within a single coherent field.

When an infant first becomes conscious — if we may speak of such a moment — the emergence is plausibly understood not as the appearance of isolated sensory episodes but as the coalescence of disparate sensory inputs into a unified perspective: the infant begins to experience its surroundings as a single, coherent scene rather than as a disconnection of separate stimuli.
definition: A mental state M is a conscious experience if and only if (i) M has qualitative character — there is something it is like for the subject to be in M, (ii) the subject can attend to and reflect upon M, and (iii) M is available for the subject's voluntary report.
conditions: M has qualitative character — there is something it is like for the subject to be in M
The subject can attend to and reflect upon M
M is available for the subject's voluntary report
counterexample: Consider a patient suffering from a rare neurological condition in which her visual and auditory experiences, though individually vivid and qualitatively rich, are entirely disconnected from one another. She sees a red ball and hears a bouncing sound, but these two experiences do not belong to the same perceptual field; she cannot relate them, cannot recognise that the sound emanates from the object she sees. She can attend to each experience individually and report upon it voluntarily. Each state has qualitative character. All three conditions are met for each experience taken separately. Yet there is a clear sense in which her consciousness is fragmented — she lacks the unified experiential perspective that the background cases reveal to be characteristic of conscious experience properly so called.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share, and what the definition fails to require, is that conscious experience involves the integration of individual mental states into a unified experiential field — a single perspective within which diverse sensory and cognitive states are bound together and experienced as belonging to the same subject at the same time.
passage:
The transition from unconsciousness to consciousness is a familiar phenomenon that invites careful analysis. When a person awakes from dreamless sleep, the return of consciousness is marked not merely by the resumption of behavioural responsiveness but by the reappearance of a unified field of experience — a single perspectival vantage from which sights, sounds, and bodily sensations are simultaneously apprehended. When a patient recovers from general anaesthesia, the re-emergence of consciousness is characterised by the restoration of an integrated experiential perspective in which disparate perceptions inform and constrain one another within a coherent scene. When an infant first becomes conscious, the emergence is plausibly understood as the coalescence of previously disconnected sensory inputs into a unified experiential whole.

These cases invite a general definition: a conscious experience is a mental state with qualitative character, upon which the subject can reflect, and which is available for voluntary report. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this formulation may be drawn from neurological pathology. Suppose a patient suffers from a rare condition in which her visual and auditory experiences, though individually vivid and qualitatively rich, are entirely disconnected from one another. She sees a red ball rolling across a table and simultaneously hears a rhythmic bouncing sound, yet these two experiences do not belong to the same perceptual field. She cannot recognise that the sound emanates from the ball she sees; the visual and auditory streams proceed in complete isolation. She can attend to each experience individually and report upon it with clarity and precision. Each state has undeniable qualitative character. All three conditions of the definition are satisfied for each experience taken on its own. Yet one might observe that her consciousness is, in a significant respect, fragmented. She lacks the unified experiential perspective that characterises normal waking consciousness — the perspective within which diverse sensory states are bound together and experienced as aspects of a single, coherent world. What the background cases reveal, and what the definition fails to capture, is that conscious experience involves not merely the existence of individual qualitative states but their integration into a unified field of awareness.

background_cases: When a judge deliberates over a difficult sentencing decision, weighing the severity of the offence against mitigating circumstances, the deliberation is regarded as an exercise of free will not merely because the judge could have decided otherwise, but because she subjects her inclinations and initial reactions to critical scrutiny, considers reasons on multiple sides, and arrives at a decision through the exercise of her rational capacities rather than through the unreflective operation of habit or impulse.

When a reformed addict declines an offer of the substance to which he was formerly addicted, his refusal is regarded as a paradigm of free action precisely because he recognises the pull of the addictive desire, evaluates it in light of his considered values, and acts against the desire on the basis of rational self-governance rather than merely finding that the desire has happened to diminish.

When a soldier disobeys an order that she judges to be unjust, her disobedience is regarded as a free act because she has critically assessed the order against her moral convictions, recognised the costs of refusal, and chosen to act on her own judgement rather than deferring unreflectively to external authority.
definition: An agent S acts freely in performing action A if and only if (i) S performs A voluntarily — not under physical compulsion, (ii) S had alternative courses of action available, and (iii) S chose A on the basis of her own desires.
conditions: S performs A voluntarily — not under physical compulsion
S had alternative courses of action available
S chose A on the basis of her own desires
counterexample: Consider a man who has been subjected, without his knowledge, to a programme of subliminal conditioning that has implanted in him a powerful desire to purchase a particular brand of motor car. He enters the showroom voluntarily. Alternative vehicles are available and he could choose any of them. He selects the conditioned brand on the basis of what he experiences as his own desire — a strong and seemingly authentic preference. All three conditions are met. Yet the desire upon which he acts was not formed through any process of rational evaluation; it was implanted by an external agent through means that bypassed his critical faculties entirely. We should hesitate to call his choice free.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share, and what the definition fails to require, is that the desires upon which the agent acts have been subjected to rational scrutiny — that the agent has evaluated her motivating desires in light of her broader values and commitments, rather than acting upon desires whose provenance she has never examined and whose formation bypassed her rational capacities.
passage:
The exercises of free will that most clearly merit the designation share a structural feature that repays examination. When a judge deliberates over a difficult sentence, she subjects her initial inclinations to critical scrutiny, weighs competing considerations, and arrives at a decision through the exercise of her rational capacities. When a reformed addict declines the substance to which he was formerly enslaved, his refusal reflects a process of self-governance in which the addictive desire is recognised, evaluated, and overridden by his considered values. When a soldier disobeys an order she judges unjust, her disobedience issues from a critical assessment of authority against the claims of conscience.

These cases suggest a definition: an agent acts freely when she acts voluntarily, has alternatives available, and chooses on the basis of her own desires. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this formulation may be constructed from the realm of psychological manipulation. Suppose a man has been subjected, without his knowledge, to a programme of subliminal conditioning designed to implant in him a powerful desire to purchase a particular brand of motor car. The conditioning is wholly successful: the man develops what he experiences as a genuine and spontaneous preference for that brand. He enters the showroom voluntarily — no one compels him. Alternative vehicles are available, and he is free to choose any of them. He selects the conditioned brand on the basis of a desire that feels entirely his own. All three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied. Yet one might observe that the man's choice is not free in the sense that the background cases exemplify. The judge, the reformed addict, and the soldier each subjected the desires and inclinations that motivated their action to rational scrutiny. The man in the showroom has done nothing of the sort. The desire upon which he acts was implanted by an external agent through a process that entirely bypassed his capacity for critical evaluation. He has never examined its origins, weighed it against his broader values, or assessed whether it reflects the kind of person he wishes to be. What the background cases reveal, and what the definition fails to require, is that the desires upon which a free agent acts must themselves have been subjected to rational scrutiny and endorsement.

background_cases: When a pianist performs a complex passage in a concerto, the performance is regarded as an action not merely because the movements of her fingers are caused by her intention to play the music, but because she exercises continuous skilled control throughout the passage — adjusting tempo, dynamics, and articulation in response to what she hears, maintaining a form of ongoing guidance that shapes the movements as they unfold.

When a surgeon makes a precise incision, the incision counts as an action because the surgeon guides the scalpel continuously, adjusting pressure and angle in response to the resistance of the tissue, exercising a form of skilled control that runs throughout the movement rather than merely initiating it.

When an orator delivers a speech from memory, the delivery is an action because the speaker exercises ongoing control — modulating pitch, adjusting pace, responding to the audience's reactions — rather than merely setting a sequence of vocalisations in motion and letting them proceed without further guidance.
definition: An event E constitutes an action by agent S if and only if (i) E is caused by an intention of S, (ii) S could have refrained from E, and (iii) E is a bodily movement of S.
conditions: E is caused by an intention of S
S could have refrained from E
E is a bodily movement of S
counterexample: Suppose a man intends to pour a glass of water. His intention causes him to reach for the pitcher, but at the moment his hand contacts it, a sudden tremor in his arm — triggered by the intention itself through a chain of nervous excitement — causes the pitcher to slip and the water to spill across the table. The spilling is a bodily movement caused by his intention (via the nervous tremor the intention produced). He could have refrained from reaching for the pitcher. All three conditions are met. Yet the spilling is not an action; it is an accident that occurred despite, not because of, the exercise of the agent's skilled control.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share, and what the definition fails to require, is that an action involves the agent's continuous skilled guidance of her bodily movements — that the causal connection between intention and movement proceeds through a process of ongoing control and adjustment, rather than through any causal pathway, however deviant, that merely links intention to movement.
passage:
The events that we most readily recognise as actions share a structural feature that deserves attention. When a pianist performs a complex passage, the performance is an action not merely because her fingers move in consequence of her intention to play, but because she exercises continuous skilled control — adjusting tempo, dynamics, and articulation as the music unfolds. When a surgeon makes a precise incision, the movement counts as an action because the surgeon guides the scalpel throughout, responding to the resistance of the tissue with ongoing adjustments of pressure and angle. When an orator delivers a speech, the delivery is an action because the speaker modulates and adjusts her performance in response to the audience, exercising a form of guidance that persists throughout.

These cases invite a general characterisation: an event is an action when it is caused by the agent's intention, the agent could have refrained, and the event is a bodily movement. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this formulation may be constructed as follows. Suppose a man forms the intention to pour himself a glass of water. His intention causes him to reach for the pitcher. At the moment his hand makes contact with the handle, the very excitement produced by the intention triggers a nervous tremor in his arm, and the tremor causes the pitcher to slip from his grasp and the water to spill across the table. The spilling is a bodily movement. It was caused by his intention — the intention produced the reaching, which produced the excitement, which produced the tremor, which produced the spilling. He could have refrained from reaching for the pitcher in the first place. All three conditions of the definition are satisfied. Yet one might observe that the spilling of the water is not an action but an accident. The man did not guide the water across the table; the spilling occurred despite his control, not through it. What the background cases reveal, and what the definition fails to require, is that the causal connection between intention and bodily movement must proceed through a process of continuous skilled guidance — the kind of ongoing, responsive control that the pianist, the surgeon, and the orator each exercise throughout the course of their performances.

background_cases: When a mother feels fear upon seeing her child approach the edge of a cliff, the fear is recognisable as a genuine emotion partly because it is appropriate to the situation — the child is in danger, and the mother's fear reflects a correct appraisal of that danger. An identical surge of fear experienced while watching the child play safely in a garden would strike us as disproportionate and, in some sense, defective.

When a scientist feels pride upon the publication of a paper that represents years of careful research, the pride is apt: the achievement is genuine, the effort was real, and the emotion corresponds to the significance of what has been accomplished. Pride of similar intensity felt over a trivial accomplishment — tying one's shoelaces, say — would seem misplaced.

When a mourner feels grief at the death of a beloved friend, the grief is fitting because the loss is real, the relationship was deep, and the emotion matches the gravity of what has been lost. Grief of equal intensity over the loss of a penny would not be regarded as a proper emotion but as a symptom of some disturbance.
definition: A mental state M constitutes a genuine emotion if and only if (i) M has phenomenal character — a felt quality experienced by the subject, (ii) M is directed at an object or state of affairs, and (iii) M involves a cognitive appraisal of its object as significant in some respect.
conditions: M has phenomenal character — a felt quality experienced by the subject
M is directed at an object or state of affairs
M involves a cognitive appraisal of its object as significant in some respect
counterexample: Suppose a man develops, through a peculiar neurological condition, a vivid feeling of indignation directed at the number seven. He appraises the number seven as offensive and unjust — he has formed a settled cognitive judgement, however bizarre, that the number seven is morally repugnant. His state has felt quality; it is directed at an object; and it involves a cognitive appraisal of that object as significant. All three conditions are met. Yet the indignation is not a genuine emotion in the sense that the background cases exemplify, for the object at which it is directed is not the kind of thing that can bear the significance the appraisal attributes to it.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share, and what the definition fails to capture, is the requirement that the emotion be apt — that the object at which the emotion is directed be of a kind that can, in principle, bear the evaluative significance that the emotion's appraisal attributes to it, so that there is a fitting relationship between the character of the emotion and the nature of its object.
passage:
The emotions that we most readily recognise as genuine share a feature that invites philosophical attention. When a mother fears for her child at the cliff's edge, the fear is appropriate to its object — the child is genuinely in danger, and the emotion reflects a correct appraisal of that danger. When a scientist feels pride at the publication of a substantial paper, the pride is apt: it corresponds to the significance of a real achievement. When a mourner grieves the death of a beloved friend, the grief fits the gravity of the loss. In each case, the emotion stands in a relation of appropriateness to the situation that occasions it.

These observations suggest a general characterisation: a genuine emotion is a mental state with phenomenal character, directed at an object, and involving a cognitive appraisal of that object as significant. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this formulation may be drawn from neurological pathology. Suppose a man develops, through a rare disorder, a vivid and persistent feeling of indignation directed at the number seven. He appraises the number seven as morally repugnant — he has formed a settled cognitive judgement that the number seven is offensive and unjust. The state has undeniable phenomenal character: he feels a hot surge of moral outrage whenever the number is mentioned. It is directed at a definite object — the number seven. And it involves a cognitive appraisal of that object as significant: he judges it to be morally objectionable. All three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied. Yet one might observe that this is not a genuine emotion in the sense that the background cases exemplify. The number seven is not the kind of thing that can be morally repugnant or unjust; it is an abstract mathematical entity that cannot bear the evaluative properties the man's appraisal attributes to it. The mother's fear is appropriate because cliffs are genuinely dangerous to children. The scientist's pride is fitting because the achievement is real. The mourner's grief matches a genuine loss. What these cases share, and what the definition fails to require, is that the emotion's object be of a kind that can, in principle, bear the evaluative significance the emotion attributes to it — that there be a fitting relationship between the nature of the object and the character of the emotional response.

background_cases: When a patient is administered a general anaesthetic and loses all responsiveness, we judge that consciousness has been lost. The patient does not respond to speech, does not withdraw from painful stimuli, and upon recovery reports no experiences during the period of anaesthesia. The absence of any behavioural response and the absence of subsequent experiential report together indicate an absence of consciousness.

When a person enters deep dreamless sleep, we judge that consciousness is suspended. The sleeper does not respond to moderate stimuli, produces no verbal reports, and upon waking has no recollection of any experience during that phase of sleep. Again, the conjunction of behavioural unresponsiveness and the absence of experiential report signals the absence of consciousness.

When a person is fully awake and engaged in conversation, we judge that consciousness is present. The person responds appropriately to questions, reports on his current sensory experiences, and adjusts his behaviour in response to changing circumstances. The presence of behavioural responsiveness and the capacity for experiential report together indicate the presence of consciousness.
definition: A subject S is conscious at time t if and only if (i) S is behaviourally responsive to environmental stimuli at t, (ii) S is capable of producing verbal or non-verbal reports about his current experiential states at t, and (iii) S will subsequently be able to recall experiences had at t.
conditions: S is behaviourally responsive to environmental stimuli at t
S is capable of producing verbal or non-verbal reports about his current experiential states at t
S will subsequently be able to recall experiences had at t
counterexample: Suppose a patient suffering from a condition in which the motor pathways are entirely destroyed while the sensory and cognitive faculties remain intact — a state of complete paralysis with preserved awareness. The patient cannot move, cannot speak, cannot produce any behavioural response whatsoever. He is behaviourally unresponsive to all environmental stimuli; he cannot produce any report, verbal or otherwise, about his current experiential states; and because no report is possible, there is no external evidence that he will later recall his experiences. All three conditions of the definition fail. Yet the patient is conscious: he hears the voices of the physicians, feels the touch of the nurse's hand, and experiences the passage of time with agonising clarity. His consciousness is complete; only its outward expression is blocked.
abductive_insight: The background cases support the generalisation that consciousness is present when behavioural responsiveness and reportability are present, and absent when they are absent. But this inductive inference confuses the reliable indicators of consciousness with its constitutive conditions. The paralysed patient reveals that consciousness can be wholly dissociated from its behavioural and reportorial manifestations, showing that the generalisation from the background cases mistakes a correlation — between consciousness and its typical outward signs — for a constitutive relation.
passage:
Reflection upon the most familiar instances of consciousness and its absence suggests a pattern that is at once natural and, as we shall see, ultimately insufficient. When a patient under general anaesthesia lies unresponsive, producing no reports and later recalling nothing, we conclude that consciousness was absent. When a person sleeps without dreaming, the same conjunction of unresponsiveness, silence, and subsequent amnesia leads us to the same conclusion. And when a person is awake and conversing, his behavioural responsiveness, his capacity for report, and his later recollection all confirm the presence of consciousness. These cases invite a general characterisation: a subject is conscious when he is behaviourally responsive, capable of reporting his experiential states, and able to recall them afterward.

Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this formulation may be drawn from the clinical literature on complete motor paralysis with preserved awareness. Suppose a patient suffers a catastrophic lesion of the motor pathways that destroys all capacity for voluntary movement while leaving the sensory and cognitive faculties entirely intact. The patient lies motionless, unable to speak, unable to blink, unable to produce any behavioural response whatsoever. He is behaviourally unresponsive to every stimulus; he can produce no report, verbal or otherwise; and because no outward expression of his experience is possible, there is no evidence from which an observer could infer that he will later recall what he underwent. All three conditions of the proposed definition fail. Yet the patient is vividly conscious: he hears the conversation of the physicians at his bedside, feels the pressure of the nurse's hand upon his arm, and experiences the passage of time with excruciating clarity. His consciousness is complete; only its outward expression is blocked. What the background cases revealed was a reliable correlation between consciousness and its behavioural manifestations, but the paralysed patient shows that this correlation is not a constitutive relation. The inductive generalisation from familiar cases mistakes the typical signs of consciousness for its necessary conditions.

background_cases: When a man is held at gunpoint and ordered to hand over his wallet, we judge that he does not act freely. His action is compelled by an external threat; he would not have surrendered the wallet in the absence of the threat. The presence of external coercion is what renders the act unfree.

When a woman chooses a career after years of reflection, weighing her interests and abilities without any external pressure, we judge that she acts freely. No one compels her; no threat constrains her choice. The absence of external coercion is what renders the act free.

When a prisoner is ordered to perform labour under threat of punishment, we judge that his labour is unfree. The coercive threat from external authorities is what distinguishes his situation from that of a person who freely chooses to work. Once more, the presence or absence of external compulsion appears to be the decisive factor.
definition: An agent A acts freely in performing X if and only if (i) A's performance of X is not produced by external coercion or threat, (ii) A chose X from among alternatives that were genuinely available, and (iii) A's choice of X was the product of A's own deliberation.
conditions: A's performance of X is not produced by external coercion or threat
A chose X from among alternatives that were genuinely available
A's choice of X was the product of A's own deliberation
counterexample: Suppose a man has been subjected, without his awareness, to a sophisticated programme of psychological conditioning that has implanted in him an irresistible desire to choose a particular political party at every election. No external agent threatens or coerces him at the moment of voting. He perceives alternatives as genuinely available; he could, so far as he can tell, vote for any party. He deliberates carefully, weighing the merits of each party, and arrives at what he takes to be a considered judgement. All three conditions are satisfied: no external coercion is present, alternatives appear available, and the choice follows deliberation. Yet the man's deliberation is systematically corrupted by the implanted desire, which operates beneath his awareness and determines the outcome of his reasoning in advance. His freedom is illusory.
abductive_insight: The background cases support the generalisation that freedom consists in the absence of external coercion together with genuine alternatives and deliberation. But they draw exclusively upon cases where the constraint on freedom is external and overt. The conditioned voter reveals that constraints on freedom may be internal and covert — implanted desires, compulsions, or manipulated dispositions that corrupt deliberation from within without the agent's awareness. The inductive inference from the background cases mistakes the absence of external constraint for the presence of genuine autonomy.
passage:
The cases most commonly adduced in discussions of human freedom share a structural feature: the presence or absence of external coercion serves as the decisive criterion. When a man is held at gunpoint, his surrender of his wallet is unfree because an external threat determines his action. When a woman chooses a career without pressure, her choice is free because no such threat is operative. When a prisoner labours under the threat of punishment, his labour is unfree for the same reason. These cases invite the generalisation that an agent acts freely when no external coercion or threat produces his action, when alternatives are genuinely available, and when the choice is the product of the agent's own deliberation.

Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this formulation may be constructed as follows. Suppose a man has been subjected, without his awareness, to a programme of psychological conditioning that has implanted in him an irresistible desire to support a particular political party. At the moment of voting, no external agent threatens or coerces him. He perceives the full range of parties as available to him. He deliberates with apparent care, weighing the platforms and records of each party, and arrives at what he takes to be a considered judgement. All three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied. Yet the outcome of his deliberation was determined in advance by the implanted desire, which operates beneath the surface of his conscious reasoning and bends his reflections inexorably toward the predetermined conclusion. His deliberation, though subjectively experienced as genuine, is systematically corrupted. What the background cases revealed was a correlation between external coercion and the absence of freedom, but the conditioned voter shows that freedom may be undermined by forces that are internal and invisible. The inductive generalisation from familiar cases of coercion mistakes the absence of external constraint for the presence of genuine autonomy, failing to recognise that deliberation itself may be compromised from within.

background_cases: When a mother sees her child in danger, she experiences a surge of fear: her heart races, her muscles tense, and she is immediately disposed to act to protect the child. The felt bodily disturbance, the directedness toward the child's peril, and the behavioural disposition together constitute the emotion of fear.

When a man is insulted in public, he experiences anger: his face flushes, his fists clench, and he is disposed to confront or retaliate against the person who insulted him. The felt disturbance, the directedness toward the insult, and the disposition to act together constitute the emotion of anger.

When a person receives unexpected good news — say, a promotion — she experiences joy: her chest feels light, she smiles involuntarily, and she is disposed to celebrate and share the news. The felt change, the directedness toward the good fortune, and the behavioural tendency together constitute the emotion of joy.
definition: A mental state M is an emotion if and only if (i) M involves a characteristic felt bodily disturbance, (ii) M is directed toward a particular object or event that the subject takes to be significant, and (iii) M disposes the subject to act in ways characteristic of that type of emotion.
conditions: M involves a characteristic felt bodily disturbance
M is directed toward a particular object or event the subject takes to be significant
M disposes the subject to act in ways characteristic of that type of emotion
counterexample: Consider a case in which a man feels a deep, abiding grief over the passage of time and the transience of all things. This grief is not directed toward any particular object or event; it is a pervasive sense of loss that attaches to no specific occasion or person. It involves a characteristic felt bodily heaviness, and it disposes him to withdraw from activity and to reflect in solitude. The first and third conditions are met. But the second condition — directedness toward a particular object or event — is not, for the grief has no particular intentional object. Yet it would be most unreasonable to deny that this state is an emotion. Existential grief, melancholy, and certain forms of anxiety are widely recognised as emotions despite lacking a determinate object.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involve emotions directed at specific objects or events — a child in danger, a public insult, a promotion. The generalisation that emotions require directedness toward a particular object is natural given these cases. But the case of objectless grief reveals that the background cases were unrepresentative: they sampled only those emotions that happen to have determinate objects, ignoring the significant class of emotions — existential dread, free-floating anxiety, pervasive melancholy — that are genuine affective states without specific intentional targets.
passage:
The most familiar examples of emotion share a notable structural feature: each is directed toward a definite object or event. A mother's fear is directed at her child's danger; a man's anger is directed at a public insult; a woman's joy is directed at a promotion. These cases suggest a general characterisation: an emotion is a felt bodily disturbance directed toward a particular significant object or event and disposing the subject to characteristic behaviour. The pattern is consistent and compelling.

Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this formulation may be drawn from a form of experience well known to poets and philosophers alike. Suppose a man is pervaded by a deep, abiding grief over the passage of time and the transience of all things. This grief involves a characteristic felt bodily heaviness — a weight in the chest, a sluggishness in the limbs — and it disposes him to withdraw from activity, to sit in solitude, and to reflect upon the impermanence of human endeavour. The first and third conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied. But the grief has no particular intentional object. It is not directed at any specific loss, any determinate event, any identifiable person or thing. It is a pervasive, diffuse sense of sorrow that attaches to nothing in particular and to everything in general. The second condition is not met. Yet one might observe that it would be most unreasonable to deny that this state is an emotion. Existential melancholy, free-floating anxiety, and pervasive dread are widely recognised as genuine affective states, and to exclude them from the category of emotion would impoverish the concept beyond recognition. The background cases, it appears, were drawn from a restricted sample: they included only those emotions that happen to be directed at determinate objects, overlooking the significant class of emotions that lack such specificity. The inductive generalisation from object-directed cases mistook a common but not universal feature of emotion for a necessary condition.

background_cases: When a man raises his arm to hail a cab, we identify the arm-raising as an action because it was preceded by his decision to hail the cab, and the decision caused the bodily movement. The presence of a prior decision and its causal efficacy in producing the movement are what distinguish the action from a mere involuntary twitch.

When a woman signs her name on a contract, the signing is an action because she decided to sign, and her decision produced the specific sequence of hand movements. Without the prior decision and its causal role, the movements would not constitute an act of signing.

When a chess player moves his bishop, the move is an action because the player decided upon this particular move after deliberation, and the decision caused the hand to place the piece on the intended square. The deliberate decision and its causal connection to the bodily movement are what make the event an action.
definition: An event E constitutes an action if and only if (i) E is a bodily movement, (ii) E is preceded by a decision or intention on the part of the agent, and (iii) the decision or intention causes E.
conditions: E is a bodily movement
E is preceded by a decision or intention on the part of the agent
The decision or intention causes E
counterexample: Suppose a man decides to shoot a deer while hunting and forms the intention to squeeze the trigger when the animal next appears. The nervousness produced by this intention causes an involuntary spasm in his trigger finger, and the gun fires, killing the deer. The event — the pulling of the trigger — is a bodily movement. It was preceded by the decision and intention to shoot the deer. And the intention caused the bodily movement, albeit through the intermediary of nervous spasm rather than through deliberate muscular control. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet one might observe that the man did not act in pulling the trigger; the shot was discharged by an involuntary spasm, not by the controlled execution of his intention.
abductive_insight: The background cases all feature a straightforward causal chain from decision to bodily movement: the decision produces the movement through the normal exercise of motor control. The generalisation that action requires only a decision that causes a bodily movement is natural given these cases. But the nervous hunter reveals that the causal connection between intention and movement may take a deviant path — through involuntary physiological processes rather than through rational motor control — and that the background cases, by sampling only normal causal chains, failed to reveal the importance of the manner in which the intention produces the movement.
passage:
The most straightforward cases of human action share a common structure: a decision or intention precedes a bodily movement, and the decision causes that movement. When a man raises his arm to hail a cab, his decision to hail the cab produces the arm-raising through the normal exercise of motor control.
