When a woman signs a contract, her decision to sign causes the specific sequence of hand movements that inscribe her name. When a chess player moves his bishop, his deliberate decision causes the hand to place the piece on the intended square. These cases invite the generalisation that an action is a bodily movement preceded by and caused by a decision or intention.

Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this formulation may be constructed from a hunting scenario. Suppose a man in the field decides to shoot a deer and forms the firm intention to squeeze the trigger when the animal next comes into view. The intensity of his anticipation produces a nervous tremor in his hands, and this tremor causes an involuntary spasm of his trigger finger. The gun discharges and the deer is killed. The event — the squeezing of the trigger — is a bodily movement. It was preceded by the man's decision and intention to shoot. And the intention caused the movement: the nervousness that produced the spasm was itself a product of the intention to shoot. All three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied. Yet we should hesitate to say that the man acted in pulling the trigger. The shot was discharged by an uncontrolled spasm, not by the deliberate execution of his plan. What the background cases shared — and what the definition fails to require — is that the causal chain from intention to movement must proceed through the agent's rational motor control, not through some deviant physiological pathway. The inductive generalisation from normal cases mistook any causal connection between intention and movement for the right kind of causal connection.

background_cases: When a man stubs his toe on a stone, he is immediately aware of a sharp, localised pain. The pain has a distinctive qualitative character, is available to him from the first-person perspective, and he can report it to others. The awareness is direct and unreflective: he does not infer that he is in pain but simply finds himself pained.

When a woman tastes a ripe strawberry, she has an immediate qualitative experience of sweetness that she need not infer from any prior evidence. The experience is available to her from the first-person perspective, and she can describe it to others. She is directly acquainted with the qualitative character of the taste.

When a child looks at a bright red balloon, the vivid redness of the experience is immediately present to him. He does not deduce the redness from other information; he simply sees it. The qualitative character is available from the first-person perspective and can be reported to others.
definition: A mental state M is a conscious experience if and only if (i) M has a qualitative character that is immediately available to the subject from the first-person perspective, (ii) the subject can report M to others, and (iii) M does not require inference from prior evidence but is directly apprehended.
conditions: M has a qualitative character immediately available to the subject from the first-person perspective
The subject can report M to others
M does not require inference from prior evidence but is directly apprehended
counterexample: Suppose a patient with a neurological condition experiences chronic pain in her left hand. The pain has a qualitative character immediately available from her first-person perspective, she can report it to her physician, and she does not infer it but directly apprehends it. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the pain is a phantom: her left hand was amputated months ago. The qualitative character is genuine, the report is sincere, and the apprehension is direct, yet the experience has no corresponding bodily state. The conscious experience is, in a significant sense, about nothing at all, for the limb to which it ostensibly refers does not exist.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share is that the conscious experiences are directed towards objects or states that actually exist in the subject's body or environment. The definition, having been generalised from such cases, fails to require that the qualitative character correspond to any actual state of affairs. It thereby counts as a standard conscious experience a state that, despite meeting every formal criterion, is entirely disconnected from the reality it purports to represent.
passage:
The most familiar instances of conscious experience share a structural pattern that a natural but incomplete definition may capture only in part. When a man stubs his toe, the resulting pain is qualitatively vivid, immediately available from his first-person perspective, and reportable without inference. When a woman tastes a ripe strawberry, the sweetness is directly apprehended and can be communicated to others. When a child gazes at a bright red balloon, the redness is present to him without deduction. These cases invite a general characterisation: a conscious experience is a mental state with qualitative character immediately available from the first-person perspective, reportable to others, and directly apprehended without inference.

Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this formulation may be drawn from the phenomenon of phantom limb pain. Suppose a patient with a neurological condition experiences chronic pain in her left hand. The pain has a qualitative character immediately available from her first-person perspective. She can report the pain to her physician in vivid detail. And she does not infer the pain but directly apprehends it, just as one apprehends a stubbed toe. All three conditions of the definition are satisfied. Nevertheless, her left hand was amputated months ago; the pain has no corresponding bodily state whatsoever. The qualitative experience is genuine, the report sincere, and the apprehension direct, yet the experience is directed towards nothing that exists. What the background cases share, and what the definition fails to demand, is that the qualitative character correspond to some actual state of affairs in the subject's body or environment. Without this requirement, the analysis cannot distinguish between experiences that are about something and those that are, in the most fundamental sense, about nothing at all.

background_cases: When a man raises his arm to hail a taxi, his bodily movement is preceded by a desire to attract the driver's attention and an intention to raise his arm. The desire and intention cause the arm movement through the normal operation of the motor system, and the man is aware of what he is doing as he does it.

When a woman reaches for a glass of water, her movement is preceded by a desire to drink and an intention to grasp the glass. The desire and intention cause the reaching through the standard channels of motor planning and execution, and she is aware of the goal she is pursuing.

When a pianist strikes a particular key during a performance, his finger movement is preceded by a musical intention and a desire to produce a certain note. The intention causes the movement through trained motor pathways, and the pianist is aware of his musical purpose throughout.
definition: A bodily movement B constitutes a genuine action if and only if (i) B is preceded by a desire or intention on the part of the agent, (ii) the desire or intention causes B through the normal operation of the agent's motor system, and (iii) the agent is aware of the goal towards which B is directed during its performance.
conditions: B is preceded by a desire or intention on the part of the agent
The desire or intention causes B through the normal operation of the motor system
The agent is aware of the goal towards which B is directed during its performance
counterexample: Suppose a man desires to scratch an itch on his nose and forms the intention to raise his hand to his face. The intention causes the movement through the normal operation of his motor system, and he is aware of the goal throughout. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the itch is caused by an allergic reaction to a substance he does not know he has contacted, and his desire to scratch is so overpowering that he could not have refrained from the movement even had he wished to. The movement satisfies every condition of the definition, yet it is more akin to a compulsion than to what we ordinarily recognise as a free and genuine action.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share is that the agent could have refrained from the movement and chose not to. The definition, generalised from these voluntary cases, fails to require that the agent possess the capacity to withhold the movement. It thereby classifies as genuine actions those movements that are compelled by irresistible urges, even though we should ordinarily distinguish such compulsions from genuinely voluntary conduct.
passage:
The paradigmatic cases of human action share a common structure that a natural but incomplete generalisation may express only imperfectly. When a man raises his arm to hail a taxi, the movement is preceded by a desire and intention that cause it through the normal motor system, and the man is aware of his goal throughout. When a woman reaches for a glass of water, or a pianist strikes a key, the same pattern obtains: desire or intention, normal motor causation, and concurrent awareness of the goal.

These cases invite a general characterisation: a bodily movement is a genuine action when it is preceded by a desire or intention, caused by that mental state through normal motor channels, and accompanied by awareness of the goal. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this formulation may be drawn from the phenomenon of irresistible compulsion. Suppose a man desires to scratch an itch on his nose and intends to raise his hand to his face. The intention causes the movement through the normal operation of his motor system, and he is aware of his goal throughout. All three conditions are met. Yet the itch is caused by a severe allergic reaction, and the desire to scratch is so overpowering that the man could not have refrained from the movement even had he wished to do so. The movement satisfies every stated condition, yet we should hesitate to classify it as a genuine action, for it bears a closer resemblance to a compulsion than to the voluntary conduct we observed in the background cases. What those cases share, and what the definition fails to require, is that the agent possesses the capacity to withhold the movement, so that the action is performed because the agent chooses to perform it rather than because an irresistible urge leaves no alternative.

background_cases: When a mother sees her child in danger, she experiences fear. The fear involves a felt bodily disturbance — racing heart, tightened muscles — together with an appraisal that the child is threatened, and the emotion disposes her to protective behaviour.

When a student receives an unexpectedly high mark on an examination, he experiences joy. The joy involves a felt lightness and warmth, together with an appraisal that something good has happened, and it disposes him to smile and celebrate.

When a man discovers that a trusted friend has lied to him, he experiences anger. The anger involves a felt tension and heat, together with an appraisal that he has been wronged, and it disposes him to confront the friend.
definition: A mental state E is an emotion if and only if (i) E involves a felt bodily disturbance or sensation, (ii) E includes an appraisal of the subject's situation as being of a particular evaluative character, and (iii) E disposes the subject towards behaviour appropriate to the appraised situation.
conditions: E involves a felt bodily disturbance or sensation
E includes an appraisal of the situation as having a particular evaluative character
E disposes the subject towards behaviour appropriate to the appraised situation
counterexample: Suppose a man drinks a strong cup of coffee and, as a result of the caffeine, experiences a felt bodily disturbance: his heart races, his muscles tighten, and he feels a surge of energy. He happens at that moment to be reading a letter informing him of a minor legal dispute, and he appraises the situation as threatening, interpreting his bodily arousal as a sign of anxiety. The state disposes him towards defensive behaviour: he immediately telephones his solicitor. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the bodily disturbance is produced by caffeine, not by the letter. The appraisal is a confabulation, prompted by the coincidence of pharmacological arousal and a mildly worrying letter. The emotion that the definition identifies as genuine is, in reality, a misattribution of a chemically induced state.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share is that the bodily disturbance and the appraisal arise from the same source: the actually threatening, gratifying, or offensive situation. The definition, generalised from these congruent cases, fails to require that the felt bodily disturbance be produced by the situation that the appraisal identifies as its cause. Without this requirement, any pharmacologically induced arousal that happens to coincide with an evaluative appraisal satisfies the definition.
passage:
The most recognisable instances of emotion share a pattern that an initially plausible definition may fail to capture fully. When a mother sees her child in peril, the fear she feels involves bodily disturbance, an appraisal of threat, and a disposition to protect. When a student receives a high mark, the joy involves felt warmth, an appraisal of good fortune, and a disposition to celebrate. When a man discovers a friend's dishonesty, the anger involves felt tension, an appraisal of wrongdoing, and a disposition to confront.

These cases invite a general characterisation: an emotion is a felt bodily disturbance accompanied by an evaluative appraisal and a disposition to behave in accordance with the appraisal. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this formulation may be drawn from the phenomenon of misattributed arousal. Suppose a man drinks a strong cup of coffee and, as a consequence of the caffeine, experiences a racing heart, tightened muscles, and a surge of energy. He happens at that moment to be reading a letter informing him of a minor legal dispute, and he appraises the situation as threatening, interpreting the bodily arousal as anxiety about the dispute. The state disposes him to telephone his solicitor at once. All three conditions are met. Yet the bodily disturbance is produced by the caffeine, not by the letter. The appraisal is a confabulation, prompted by the coincidence of pharmacological arousal and a mildly worrying piece of correspondence. What the background cases share, and what the definition fails to demand, is that the bodily disturbance and the evaluative appraisal arise from the same situational source, so that the emotion is a genuine response to the appraised circumstances rather than a misattribution of an independently caused physical state.

background_cases: When we encounter a friend after a long absence, we identify him as the same person on the basis of physical appearance, voice, and manner of speech. These bodily and behavioural features provide the continuity that grounds our recognition of him as the individual we knew before.

When a court of law identifies a defendant as the person who committed a crime years earlier, it relies upon fingerprints, photographs, and testimony establishing bodily continuity. The sameness of the body, documented over time, grounds the attribution of responsibility.

When a family recognises a soldier returning from a long campaign, they identify him by his scars, his gait, and the peculiarities of his physiognomy. Bodily continuity, despite the changes wrought by time, is the basis of their recognition.
definition: A person P at time t2 is the same person as Q at time t1 if and only if (i) P's body is spatiotemporally continuous with Q's body, (ii) P's body retains sufficient physical similarity to Q's body to permit recognition, and (iii) P's body bears the marks — scars, fingerprints, and other identifying features — that were present on Q's body.
conditions: P's body is spatiotemporally continuous with Q's body
P's body retains sufficient physical similarity to Q's body to permit recognition
P's body bears identifying marks that were present on Q's body
counterexample: Suppose a man suffers a catastrophic brain injury that destroys all of his memories, personality traits, and cognitive abilities, leaving him in a persistent vegetative state. His body is spatiotemporally continuous with the body of the man he was before the injury. His physical appearance retains sufficient similarity for his family to recognise him. And his body bears the same scars and fingerprints it always did. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the man's wife says, with considerable philosophical plausibility, that the person she married is gone, even though his body remains. The individual who lies before her has no memories, no personality, and no capacity for the relationships that defined the person she knew. Bodily continuity is fully preserved, but personal identity, in the sense that matters for our deepest practices of love and responsibility, appears to have been severed.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share is that bodily continuity was accompanied by psychological continuity: the friend, the defendant, and the soldier all retained their memories, personalities, and characteristic ways of engaging with others. The definition, generalised from these cases, treats bodily continuity as sufficient whilst ignoring the psychological continuity that was silently present in every background case. When bodily continuity persists but psychological continuity is destroyed, the definition affirms identity where our deepest convictions deny it.
passage:
The most natural basis for identifying a person over time is the continuity of the body. When we recognise a friend after a long absence, we rely upon physical appearance and voice. When a court identifies a defendant, it relies upon fingerprints and photographs. When a family welcomes a soldier home, they recognise his scars and gait. These cases invite a general characterisation: a person at one time is the same as a person at an earlier time when the body is spatiotemporally continuous, retains sufficient similarity for recognition, and bears the same identifying marks.

Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this formulation may be imagined from the domain of catastrophic neurological injury. Suppose a man suffers a brain injury that destroys all of his memories, personality traits, and cognitive capacities, leaving him in a persistent vegetative state. His body is spatiotemporally continuous with the body he inhabited before the injury. His appearance retains sufficient similarity for his family to recognise him at his bedside. His fingerprints and scars are unchanged. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet his wife says, with considerable philosophical plausibility, that the person she married is gone. The individual before her has no memories, no personality, no capacity for the relationships that defined the man she knew. What the background cases share, and what the definition fails to recognise, is that bodily continuity was always accompanied by psychological continuity: the friend retained his manner, the defendant his memories, the soldier his personality. The definition, generalised from these doubly continuous cases, treats bodily continuity alone as sufficient and has no resources to account for the loss of identity that occurs when psychological continuity is destroyed whilst the body endures.

background_cases: A man reports experiencing vivid visual imagery while dreaming; upon waking, he can describe the colours, shapes, and movements of the dream in detail. We are inclined to say that he was conscious during the dream.
A woman under light sedation reports a vague sense of discomfort and an awareness that she is in a hospital. Despite her impaired faculties, we judge that she retains some measure of consciousness.
A child absorbed in imaginative play narrates an elaborate fantasy to herself, responding emotionally to the imagined events. We regard the child as conscious throughout.
definition: A subject is conscious at a given time if and only if (i) the subject undergoes qualitative experience at that time, (ii) the subject can subsequently report on some aspect of the experience, and (iii) the experience is integrated into the subject's ongoing mental life.
conditions: the subject undergoes qualitative experience at that time
the subject can subsequently report on some aspect of the experience
the experience is integrated into the subject's ongoing mental life
counterexample: Suppose that a patient undergoing surgery under general anaesthesia forms no subsequent report of any experience during the operation, and the experience — if any — leaves no trace upon her ongoing mental life. Yet careful measurement of brain activity during the procedure reveals patterns strongly associated with conscious processing, and isolated studies of similar patients have, upon the use of different interview techniques, elicited fragmentary reports of intraoperative awareness. The second and third conditions are not met in the standard case, yet there is reason to suspect that qualitative experience was present.
abductive_insight: The background cases led us to generalise that consciousness is always accompanied by reportability and integration into ongoing mental life, for in each case these features were conspicuously present. The surgical case reveals that our inductive generalisation may have been drawn from instances in which the channels of report and integration were functioning normally, and that consciousness may occur even when those channels are pharmacologically suppressed. The inference from 'reportable and integrated experience' to 'consciousness' may be sound in the positive direction, but the converse — that unreportable experience is unconscious — does not follow.
passage:
In the familiar cases from which we draw our understanding of consciousness, certain features reliably co-occur. The dreamer recalls his dream; the sedated patient, though confused, can articulate something of her experience; the imaginative child weaves her fantasy into a coherent narrative. From these and similar instances, one might reasonably infer a general characterisation: a subject is conscious at a given time when she undergoes qualitative experience, can subsequently report upon it, and the experience is woven into the fabric of her ongoing mental life. The inference is a natural one, for in every case we have examined, reportability and integration accompany consciousness as faithfully as a shadow accompanies its object.

Yet we should hesitate before pronouncing this generalisation secure. Let us imagine a patient who undergoes a lengthy surgical procedure under general anaesthesia. Upon waking, she reports nothing — no images, no sensations, no awareness of the passage of time. Nor does the experience, if experience there was, appear to have left any mark upon her subsequent mental life; she exhibits no behavioural changes, no emotional residue, no involuntary recollections. The second and third conditions are plainly unsatisfied. And yet neurophysiological monitoring during the operation recorded patterns of cerebral activity that bear a striking resemblance to those observed during established states of consciousness. Moreover, isolated investigations employing different methods of inquiry have, in similar patients, elicited fragmentary reports — a remembered voice, a fleeting pressure — that suggest the presence of awareness beneath the pharmacological surface. We are thus led to suspect that our original generalisation was drawn from a biased sample: cases in which the mechanisms of report and integration were functioning as they normally do. When those mechanisms are suppressed, consciousness may persist undetected. The case reminds us that the absence of evidence — of report, of integration — is not evidence of absence, and that our inductive confidence in the sufficiency of the proposed conditions must be tempered by the recognition that consciousness may elude the very instruments by which we ordinarily detect it.

background_cases: A woman decides to bake a cake for her friend's birthday. She purchases ingredients, follows a recipe, and produces the cake. We say she intended to bake the cake, and her intention guided her through a sequence of deliberate steps.

A student resolves to learn Latin. He enrols in a course, studies the grammar, and practises translation exercises daily. His intention to learn Latin structures and sustains his behaviour over months.
A carpenter plans to build a bookcase. She selects timber, measures and cuts the pieces, and assembles them according to her design. Her intention is manifest in every stage of the work.
definition: An agent intends to perform an action if and only if (i) the agent has formed a prior mental commitment to the action, (ii) the commitment guides the agent through a sequence of preparatory and executory steps, and (iii) the agent is consciously aware of the commitment throughout the execution.
conditions: the agent has formed a prior mental commitment to the action
the commitment guides the agent through a sequence of preparatory and executory steps
the agent is consciously aware of the commitment throughout the execution
counterexample: Suppose that an experienced driver intends to drive to the office by a certain route. She sets out and navigates familiar streets, making turns, stopping at traffic signals, and yielding to pedestrians — all without any conscious awareness of her commitment to the route. Her mind wanders to an unrelated problem, and she arrives at the office without being able to recall a single decision made en route. She formed a prior commitment, and that commitment guided her through a complex sequence of actions, but she was not consciously aware of the commitment throughout the execution. The third condition is unsatisfied, yet it appears that she intended to drive to the office and succeeded in doing so.
abductive_insight: The background cases — baking, studying, carpentry — are all instances of effortful, attention-demanding activity in which conscious awareness naturally accompanies the guiding intention. From these we generalised that conscious awareness throughout execution is a necessary condition of intention. The driving case reveals that our sample was biased toward tasks requiring sustained concentration. When an action is habitual and well-practised, intention may guide behaviour through automatic processes that operate beneath the threshold of awareness. The induction from effortful cases to a universal awareness requirement was unwarranted.
passage:
When we examine the cases from which our understanding of intention is typically drawn, we find that they share a salient characteristic: the agent is consciously aware of her guiding commitment at every stage of the action. The woman baking a cake attends deliberately to each step of the recipe. The student of Latin is conscious of his purpose as he labours over declensions. The carpenter keeps her plan before her mind's eye as she measures and cuts. From these instances, one might reasonably generalise that intention requires three things: a prior mental commitment to the action, the guidance of behaviour by that commitment through a series of preparatory and executory steps, and the agent's conscious awareness of the commitment throughout the process.

Yet we should pause to ask whether our sample of cases has been sufficiently broad. Let us imagine an experienced driver who intends to travel from her home to her office by a familiar route. She starts the car, reverses from the driveway, and proceeds through a series of streets, making appropriate turns, observing traffic signals, and adjusting her speed to the conditions of the road. Throughout this journey, however, her conscious attention is elsewhere — absorbed in a problem from the previous day's work, perhaps, or in the melody playing on the wireless. She arrives at the office without any recollection of the specific navigational decisions she made en route. Her prior commitment to the route guided her through a complex sequence of actions with remarkable precision, yet she was not consciously aware of that commitment during the execution. The third condition is plainly unsatisfied. Nevertheless, it appears beyond reasonable doubt that she intended to drive to the office and that her intention was operative throughout. We are thus led to recognise that our original generalisation was unduly influenced by cases of effortful, attention-demanding action, and that intention may function as a guide to behaviour through well-established automaticities that require no continuous conscious supervision.

background_cases: A man receives news that his child has won a scholarship and feels a surge of joy — his heart beats faster, he smiles broadly, and he experiences a warm glow of satisfaction. We unhesitatingly classify his state as an emotion.

A woman narrowly avoids a collision while driving and feels intense fear — her hands tremble, her breathing quickens, and she experiences a vivid sense of danger. This too we classify without hesitation as an emotion.
A student reads a moving account of injustice and feels anger — his jaw tightens, his face flushes, and he is disposed to protest. We regard this as a paradigmatic emotional response.
definition: A mental state is an emotion if and only if (i) it involves a pronounced bodily feeling, (ii) it is directed toward a particular object or situation, and (iii) it arises rapidly and without deliberate choice on the part of the subject.
conditions: it involves a pronounced bodily feeling
it is directed toward a particular object or situation
it arises rapidly and without deliberate choice on the part of the subject
counterexample: Suppose that a woman has for many years nursed a quiet resentment toward a colleague who wronged her in a professional matter. The resentment involves no pronounced bodily feeling — no racing heart, no flushed face, no trembling hands. It is a cold, steady state that colours her interactions with the colleague without producing any dramatic physical sensation. It is directed toward the colleague and the past wrong, and it arose not in a sudden flash but gradually, accreting over months of reflection. The first and third conditions are unmet, yet the state appears to be a genuine emotion — specifically, resentment — of a settled and enduring character.
abductive_insight: Our background cases were all instances of acute, episodic emotions — joy, fear, anger — marked by vivid bodily sensations and sudden onset. From these we generalised that all emotions involve pronounced bodily feeling and arise rapidly. The resentment case reveals that our inductive base was biased toward the dramatic and the acute, omitting the quieter, more enduring emotional states that lack conspicuous bodily accompaniments and develop gradually. The generalisation mistook the features of acute emotion for features of emotion as such.
passage:
The cases upon which we typically rely in forming our concept of emotion exhibit certain regular features. The father's joy at his child's success comes with a quickened pulse and a warm glow; the driver's fear after a near-collision arrives with trembling hands and rapid breathing; the student's anger at injustice manifests in a clenched jaw and a flushed countenance. In each instance, the emotion involves a pronounced bodily feeling, is directed toward a specific object or situation, and arises swiftly, without deliberation on the part of the subject. One might therefore infer that a mental state is an emotion when it satisfies these three conditions jointly.

Yet we should ask whether our sample has been representative of the full range of emotional life. Let us imagine a woman who, over the course of many months, comes to harbour a quiet but settled resentment toward a colleague who treated her unjustly in a matter of professional consequence. This resentment is not an episode but a disposition; it does not announce itself with racing pulse or flushed cheeks. It is a cold, steady state that tinges her dealings with the colleague without producing any dramatic physical sensation. Nor did it arise suddenly — it gathered slowly, fed by repeated recollection and private reflection, until it became a fixed feature of her attitude toward the individual in question. The first condition — pronounced bodily feeling — is unsatisfied, for the resentment is somatically quiet. The third condition — rapid and unchosen onset — is likewise unmet, for the state accreted gradually through a process that included elements of deliberate reflection. Yet resentment is, by any reckoning, an emotion, and a significant one at that. We are thus led to conclude that our original generalisation was distorted by the salience of acute, dramatic emotions in our inductive sample, and that the concept of emotion must be broad enough to encompass states that are settled, somatically subdued, and slow in their development.

background_cases: A woman sees a red apple on a table before her. Light reflected from the apple enters her eyes, is processed by her visual system, and she forms a visual experience that accurately represents the apple's colour, shape, and location. We say she perceives the apple.

A man hears a church bell ringing in the distance. Sound waves produced by the bell travel through the air to his ears, and he forms an auditory experience that correctly represents the bell's pitch, timbre, and approximate direction. This is a clear case of perception.

A child touches a piece of ice and feels its coldness. Thermal energy is transferred from her hand to the ice, stimulating temperature receptors in her skin, and she forms a tactile experience that accurately represents the object's temperature. We regard this as perception.
definition: A subject perceives an object or event if and only if (i) the object or event causes a sensory experience in the subject through an appropriate causal chain, (ii) the sensory experience accurately represents a property of the object or event, and (iii) the subject is aware of the experience as being of something external to herself.
conditions: the object or event causes a sensory experience in the subject through an appropriate causal chain
the sensory experience accurately represents a property of the object or event
the subject is aware of the experience as being of something external to herself
counterexample: Suppose that a man looks at a white wall upon which a shadow falls, creating a dark shape. The shadow is caused by a cat sitting on a windowsill behind him, out of his line of sight. The man sees the shadow and accurately perceives its shape, its darkness, and its position on the wall. The shadow is caused by the cat through an appropriate causal chain involving light and obstruction. Yet the man is aware only of the shadow on the wall; he does not perceive the cat at all. The shadow accurately represents a property of the cat — its silhouette — but the man takes himself to be perceiving a dark mark on the wall, not a cat. He perceives the shadow, but one would hesitate to say he perceives the cat, even though the cat is the ultimate cause of his experience and the experience accurately represents the cat's outline.
abductive_insight: Our background cases involved direct causal chains between object and experience — light from the apple, sound from the bell, thermal energy from the ice. From these we generalised that perception requires the object to cause the experience through an appropriate chain and the experience to represent a property of the object accurately. The shadow case reveals that our inductive sample consisted of instances in which the perceived object was also the proximate cause of the experience. When the causal chain interposes an intermediary — the shadow — the experience may accurately represent a property of the distal object without the subject perceiving that object. The generalisation failed to distinguish between perceiving the proximate cause and perceiving the distal cause of one's experience.
passage:
The cases upon which our analysis of perception is most naturally founded share a common structure: the object perceived is the proximate cause of the sensory experience, and the experience faithfully represents a property of that object. The woman sees the apple because light from the apple reaches her eyes; the man hears the bell because sound from the bell reaches his ears; the child feels the ice because thermal energy passes from her hand to its surface. In each case, the causal chain runs directly from object to experience, the experience accurately represents a property of the object, and the subject is aware of the experience as being of something in the external world. From these instances one might generalise: a subject perceives an object when the object causes a sensory experience through an appropriate causal chain, the experience accurately represents a property of the object, and the subject is aware of the experience as externally directed.

Yet we should examine whether our inductive base has been sufficiently attentive to the complexity of causal chains. Let us imagine a man who stands before a white wall upon which a dark shadow falls. The shadow is cast by a cat sitting on the windowsill behind him, blocking a portion of the light from the window. The man sees the shadow clearly; he perceives its shape, its darkness, and its location upon the wall with perfect accuracy. The shadow is caused by the cat through a chain involving light and obstruction — a chain that appears, in its essentials, as appropriate as the one that links the apple to the woman's visual experience. Moreover, the shape of the shadow accurately represents the silhouette of the cat. Yet the man does not perceive the cat; he perceives only a dark shape on the wall. He has no awareness of a cat, no belief that a cat is present, and no experience that he would describe as being of a cat. The three conditions of our analysis are met with respect to the shadow but not, in the relevant sense, with respect to the cat — even though the cat is the distal cause of the experience and the experience represents its outline. We are thus brought to recognise that our original generalisation, drawn from cases of direct causal contact, did not anticipate the difficulty of distinguishing between the proximate and the distal objects in a complex causal chain, and that a satisfactory analysis of perception must specify more precisely which object in the chain is the one perceived.
